The passage also notes that rectal snips were not performed, which could have provided additional diagnostic information. The lack of a consistent association across all cases weakens the inference that peripheral eosinophilia is a reliable early indicator.

55.Option: The delayed diagnosis of neuroschistosomiasis in non-endemic regions is primarily due to the lack of access to advanced imaging techniques such as MRI.
Evidence: We note that in non-endemic regions, neuroschistosomiasis is rare [1,4,8] and there is evidence that diagnosis can be delayed due to lack of clinical awareness of this condition.
55.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that delayed diagnosis in non-endemic regions is due to lack of clinical awareness rather than lack of access to MRI. All four patients in the case series had MRI scans, and in one case, the MRI findings were only recognized as typical for schistosomiasis upon review by a neuroradiologist with expertise in parasitic infections. This indicates that access to MRI is not the primary issue, but rather the interpretation and recognition of the condition by clinicians.

55.Option: The long latency period between freshwater exposure and symptom onset (up to 29 months) suggests that neuroschistosomiasis can develop even in the absence of ongoing parasitic infection.
Evidence: The mean time between travel from endemic regions and presentation was 12.7 months (range 0–29 months) (Fig 4).
The exact time from exposure to freshwater to symptom onset was unclear, as the patient was likely to have been exposed regularly during his year in Nigeria when swimming in a local lake.
55.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that neuroschistosomiasis can develop in the absence of ongoing parasitic infection. The long latency period is attributed to the time it takes for eggs to reach the central nervous system and trigger an immune response, not to the absence of the parasite. The passage also notes that all patients received praziquantel treatment, which targets the adult worms, and one patient had negative serology after treatment, indicating that the infection was eradicated. The latency period does not imply the absence of ongoing infection but rather the time required for the disease process to manifest.

55.Option: Despite early treatment with praziquantel and high dose steroids, only one of four patients in this series achieved complete neurological recovery, indicating that delayed diagnosis and treatment are associated with poor long-term outcomes.
Evidence: All patients received a minimum of 3 days praziquantel and high dose steroids. Clinical features resolved fully in only one out of four patients.
Mean time from first presentation to UK health services to commencement of praziquantel was 42.15 days (range 16–74 days) (Fig 4).
55.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that only one of four patients achieved complete neurological recovery despite treatment with praziquantel and high-dose steroids. The mean time from presentation to treatment was 42.15 days, indicating a delay in diagnosis and treatment. The association between delayed treatment and poor outcomes is supported by the fact that the patient who achieved full recovery (Case 2) had the shortest delay in treatment (74 days from exposure to treatment, but only 44 days from presentation to treatment). The other patients, who had longer delays, experienced persistent neurological deficits. This supports the inference that delayed diagnosis and treatment are associated with poor long-term outcomes.

55.Option: The fact that all patients had positive CSF serology confirms that serological testing is the gold standard for diagnosing neuroschistosomiasis in clinical practice.
Evidence: Diagnosis was confirmed with CSF serology for schistosomiasis in all cases.

The accepted criteria for confirmation of spinal cord schistosomiasis are as follows [1]: Evidence of lower thoracic or upper lumbar neurological lesions clinically and on imaging; Evidence of infection with schistosomiasis through parasite isolation in tissue or serological techniques; Exclusion of other causes of myelitis.
55.Rationale: While all patients in the case series had positive CSF serology, the passage does not state that serological testing is the gold standard for diagnosing neuroschistosomiasis. The accepted criteria for confirmation include both imaging and serological evidence, as well as exclusion of other causes. The passage also notes that the absence of eggs in stool or urine does not exclude neuroschistosomiasis, implying that serology is one of several diagnostic tools. Therefore, the inference that serological testing is the gold standard cannot be drawn solely from the case series.

56.Option: The high expression of proinflammatory cytokines such as IL-6 and IL-17A in the brain tissue surrounding cysticerci indicates that the inflammatory response is sufficient to cause neurological symptoms, which were absent due to compensatory mechanisms not yet identified.
Evidence: The immunohistochemical analysis of the brain tissues surrounding cysticerci showed a high expression of CD80, CD106, CD69, and IL-10; a moderate expression of IL4, TNF-α, and CD54, and a low expression of IL-17 and IL-6 with respect to brain tissues from sham-operated pigs (Figs 3 and 4).
56.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the expression of IL-17 and IL-6 was low, not high, in the brain tissue surrounding cysticerci. The option incorrectly claims high expression of these proinflammatory cytokines. Since the evidence directly contradicts the claim, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

56.Option: The absence of neurological symptoms in infected pigs, despite the presence of degenerating cysticerci and a strong inflammatory response, suggests that immunomodulatory mechanisms may play a key role in preventing clinical disease in this species.
Evidence: During this experiment and until sacrifice, pigs were in good health condition, with no neurological signs.

The immunohistochemical analysis of the brain tissues surrounding cysticerci showed a high expression of CD80, CD106, CD69, and IL-10; a moderate expression of IL4, TNF-α, and CD54, and a low expression of IL-17 and IL-6 with respect to brain tissues from sham-operated pigs (Figs 3 and 4).
Most cysticerci were in a degenerating stage and were surrounded by an exacerbated inflammatory reaction (grade 4).
56.Rationale: The passage confirms that pigs showed no neurological symptoms despite having degenerating cysticerci and a strong inflammatory response. The high expression of IL-10 (an anti-inflammatory cytokine) and the presence of a strong inflammatory reaction suggest that immunomodulatory mechanisms may be balancing the immune response to prevent clinical disease. This inference is directly supported by the evidence presented.

56.Option: The absence of neurological signs in infected pigs is likely due to the limited number of cysticerci present in the central nervous system, which is insufficient to trigger a clinical response.
Evidence: 63 cysticerci from the brain and 11 cysticerci from the muscle were macroscopically examined.
The number of cysts in the brain was higher when 1000 oncospheres were inoculated than those recovered when 500 oncospheres were used (36 versus 27).
56.Rationale: The passage states that 63 cysticerci were found in the brain, which is a substantial number. The option incorrectly attributes the absence of neurological signs to a limited number of cysticerci. The evidence does not support this claim, as the number of cysticerci was not described as limited.

56.Option: The lack of neurological symptoms in pigs is due to the absence of viable neurons in the brain tissue surrounding the cysticerci, which prevents the transmission of neural signals that would otherwise lead to clinical manifestations.
Evidence: The immunohistochemical analysis of vimentin expression showed that immature astrocytes were more frequently found in areas proximal to the parasite (A) than in tissues distal to the parasite (B) (40X).

In general, the expression of NeuN (viable neurons) and nestin (immature neurons) was low, particularly in tissues proximal to the parasite, and increased slightly in tissues distal to the parasite (Fig 5).
56.Rationale: The passage states that NeuN (viable neurons) expression was low in tissues proximal to the parasite but increased in distal tissues. This suggests that viable neurons are not entirely absent, contradicting the claim in the option. The absence of neurological symptoms cannot be attributed to the complete absence of viable neurons.

56.Option: The higher systemic antibody levels observed in pigs infected intramuscularly compared to those infected in the subarachnoid space suggest that peripheral infections elicit a stronger immune response, which may contribute to the faster clearance of cysticerci in muscle tissue.
Evidence: Antibody levels were significantly higher in the groups inoculated in the subarachnoid space (SA) (500 oncospheres, R = 0.75, P = 0.05; 1000 oncospheres, R = 0.80, P = 0.03), while only a tendency was observed in the group inoculated intramuscularly (IM) (R = 0.73, P = 0.06).

The animals intramuscularly challenged exhibited higher antibody levels. Significant differences in OD values between groups of animals were observed at different times: at day 53 PI, control versus inoculation of 500 oncospheres in SA (P = 0.01); at day 81 PI, control versus IM inoculation (P = 0.02), controls versus inoculation of 1000 oncospheres in SA (P = 0.03), and inoculation of 500 oncospheres in SA versus IM (P = 0.02); at day 123 PI, control and IM inoculation (P = 0.001).
56.Rationale: The passage states that antibody levels were significantly higher in the subarachnoid space groups, not in the intramuscularly infected group. The option incorrectly claims that intramuscular infection resulted in higher antibody levels. Since the evidence contradicts the claim, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

57.Option: The model's high fit to serological data confirms that the RVF virus has been continuously circulating in Mayotte's livestock population since 2004, despite the absence of clinical symptoms.
Evidence: Subsequent serological studies carried out in Mayotte in livestock (2004–2016) showed that RVF had been present at least since 2004, and re-emerged in 2008–10, despite no symptoms in animals being detected

Models without any animal import (Models 2a and 2b) had the worst fit (Table 2), and failed to capture the IgG prevalence peak (S4 Fig, blue and green solid lines), suggesting that the re-emergence of RVF virus in 2008–10 may have been due to the import of infectious animals.
57.Rationale: The passage states that RVF was present in Mayotte since 2004 but re-emerged in 2008-10. The model's fit to serological data does not confirm continuous circulation; instead, the data suggests a re-emergence event in 2008-10. The models that included animal imports were necessary to explain the IgG prevalence peak, indicating that the virus was not continuously circulating but rather introduced through imports. Therefore, the option is incorrect.

57.Option: The model forecasts indicate that the risk of RVF re-emergence in Mayotte is negligible, even in the presence of new animal imports, due to the island's low vector density and stable climate.
Evidence: The model predicted a very low probability of endemicity (Fig 4B, Forecast 1), with 99.74% of the trajectories indicating extinction in 2016 in a closed ecosystem.

However, if the number of infectious animals introduced into Mayotte is less than 40, then the incidence peak remains substantially higher if importations take place in April, compared to importations taking place in October (April: Forecasts 2–5 in S8A–S8D Fig and October: Forecasts 6–10 in S9A–S9D Fig).
57.Rationale: The model forecasts do not indicate a negligible risk of re-emergence. Instead, they show that the risk is significant if infectious animals are introduced, especially during high NDVI periods. The low probability of endemicity in a closed system does not negate the risk from new imports. Therefore, the option is incorrect.

57.Option: The model forecasts indicate a very low probability of RVF virus endemicity and therefore of re-emergence in a closed system, but the livestock population remains vulnerable to the introduction of infectious animals due to a high proportion of naive animals.
Evidence: The model predicted a very low probability of endemicity (Fig 4B, Forecast 1), with 99.74% of the trajectories indicating extinction in 2016 in a closed ecosystem.

As of October 2016, 97.9% (95%CrI [97.6–98.2]) of the Mayotte livestock is estimated to be susceptible to RVF, such that the import of 40 infectious animals at that date (low NDVI values, Fig 4C, Forecast 6) or in April 2017 (high NDVI values, Fig 4D, Forecast 11) would result in an incidence peak similar to 2008.
57.Rationale: The model clearly indicates a very low probability of endemicity in a closed system, which aligns with the first part of the option. Additionally, the high proportion of susceptible animals (97.9%) in 2016 supports the vulnerability to new introductions. The forecasts show that even a small number of imported infectious animals can lead to significant re-emergence, confirming the second part of the option. Therefore, the option is correct.

57.Option: The use of NDVI as a proxy for vector habitat allowed the model to accurately capture the seasonal dynamics of RVF transmission, even in the absence of direct vector data.
Evidence: NDVI data for Mayotte did not show any three-months NDVI anomaly over the study period as measured for the Horn of Africa [10] therefore it was not used in this model (S1 Fig). Instead, we estimated the transmission parameter βt that varied over time as a function of the observed NDVI value at time t (NDVIt) [33].
The seasonal variation of Rst over time reflected the seasonal NDVI values, and was compared to Rst values under the average-NDVI conditions (Fig 4A).
57.Rationale: The model used NDVI as a proxy for vector habitat and successfully captured the seasonal dynamics of RVF transmission. The seasonal variation of the reproduction number (Rst) aligned with NDVI values, demonstrating the effectiveness of NDVI as a proxy despite the lack of direct vector data. Therefore, the option is correct.

57.Option: The model's prediction of a high proportion of susceptible animals in 2016 suggests that the livestock population has developed herd immunity through natural exposure to sub-clinical infections.
Evidence: As of October 2016, 97.9% (95%CrI [97.6–98.2]) of the Mayotte livestock is estimated to be susceptible to RVF.

Models without any animal import (Models 2a and 2b) had the worst fit (Table 2), and failed to capture the IgG prevalence peak (S4 Fig, blue and green solid lines), suggesting that the re-emergence of RVF virus in 2008–10 may have been due to the import of infectious animals.
57.Rationale: The high proportion of susceptible animals in 2016 indicates a lack of herd immunity, not its development. The model's fit to serological data, which required the inclusion of animal imports, suggests that the virus was not circulating naturally to confer immunity. Therefore, the option is incorrect.

58.Option: The persistence of T. infestans populations in the Gran Chaco region is primarily due to low insecticide efficacy in peridomestic structures and unsustained vector control interventions, rather than insecticide resistance.
Evidence: Persistent bug populations that survived the insecticide application at local spatial scales, were related with sources of peridomestic populations, operational failures, reduced residual effect of insecticide or development of resistance to pyrethroid insecticides that decrease the vector control efficacy

The occurrence of insecticide resistance was relatively unexpected for a long life cycled-insect duration (compared with other pest species with insecticide resistance records), relatively low frequency of insecticide applications, unsustained-over-time, and low insecticide efficacy in the peridomestic structures that would leave many residual populations (not necessarily resistant, but susceptible individuals that were not affected by the insecticide that degraded before contacting the insects).
58.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that insecticide resistance is one of the factors contributing to the persistence of T. infestans populations in the Gran Chaco region, alongside low insecticide efficacy and unsustained interventions. The option incorrectly claims that resistance is not a primary factor, which contradicts the evidence presented.

58.Option: The occurrence of pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans is independent of environmental variables and is solely driven by repeated insecticide application across the region.
Evidence: The diversity of resistance mechanisms and the genetic variability around the putative dispersion center of the T. infestans encouraged the consideration of the influence of environmental variables as another potential cause of pyrethroid resistance occurrence

The analysis of the geographic distribution of these two T. infestans populations based on the Log (LD50), with 2.6 as the threshold value, showed a significant fit of the GLM model with the environmental variables as predictors.

The environmental variables Mean Diurnal Range (Bio2), Mean Temperature of the Driest Quarter (Bio9), Precipitation Seasonality (Bio15), Precipitation of the Warmest Quarter (Bio18) are positively correlated, whereas isothermality (Bio3) is negatively correlated with the occurrence of high LD50 populations.
58.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that environmental variables are significantly associated with the occurrence of pyrethroid resistance in T. infestans. The GLM model using environmental variables explained 55% of the variation in resistance levels, indicating that environmental factors play a role. The option incorrectly claims that resistance is solely due to insecticide application and independent of environmental variables.

58.Option: The geographic distribution of highly resistant Triatoma infestans populations is associated with a specific combination of environmental variables, particularly temperature and rainfall patterns, and is spatially limited to the region around the putative center of dispersion of the species.
Evidence: The geographic distribution of these two T. infestans populations based on the Log (LD50), with 2.6 as the threshold value, showed a significant fit of the GLM model with the environmental variables as predictors.

The environmental variables Mean Diurnal Range (Bio2), Mean Temperature of the Driest Quarter (Bio9), Precipitation Seasonality (Bio15), Precipitation of the Warmest Quarter (Bio18) are positively correlated, whereas isothermality (Bio3) is negatively correlated with the occurrence of high LD50 populations.

The area identified as the one where T. infestans populations could show highest LD50 includes the border between Bolivia and Argentina (see S2 Fig), and southward to the east of Salta and north central Santiago del Estero provinces (Argentina). The model predicts a disjunction area towards the border of La Rioja and San Juan provinces (Argentina) and towards the north of the Cochabamba Department (Bolivia) (see S3 Fig).

A map of the distribution of the three T. infestans cytogenetic groups (andean, non-andean and intermediate reported by [21]) and the distribution of Log (LD50) measured on T. infestans populations shows an almost perfect match between the highly resistant T. infestans populations (Log (LD50)> 2.6) and the intermediate cytogenetic group (Fig 3).
58.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that the geographic distribution of highly resistant T. infestans populations is associated with specific environmental variables (temperature and rainfall patterns) and is spatially limited to the region around the putative center of dispersion. The GLM model using environmental variables explained 55% of the variation in resistance levels, and the highly resistant populations are concentrated in the border area between Bolivia and Argentina, which is the putative center of dispersion. The option correctly captures this pattern.

58.Option: The bimodal distribution of LD50 values in T. infestans populations reflects differences in laboratory testing methods rather than biological or environmental variation.
Evidence: The descriptive analysis of LD50 values, showed that the frequency distribution of the Log (LD50) is bimodal, suggesting the existence of two statistical subpopulations (groups).

The analysis of the geographic distribution of these two T. infestans populations based on the Log (LD50), with 2.6 as the threshold value, showed a significant fit of the GLM model with the environmental variables as predictors.

The environmental variables Mean Diurnal Range (Bio2), Mean Temperature of the Driest Quarter (Bio9), Precipitation Seasonality (Bio15), Precipitation of the Warmest Quarter (Bio18) are positively correlated, whereas isothermality (Bio3) is negatively correlated with the occurrence of high LD50 populations.
58.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the bimodal distribution of LD50 values is associated with environmental variables and genetic factors (cytogenetic groups), not laboratory testing methods. The GLM model using environmental variables explained 55% of the variation in resistance levels, indicating that the bimodal distribution reflects biological and environmental variation. The option incorrectly attributes the bimodal distribution to testing methods.

58.Option: The intermediate cytogenetic group of T. infestans is the only population with high resistance to pyrethroids, and its presence is solely due to recent genetic mutations caused by insecticide exposure.
Evidence: A map of the distribution of the three T. infestans cytogenetic groups (andean, non-andean and intermediate reported by [21]) and the distribution of Log (LD50) measured on T. infestans populations shows an almost perfect match between the highly resistant T. infestans populations (Log (LD50)> 2.6) and the intermediate cytogenetic group (Fig 3).

The accumulation of evidence over the last years suggests that the occurrence of insecticide resistance in T. infestans populations is associated with the high genetic variability detected in the historical dispersion site of the species towards the southern cone of South America [21, 42, 43] and the strong spatial structure of the populations derived by low population dispersal rates [24, 44–45].
58.Rationale: The passage indicates that the intermediate cytogenetic group is associated with high resistance, but it does not state that this group is the only one with high resistance or that its resistance is solely due to recent genetic mutations from insecticide exposure. The evidence suggests that genetic variability and historical dispersion patterns are also important factors. The option incorrectly claims exclusivity and recent mutation as the sole cause.

59.Option: The small-scale spatial clustering of dengue cases in both cities suggests that reactive spatially targeted interventions such as insecticide spraying are highly effective in reducing transmission.
Evidence: In Ho Chi Minh City cases occurring within a month and 200m of each other were 1.6 times more likely to be homotypic than the probability of any two cases being homotypic at that time point (95% CI: 1.2–2.1) (Fig 6). This dropped to 1.0 for cases separated by 750m – 1.25km (95% CI: 0.9–1.2). Significant spatial dependence was observed at distances up to 500m.

The magnitude and extent of spatial dependence observed in Ho Chi Minh City were very similar to previously reported results from Bangkok using hospitalized dengue cases from 1995 to 1999. The Bangkok study found cases occurring within the same month were 1.8 times more likely to be homotypic at distances under 200m (95% CI: 1.5–2.2), although significant spatial dependence was observed at distances up to 1km.
59.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of small-scale spatial clustering of dengue cases in both cities, but it does not discuss the effectiveness of reactive spatially targeted interventions like insecticide spraying. The observation of spatial clustering is presented as an epidemiological finding, not as evidence of intervention effectiveness. The inference about intervention effectiveness requires additional information not provided in the passage.

59.Option: The high similarity in the temporal patterns of dengue transmission between Ho Chi Minh City and Bangkok is likely driven by comparable local environmental factors such as human and vector population density and climate, rather than by significant viral flow between the cities.
Evidence: The number of annual cases was highly correlated across the two cities (correlation of 0.77, 95% CI: 0.74–0.79) (Fig 2).

The mean force of infection between 2003 and 2009 was very similar in the two cities with an estimated 2.9% of the susceptible population infected per serotype each year in Ho Chi Minh City (95% CI: 2.8%-3.0%) compared to 2.4% in Bangkok (95% CI: 2.3% - 2.5%).
The pattern in annual force of infections in the two locations tracked each other closely (Fig 2) with a correlation of 0.57 (95% CI 0.46–0.68) between the annual estimates between 2003 and 2009.

Bangkok and Ho Chi Minh City are similar in size, population and climate (Table 1 and S1 Fig). These two urban cities in Southeast Asia share similar characteristics with high population density, high rate of immigrants and typical tropical weather with only two seasons: dry and rainy season.
59.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high similarity in the temporal patterns of dengue transmission between the two cities, with a strong correlation in annual case numbers and force of infection. The cities are described as having similar size, population density, and climate. The passage also notes that previous studies have suggested Thailand may act as a source of virus for the region but acknowledges limitations in phylogenetic studies due to limited sequence availability. The evidence supports the inference that comparable local environmental factors (population density, climate) are likely the primary drivers of the similar temporal patterns, rather than significant viral flow between the cities.

59.Option: The similarity in the annual force of infection between Ho Chi Minh City and Bangkok is likely due to the shared use of a standardized dengue surveillance system across both countries.
Evidence: To allow appropriate comparison in case trends we normalized the annual number of cases from each city.

We estimated the mean force of infection between 2003 and 2009 was very similar in the two cities with an estimated 2.9% of the susceptible population infected per serotype each year in Ho Chi Minh City (95% CI: 2.8%-3.0%) compared to 2.4% in Bangkok (95% CI: 2.3% - 2.5%).
The pattern in annual force of infections in the two locations tracked each other closely (Fig 2) with a correlation of 0.57 (95% CI 0.46–0.68) between the annual estimates between 2003 and 2009.
59.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the annual force of infection was estimated using methods that are robust to differences in surveillance systems. The analysis used age distribution of cases rather than raw case counts, which allows for comparison across locations with different surveillance systems. The similarity in force of infection is attributed to the epidemiological patterns and not to a shared surveillance system. The inference that the similarity is due to a standardized surveillance system is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

59.Option: The absence of synchrony in the prevalence of particular virus serotypes, yet reasonably similar overall epidemiological profiles, suggests that inter-city transmission plays a major role in shaping dengue dynamics in Southeast Asia.
Evidence: We found widely differing levels of correlation by serotype, ranging from high correlation for DENV-1 (the dominant serotype in both locations over the time series) and DENV-3 to no correlation in DENV-2 and negative correlation for DENV-4 (Fig 3). The correlation in annual number of cases was 0.65 (95% CI: 0.54–0.70) for DENV-1, 0.04 (95% CI: -0.19–0.19) for DENV-2, 0.62 (95% CI: 0.35–0.75) for DENV-3 and -0.14 (95% CI: -0.67–0.32) for DENV-4.
59.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of varying levels of correlation by serotype, with some serotypes showing high correlation and others showing no or negative correlation. The inference that inter-city transmission plays a major role in shaping dengue dynamics is not supported by the evidence. In fact, the lack of synchrony in serotype prevalence suggests that inter-city transmission is not a major driver, as significant viral flow would likely result in more consistent serotype patterns across cities. The passage also notes limitations in phylogenetic studies that could provide evidence for inter-city transmission.

59.Option: The high correlation in annual case numbers between the two cities indicates that the dengue epidemics are driven primarily by regional climatic factors that affect both locations simultaneously.
Evidence: The number of annual cases was highly correlated across the two cities (correlation of 0.77, 95% CI: 0.74–0.79) (Fig 2).

Bangkok and Ho Chi Minh City are similar in size, population and climate (Table 1 and S1 Fig). These two urban cities in Southeast Asia share similar characteristics with high population density, high rate of immigrants and typical tropical weather with only two seasons: dry and rainy season.
59.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high correlation in annual case numbers and similar climate between the two cities. However, the inference that regional climatic factors are the primary driver is not directly supported. The passage also discusses other potential factors such as human movement and population immunity. The evidence supports the inference that local environmental factors (including climate) are important, but it does not rule out other factors or establish that regional climatic factors are the primary driver. The inference requires assuming that climate is the primary driver, which is not explicitly stated in the passage.

60.Option: The finding that individuals who reported always wearing shoes outside had reduced odds of T. trichiura infection suggests that promoting footwear use should be a core component of STH prevention in this population.
Evidence: Participants who reported always wearing shoes outside were significantly less likely to have T. trichiura infection (AOR 0.19, 95% CI 0.05–0.71, p = 0.013).
60.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that wearing shoes outside is significantly associated with reduced odds of T. trichiura infection. This supports the epidemiological inference that promoting footwear use could be an effective prevention strategy for STHs in this population. The evidence is specific to T. trichiura, which is one of the helminths transmitted via contaminated soil.

60.Option: The lack of significant differences in A. ceylanicum infection prevalence by sex or age suggests that targeted interventions for this species are unnecessary, and resources should be focused on other STHs.
Evidence: There were no detectable differences in A. ceylanicum infection prevalence by sex or age.
60.Rationale: While the passage states there are no significant differences in A. ceylanicum infection prevalence by sex or age, it does not provide evidence to conclude that targeted interventions are unnecessary. The decision to allocate resources should consider other factors such as overall prevalence, transmission dynamics, and the zoonotic nature of the disease. The passage mentions that A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach incorporating One Health strategies, indicating that targeted interventions may still be necessary.

60.Option: The considerable adult reservoir of soil-transmitted helminth infections in Solomon Islands suggests that treatment should ideally be expanded to entire communities, rather than focusing solely on school-aged children.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of infection with any STH was 63.3%, ranging from 27.5 to 91.5% across villages.

For N. americanus, there was a pattern of increasing risk of infection with age relative to young children aged 1–5 years (6–11 years [AOR 5.16, 95% CI 3.04–8.77, p<0.001]; 12–17 years [AOR 9.55, 95% CI 4.63–19.68, p<0.001]; 18–34 years [AOR 5.79, 95% CI 3.04–11.01, p<0.001]; 35–49 years [AOR 6.91, 95% CI 3.66–13.02, p<0.001]; ≥50 years [AOR 6.32, 95% CI 3.43–11.64, p<0.001]).

For T. trichiura, we observed increasing prevalence by age within children (6–11 years; AOR 3.0, 95% CI 1.49–6.01, p = 0.002) and adolescents (12–17 years; AOR 5.99, 95% CI 2.56–14.00, p<0.001), compared young children.
60.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high overall STH prevalence and a clear pattern of increasing infection risk with age for several helminths, including N. americanus and T. trichiura. This indicates a significant adult reservoir of infection. Given that school-aged children programs may not reach adults, the evidence supports the epidemiological inference that treatment should be expanded to entire communities to effectively control STHs in this population.

60.Option: The higher annual precipitation in the study area is likely to be a key driver of increased T. trichiura transmission, suggesting that climate adaptation strategies should be prioritized in STH control programmes.
Evidence: Greater annual precipitation was associated with marginally higher odds of infection (AOR 1.14, 95% CI 1.04–1.25, p = 0.008), as was co-infection with N. americanus (AOR 3.39, 95% CI 1.66–6.91, p = 0.001).
60.Rationale: The passage indicates that greater annual precipitation is associated with marginally higher odds of T. trichiura infection, but this association is not strong enough to conclude that it is a key driver of transmission. The evidence does not support the inference that climate adaptation strategies should be prioritized in STH control programs. Other factors, such as WASH and environmental conditions, may be more directly modifiable for control.

60.Option: The increased risk of Strongyloides spp. infection in adolescents and adults, combined with the zoonotic nature of A. ceylanicum, indicates that control efforts should focus on veterinary interventions in domestic animals.
Evidence: The increased risk of Strongyloides spp. infection in adolescents and adults

As a zoonotic disease, control of A. ceylanicum requires an intersectoral approach, that incorporates One Health strategies as well as preventive chemotherapy [5] particularly in endemic countries in the Asia-Pacific region where high burdens have been documented [6].
60.Rationale: The passage mentions that A. ceylanicum is a zoonotic disease requiring an intersectoral approach and One Health strategies, but it does not provide evidence that control efforts should focus on veterinary interventions in domestic animals. The increased risk of Strongyloides spp. in adolescents and adults is noted, but the passage does not suggest that this should lead to a focus on veterinary interventions. The evidence does not support the specific inference that veterinary interventions should be the focus of control efforts.

61.Option: The attenuated replication of ZIKV-15555 is due to a loss of glycosylation in the envelope protein, which impairs viral entry into host cells.
Evidence: The GUINEA-18 and MR766MC E proteins differ by six amino-acid substitutions with the cluster of mutations I152T/T156I/H158Y that are part of ZIKV glycan loop (GL) region (Table 1). The GL region of GUINEA-18 but not MR766MC may be N-glycosylated to residue E-N154 [27]. Immunoblot assay using anti-E mAb 4G2 showed that GUINEA-18 E protein migrated slower than MR766MC E protein consistent with a glycan potentially linked to GUINEA-18 E-N154 residue (S1 Fig).
61.Rationale: The passage indicates that GUINEA-18 has a potential N-glycosylation site at E-N154 that is not present in MR766MC. The slower migration of GUINEA-18 E protein in immunoblotting suggests glycosylation. However, the data does not establish that this glycosylation is lost or that it impairs viral entry. In fact, the glycosylation appears to be a feature of GUINEA-18, not a loss. The attenuated replication phenotype is shown to be associated with NS1-NS4B proteins, not the envelope protein glycosylation. This option misrepresents the evidence presented.

61.Option: The 5’ and 3’ non-coding regions of the ZIKV-15555 genome are responsible for its attenuated replication in mammalian cells due to structural instability in the RNA secondary structures.
Evidence: We next examined whether the reduced replicative abilities of GUINEA-18 depended on virus-specific determinants. The replicative properties of GUINEA-18 depend on NS1 to NS4B proteins

The option incorrectly states that the reduced replication is due to an inability to inhibit SG formation, which contradicts the evidence presented.

63.Option: In semi-field experiments, a 3:1 ratio of wAlbB-Tw males to wild-type males was sufficient to achieve 70% population suppression of Ae. aegypti, suggesting that this ratio could serve as a suitable starting point for future release trials in Taiwan.
Evidence: From 2019/04/12–2019/07/02, the field environment temperature and humidity were 27.90 ± 2.31°C, 87.23 ± 3.71% and 27.80 ± 2.20°C, 86.70 ± 2.86% in Site A and Site B, respectively. During this period, the hatch rate of 1:1:0 group and 1:1:3 group was 72.70 ± 21.66% and 16.57 ± 6.80%, respectively. 63.Rationale: The passage states that a 1:1:3 ratio (female WT: male WT: male wAlbB-Tw) resulted in a hatch rate of 16.57 ± 6.80%, compared to 72.70 ± 21.66% in the control group. This represents a significant decrease in hatch rate, but the option incorrectly states this as 70% population suppression. The actual hatch rate is approximately 23% of the control (16.57/72.70 ≈ 0.23), not 70% suppression. The option misrepresents the data and cannot be considered correct.

63.Option: The suppression of wild-type mosquito populations in semi-field trials was not affected by seasonal variations in temperature and humidity, indicating that environmental conditions do not influence the effectiveness of Wolbachia release strategies.
Evidence: According to the above results, the hatch rate was significantly decreased after male wAlbB-Tw competed for mating with male WT in semi-field experiments. The temperature and humidity of Site A and Site B were similar, and the hatch rate in the control group (Site A) was 72.70 ± 21.66%, 59.48 ± 1.12%, and 62.73 ± 19.25%, with no statistical difference between these data points (Fig 7D).

At the same time, we also analyzed the results of each mating competitiveness during the test period (S4 Fig and S4 Table), and found that in our test, the temperature and humidity of the test site did not seem to affect the population suppression results of the semi-field.
63.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that temperature and humidity did not significantly affect the hatch rate in the control group across different seasons and that these environmental factors did not seem to influence the population suppression results in the semi-field experiments. This directly supports the inference that environmental conditions do not influence the effectiveness of Wolbachia release strategies in this context.

63.Option: A 10:1 release ratio of wAlbB-Tw males to wild-type males is optimal for achieving complete population suppression in future trials, as it resulted in 100% suppression in laboratory trials.
Evidence: In the laboratory transparent acrylic cages, WT mosquito populations were suppressed in a ratio-dependent manner with wAlbB-Tw males. At mating ratios of ♂WT and virgin ♀WT vs ♂wAlbB-Tw were 1:1:1, 1:1:3, 1:1:5 and 1:1:10, viable eggs were still observed (egg hatch rate [1:1:1] = 76.28 ± 8.9%, p < 0.001; egg hatch rate [1:1:3] = 42.9 ± 18.1%, p < 0.001; egg hatch rate [1:1:5] = 14.9 ± 10.5%, p < 0.001) but a significant reduction in the egg hatch rate was detected at mating ratios of 1:1:10 (egg hatch rate [1:1:10] = 0.0 ± 0.0%) (S2 Table). Under these conditions, population suppression reached 100% for males released at the ratio of 1:1:10 (Fig 5A).

From 2020/01/17–2020/06/10, the field environment temperature and humidity were 22.53 ± 3.60°C, 84.65 ± 5.37% and 22.70 ± 3.55°C, 84.75 ± 6.21% in Site A and Site B, respectively. During this period, the hatch rate of 1:1:0 group and 1:1:10 group was 62.73 ± 19.25% and 24.45 ± 11.02%, respectively. The hatch rate was significantly decreased in the field containing male wAlbB-Tw (student t-test; p = 0.02, Fig 7C).
63.Rationale: While the passage states that a 1:1:10 ratio achieved 100% suppression in laboratory trials, the semi-field trials at the same ratio only achieved a hatch rate reduction to 24.45 ± 11.02% (from 62.73 ± 19.25% in the control group). This is not 100% suppression, and the option incorrectly assumes that the laboratory results can be directly extrapolated to field conditions without considering the semi-field data. The semi-field results suggest that a 10:1 ratio may not be sufficient for complete suppression in real-world conditions, making this option incorrect.

65.Option: Undernutrition, as defined by low body mass index, is independently associated with an increased risk of developing clinical leprosy, even after adjusting for age, sex, and education.
Evidence: On univariate analysis, both low BMI (OR = 9.25, 95% CI 2.77, 30.81) and low MUAC were significantly associated with leprosy (OR = 6.82, 95% CI 1.78, 26.13).

On multivariate analysis (Table 2), underweight was still significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 10.32, 95% CI 1.79, 59.67) after controlling for age, sex, and education (as a surrogate marker of socioeconomic status).
65.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that undernutrition, as defined by low BMI, is independently associated with an increased risk of developing clinical leprosy. The univariate analysis shows a strong association (OR = 9.25), and this association remains significant in the multivariate analysis after adjusting for age, sex, and education (aOR = 10.32). This indicates that the relationship between undernutrition and leprosy is independent of these demographic factors.

65.Option: The association between underweight status and leprosy in this study population is likely due to the stigma and disability associated with leprosy, which leads to reduced food intake and weight loss.
Evidence: Most individuals affected by leprosy are born and raised in poor environments and continue to be pushed into greater depths of poverty due to the stigma and disabilities associated with the disease [9].
65.Rationale: While the passage mentions that stigma and disability associated with leprosy can lead to poverty, it does not provide evidence that the association between underweight status and leprosy in this study is due to reduced food intake and weight loss caused by leprosy itself. The study design is case-control, and the analysis suggests that undernutrition is a risk factor for leprosy, not a consequence of it. Therefore, this option is not supported by the data presented.

65.Option: Middle Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC) is a more reliable indicator of nutritional status than BMI in predicting clinical leprosy in this population.
Evidence: On univariate analysis, both low BMI (OR = 9.25, 95% CI 2.77, 30.81) and low MUAC were significantly associated with leprosy (OR = 6.82, 95% CI 1.78, 26.13).

On multivariate analysis (Table 2), underweight was still significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 10.32, 95% CI 1.79, 59.67) after controlling for age, sex, and education (as a surrogate marker of socioeconomic status). MUAC, also included in the multivariate model, was no longer statistically significantly associated with leprosy (aOR = 1.50, CI 0.22, 10.05).
65.Rationale: The passage shows that while both BMI and MUAC are significantly associated with leprosy in univariate analysis, only BMI remains significantly associated in the multivariate analysis. This suggests that BMI is a more reliable indicator of nutritional status in predicting clinical leprosy in this population, not MUAC. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

65.Option: The high prevalence of multibacillary (MB) leprosy in this study population suggests that undernutrition is a key driver of disease progression from paucibacillary to multibacillary forms.
Evidence: The majority of cases were MB (90%) and 21 participants (25.9%) had S. mansoni infection.
65.Rationale: The passage states that the majority of cases were MB, but it does not provide evidence that undernutrition is a key driver of disease progression from paucibacillary to multibacillary forms. The study focuses on the association between undernutrition and the risk of developing leprosy, not on the progression from one form to another. Therefore, this option is not supported by the data presented.

65.Option: Food insecurity, measured by having insufficient funds for meals, is a direct cause of leprosy in this study population, as it leads to malnutrition and subsequent immune suppression.
Evidence: Cutting the size of meals/skipping meals (OR = 2.87, 95% CI 1.0, 8.32) or not having enough money to get more food (OR = 10, 95% CI 3.44, 29.06) was more common in cases of leprosy than controls.
65.Rationale: The passage shows that food insecurity is more common in cases of leprosy than in controls, but it does not establish a direct causal relationship between food insecurity and leprosy. The study suggests an association, but causation cannot be inferred from this observational study. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

66.Option: In settings where community members have had negative experiences with prior health campaigns, perceptions of cMDA safety and effectiveness may be undermined, even if overall treatment acceptance remains high.
Evidence: Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more community resistance to previous school-based MDA and vaccination programs. This was due to reports of negative side-effects following treatment that led people to resist the new STH cMDA program.

Participants in India and Malawi, and participants in HCCs in Benin reported more positive experiences with previous community health programs, like vaccination campaigns. These prior experiences positively influenced their perceptions of cMDA safety and willingness to participate.
66.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that negative experiences with prior health campaigns (e.g., reports of negative side effects from previous school-based MDA and vaccination programs) led to resistance in the current cMDA program in LCCs in Benin. However, it also notes that overall treatment acceptance remained high (99.7% in HCCs and 99.6% in LCCs in Benin). This supports the inference that while past negative experiences can undermine perceptions of safety and effectiveness, they do not necessarily lead to low overall acceptance, as the high self-reported acceptance rates indicate.

66.Option: The high self-reported treatment acceptance in both high- and low-coverage clusters suggests that past public health experiences have no meaningful impact on current community attitudes toward cMDA.
Evidence: Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more community resistance to previous school-based MDA and vaccination programs. This was due to reports of negative side-effects following treatment that led people to resist the new STH cMDA program.

Participants in India and Malawi, and participants in HCCs in Benin reported more positive experiences with previous community health programs, like vaccination campaigns. These prior experiences positively influenced their perceptions of cMDA safety and willingness to participate.
66.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that past public health experiences (both positive and negative) influenced current community attitudes toward cMDA. For example, negative experiences in LCCs in Benin led to resistance, while positive experiences in other areas increased willingness to participate. Therefore, the claim that past experiences have no meaningful impact is incorrect.

Evidence: Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more community resistance to previous school-based MDA and vaccination programs. This was due to reports of negative side-effects following treatment that led people to resist the new STH cMDA program.
Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more negative experiences with prior health campaigns, which directly led to lower treatment acceptance rates in the current cMDA program.
66.Rationale: The passage states that participants in LCCs in Benin reported more negative experiences with prior health campaigns, which led to resistance to the new cMDA program. However, it does not explicitly state that these negative experiences directly caused the spread of rumors about cMDA being dangerous. The connection between past experiences and the spread of rumors is implied but not directly stated, making this option an overreach based on the provided evidence.

66.Option: Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more negative experiences with prior health campaigns, which directly led to lower treatment acceptance rates in the current cMDA program.
Evidence: Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more community resistance to previous school-based MDA and vaccination programs. This was due to reports of negative side-effects following treatment that led people to resist the new STH cMDA program.
Participants in LCCs in Benin reported more negative experiences with prior health campaigns, which directly led to lower treatment acceptance rates in the current cMDA program.
66.Rationale: The passage indicates that participants in LCCs in Benin had negative experiences with prior health campaigns, which led to resistance to the new cMDA program. However, it also states that overall treatment acceptance remained high (99.6% in LCCs in Benin). This suggests that while there was resistance, it did not significantly reduce treatment acceptance rates. Therefore, the claim that it directly led to lower treatment acceptance rates is not supported by the evidence.

66.Rationale: The passage states that in India and Malawi, cMDA was perceived as safer and more trustworthy if associated with an NGO rather than a government program. However, it does not provide evidence that this perception was a key barrier to treatment acceptance in all three study sites. The evidence is limited to India and Malawi, and there is no mention of this perception in Benin as a key barrier. Therefore, the claim that it was a key barrier in all three sites is not supported by the evidence.

67.Option: The significantly higher seroprevalence of toxoplasmosis in South India may be primarily attributed to the higher number of multigravida women in this region, as repeated pregnancies increase susceptibility to infection.
Evidence: Most (74.2%) of the 328 seropositive pregnant women were multigravida and only a quarter (25.8%) were primigravida (Figure 5, Flow chart 2).

Of the 208 seropositive pregnant women at the time of sampling, two thirds (138/208 or 66.35%) were in their first trimester while 36/208 (17.30%) were in their second trimester and 34/208 (16.35%) in their third.
67.Rationale: The passage states that most seropositive pregnant women were multigravida, but this is a correlation, not a causal explanation. The passage does not provide evidence that repeated pregnancies increase susceptibility to T. gondii infection. In fact, seroprevalence is presented as increasing with age, not as being caused by multiple pregnancies. The option misinterprets the data by suggesting a causal relationship that is not supported by the passage.

67.Option: The higher seroprevalence in South India is likely due to increased consumption of raw or undercooked meat, which is a well-documented route of Toxoplasma gondii transmission in other countries.
Evidence: Humans usually become infected by ingesting food or water contaminated with cat faeces containing oocysts or by eating under-cooked meat containing the encysted stage of the parasite (tissue cysts).
Consumption of raw salad was common (1360/1464; 92.89%) across the country.
67.Rationale: While the passage mentions that undercooked meat is a transmission route, it does not provide evidence that South India has higher rates of meat consumption or undercooking compared to other regions. The passage also states that raw salad consumption was common across the country, not specifically in South India. The option makes an inference about a regional difference in meat consumption that is not supported by the passage.

The selected population in South India resides in a humid and moderately hot environment.

The difference in the living conditions was highly significant (p<0.0001) between South India and West India and also between South India and North India (p<0.001), while the difference between North and East India was not significant (Figure 3).
The majority of women from South and East India resided in mud-plastered houses and consumed tube well/hand pump water without using any disinfectant or filter.
A history of contact with animals was found in 426 (29.1%) women but pets were significantly more common (53.5%) in South Indian households (Table 3).
67.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the study was designed to investigate the association between environmental factors and toxoplasmosis prevalence. It provides evidence that South India has a humid and moderately hot climate, which could be favorable for oocyst survival. It also shows that South Indian women had significantly different living conditions, including higher rates of pet ownership (cats) and consumption of untreated water, which are known risk factors for T. gondii transmission. These factors together provide a plausible explanation for the higher seroprevalence in South India.

67.Option: The significantly higher seroprevalence in South India is likely due to a higher rate of sexual transmission of Toxoplasma gondii, as suggested by anecdotal reports in the literature.
Evidence: Toxoplasmosis is caused by the protozoan parasite T. gondii. It has a wide host range, infecting most warm-blooded species but the life cycle is completed only in felids; Indeed only cats can shed the environmentally-resistant stage of the parasite (oocyst) in their feces.
Humans usually become infected by ingesting food or water contaminated with cat faeces containing oocysts or by eating under-cooked meat containing the encysted stage of the parasite (tissue cysts).
67.Rationale: The passage clearly states that T. gondii is not sexually transmitted. It explains that transmission occurs through ingestion of oocysts in cat feces or tissue cysts in undercooked meat. The option introduces a transmission route (sexual) that is not mentioned or supported by the passage. This is a scientifically incorrect inference based on the information provided.

67.Option: The high seroprevalence in South India may be due to the use of untreated water sources, which are more common in rural areas and have been linked to waterborne outbreaks of toxoplasmosis.
Evidence: The majority of women from South and East India resided in mud-plastered houses and consumed tube well/hand pump water without using any disinfectant or filter.

The difference in the living conditions was highly significant (p<0.0001) between South India and West India and also between South India and North India (p<0.001), while the difference between North and East India was not significant (Figure 3).
67.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that South Indian women were more likely to consume untreated water sources (tube well/hand pump water without disinfectant or filter) compared to women in other regions. This is a known risk factor for T. gondii transmission, as oocysts can contaminate water sources. The significant difference in living conditions between South India and other regions supports this as a plausible explanation for the higher seroprevalence.

68.Option: The fact that only one human case of Lassa fever has been documented in Mali despite high rodent infection rates suggests that human exposure to Lassa virus is rare or ineffective in causing disease.
Evidence: The purpose of this study was to expand upon these original findings and better define the geographical distribution of LASV-infected M. natalensis in sub-Saharan Mali as well as provide an in-depth genetic characterization of novel LASV isolates from this region.
Subsequent field studies conducted in the village of Soromba, where the man was living and working in Mali, demonstrated the presence of LASV-infected rodents [17].
68.Rationale: The passage does not state that only one human case has been documented in Mali. It mentions a specific case in Soromba and another imported case, but does not provide comprehensive data on total human cases in Mali. The inference that human exposure is rare or ineffective in causing disease cannot be drawn from the passage because the data on human cases is incomplete and the focus of the study is on rodent populations, not human incidence rates.

68.Option: The presence of Lassa virus in rodents captured in peridomestic settings, particularly in kitchens and bedrooms, suggests a high likelihood of human exposure to the virus in southern Mali.
Evidence: Trapping was conducted in various ecozones of sub-Saharan Mali including the grasslands and croplands south of the Sahel to the open and wooded savannas of Southern Mali [19]. Small mammals were live-trapped at each location for one to three consecutive nights using Sherman traps (H.B. Sherman Traps) baited with a mixture of onions and roasted peanuts and set in areas with known or suspected rodent activity.

The diversity of captures was greatest in the northern regions, especially around the Niger inland delta. In contrast, little or no rodent diversity was observed in the southern regions of Mali around the village of Soromba where, during three field expeditions, all but two animals captured in these areas were M. natalensis (246/248, 99.2%).
68.Rationale: The passage describes trapping in peridomestic settings, which are areas where humans and rodents interact closely, such as kitchens and bedrooms. The high prevalence of LASV-infected rodents in these areas, particularly in southern Mali, directly supports the inference that human exposure to the virus is likely. The close proximity of infected rodents to human dwellings increases the risk of transmission through inhalation or ingestion of virus-contaminated materials.

68.Option: The presence of Lassa virus in adult rodents with active viral replication but no detectable antibodies suggests that these animals are likely to be the primary source of human infection due to high viral shedding potential.
Evidence: Combining the results of serological and molecular testing, a total of 48 (19.4%) rodents, all M. natalensis, demonstrated evidence of LASV infection. Twenty-nine animals were seropositive and RT-PCR negative; six animals were both seropositive and RT-PCR positive; and the remaining thirteen animals were seronegative but RT-PCR positive (Table 2).
68.Rationale: The passage states that 13 rodents were seronegative but RT-PCR positive, indicating active viral replication without detectable antibodies. However, it does not provide evidence that these animals are the primary source of human infection. The inference about high viral shedding potential and being the primary source requires additional data on shedding rates and transmission dynamics, which are not provided in the passage.

68.Option: The detection of Lassa virus in rodents from multiple villages in southern Mali indicates that the virus is likely to be endemic in the region and may be spreading to neighboring areas, including western Mali.
Evidence: The purpose of this study was to expand upon these original findings and better define the geographical distribution of LASV-infected M. natalensis in sub-Saharan Mali as well as provide an in-depth genetic characterization of novel LASV isolates from this region.

The diversity of captures was greatest in the northern regions, especially around the Niger inland delta. In contrast, little or no rodent diversity was observed in the southern regions of Mali around the village of Soromba where, during three field expeditions, all but two animals captured in these areas were M. natalensis (246/248, 99.2%).
68.Rationale: The passage confirms the presence of LASV in southern Mali but does not provide evidence that the virus is spreading to neighboring areas, including western Mali. The study's focus is on defining the geographical distribution within southern Mali, and there is no mention of expansion to other regions. The inference about spreading to neighboring areas is speculative and not supported by the data presented.

68.Option: The lack of other rodent species in southern Mali, where infected rodents were documented, may indicate that low biodiversity increases the risk of human disease by promoting sustained transmission of Lassa virus in the rodent reservoir.
Evidence: The diversity of captures was greatest in the northern regions, especially around the Niger inland delta. In contrast, little or no rodent diversity was observed in the southern regions of Mali around the village of Soromba where, during three field expeditions, all but two animals captured in these areas were M. natalensis (246/248, 99.2%).
68.Rationale: The passage notes that southern Mali has low rodent diversity, with M. natalensis being the dominant species. This low biodiversity could lead to sustained transmission of LASV within the rodent population, increasing the risk of human exposure. The epidemiological principle that low biodiversity can enhance disease transmission in reservoir species supports this inference. The high prevalence of LASV in M. natalensis in this area further supports the idea that low biodiversity may contribute to the risk of human disease.

69.Option: The optimized in vitro assay is robust and capable of detecting subtle differences in morphological changes induced by compounds on juveniles and ex vivo harvested adults, and can be used to identify compounds with activity on advanced liver stages of S. mansoni.
Evidence: The electron microscopic examination of the 29–day old schistosomula tegument (NMRI strain) from its dorsal side showed a granular cytoplasm containing scattered small mitochondria and many inclusion bodies that differ in size and shape (S3A–S3C Fig).
The TEM of the 77–day old parasite tegument shows the exact characteristics of the 29–day old schistosomula described above except that the spines are larger in size and more in number (S4A–S4C Fig).

Out of 340 compounds, 20 compounds were identified as primary hits. In the next step, these hits were then tested on in vitro juvenile worms at three concentrations (3.333 μM, 10 μM, and 30 μM) in duplicates. The same compounds were also tested on adult worms at a single concentration, 30 μM.
Translation from juveniles to adult worms in terms of phenotypic changes was confirmed for two compounds, perhexiline and astemizole (Table 1 and Fig 6).
69.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the robustness of the in vitro assay. Electron microscopy confirmed the development of advanced liver stages (29-77 days old) with distinct morphological features. The assay's ability to detect compound-induced changes is demonstrated by the identification of 20 primary hits from 340 compounds and the confirmation of phenotypic effects on both juveniles and adults. The translation of effects from juveniles to adults for perhexiline and astemizole further validates the assay's capacity to detect activity on advanced liver stages.

69.Option: The platform's ability to detect subtle morphological changes in juveniles and adults suggests it is particularly suited for identifying compounds with irreversible effects on parasite viability.
Evidence: The damage to juveniles was irreversible after removal of perhexiline with a further decline in viability at day 7 (168 h) (S6 Fig).

In the case of astemizole, juveniles did not recover on day 7 (168 h). Instead, their viability further declined even after removal of astemizole (S7 Fig); the majority of liver stages were densely granulated, disintegrated and dark brown, and in the ones with shape, motility was present but reduced.
Although perhexiline and astemizole caused irreversible damage to juvenile worms, the two compounds did not show a detrimental effect on adult worms.
69.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes irreversible effects of perhexiline and astemizole on juvenile worms, with continued decline in viability after compound removal. The platform's ability to detect these irreversible effects is demonstrated through detailed morphological observations and viability scoring over time. The contrast with adult worms' response further highlights the platform's sensitivity to irreversible effects.

69.Option: The use of human serum in the culture medium better mimics the in vivo setting where the drug binds to serum proteins, and consequently, less free drug is therefore available to exert its activity.
Evidence: The culture medium for adult worms was the same as for juveniles and consisted of HybridoMed Diff 1000 (HM) (Cat. No. F 8055/1, Biochrom GmbH, Germany) supplemented with 200 U/mL penicillin and 200 μg/mL streptomycin (Sigma-Aldrich).

For primary screening, the culture medium contained either 20% donor or commercial (S-106B-EU) HSe. For confirmatory testing of compounds on juveniles and adult worms, commercial HSe (S-106B-EU) was supplemented in culture medium at 20%.
69.Rationale: While the passage describes the use of human serum in the culture medium, it does not provide any information about drug-serum protein binding or the availability of free drug. The focus is on the medium's ability to support parasite development rather than on pharmacokinetic factors. The statement about drug binding to serum proteins is an external assumption not supported by the passage's evidence.

69.Option: The optimized in vitro assay is capable of identifying compounds with activity on early-stage schistosomula, which are more vulnerable to drug exposure than adult worms.
Evidence: The majority of schistosomula in each well were in liver stage for NMRI strain, and the aim was to assess the activity of compounds on juveniles, viability score was assigned only to liver stages.
Translation from juveniles to adult worms in terms of phenotypic changes was confirmed for two compounds, perhexiline and astemizole (Table 1 and Fig 6).
Although perhexiline and astemizole caused irreversible damage to juvenile worms, the two compounds did not show a detrimental effect on adult worms.
69.Rationale: The passage indicates that the assay primarily targets liver stages (advanced juveniles) rather than early-stage schistosomula. The focus on liver stages is explicitly stated in the methodology. The comparison between juvenile and adult responses shows that some compounds are more effective against juveniles, but the passage does not claim that early-stage schistosomula are more vulnerable than adults. The statement misrepresents the assay's focus and the data presented.

70.Option: Asymptomatic cysticercosis can be detected in children in endemic areas, and serological screening of children may be highly informative for early detection and treatment.
Evidence: A total of 208 serum samples were examined by ELISA.
Glycoproteins (GPs) (1.0 µg/ml) from T. solium cyst fluid purified by preparative isoelectric focusing (pH 9.2–9.6) were used for screening of cysticercosis by ELISA [16].
Immunoblot using a recombinant chimeric antigen, 100% specific to cysticercosis (0.5 µg/mini gel) was applied for serological confirmation of cysticercosis [16]–[19].
The number of cysticercosis seropositivity was rather small (n = 3), we did not analyze the ORs of cysticercosis seropositivity.
70.Rationale: The passage states that only 3 cases of cysticercosis seropositivity were detected in the study population of 208 serum samples. It does not provide any information about whether these cases were asymptomatic or whether serological screening in children would be 'highly informative' for early detection. The small number of cases (n=3) suggests that the study was underpowered to draw conclusions about asymptomatic cases in children. The passage also does not mention any specific findings in children regarding asymptomatic infection.

70.Option: The two children with confirmed cysticercosis were symptomatic and exhibited neurological symptoms consistent with epilepsy.
Evidence: The number of cysticercosis seropositivity was rather small (n = 3), we did not analyze the ORs of cysticercosis seropositivity.
Our previous report in this area showed that 8.3% of enrolled people (14/168) suffered from epilepsy but only one of the epileptic patients simultaneously suffered from paragonimiasis [11].
70.Rationale: The passage mentions 3 cases of cysticercosis seropositivity but does not specify how many of these were children. It also does not provide any information about whether these cases were symptomatic or exhibited neurological symptoms. The only information about epilepsy in the study population is that 8.3% of enrolled people had epilepsy, but only one of these had paragonimiasis. There is no direct evidence in the passage about the clinical presentation of the cysticercosis cases.

70.Option: The detection of Paragonimus eggs in sputum was more sensitive than serological testing for diagnosing paragonimiasis in this population.
Evidence: Sputum was examined for eggs of P. africanus [11].
Somatic antigens of P. africanus adult worms (5µg/ml), which showed few cross reactivity with other parasitic infections were used for paragonimiasis [11].
70.Rationale: The passage describes both sputum examination for Paragonimus eggs and serological testing for paragonimiasis, but it does not provide any comparative data on the sensitivity of these two diagnostic methods. The passage does not state which method is more sensitive, nor does it provide any evidence to support this claim.

70.Option: The presence of cysticercosis in children is likely due to consumption of raw or undercooked pork, based on the serological findings.
Evidence: Cysticercosis caused by Taenia solium metacestodes has been assumed to be the most common parasitic infection of the brain worldwide including Cameroon [3]–[5].
The study population was asked whether they consumed raw and/or undercooked fresh water crabs or pork.
The number of cysticercosis seropositivity was rather small (n = 3), we did not analyze the ORs of cysticercosis seropositivity.
70.Rationale: The passage establishes that cysticercosis is caused by T. solium metacestodes, which is a cestode that uses humans as definitive hosts and pigs as intermediate hosts. The life cycle of T. solium involves humans becoming infected by consuming raw or undercooked pork containing cysticerci. The study population was specifically asked about pork consumption, which is the established mode of transmission for cysticercosis. While the passage does not explicitly state that the children with cysticercosis consumed pork, the serological findings combined with the known epidemiology of the disease support the inference that pork consumption is the likely source of infection.

70.Option: The high prevalence of Toxocara seropositivity in children suggests that toxocariasis is the primary cause of epilepsy in this endemic region.
Evidence: A recombinant antigen of T. canis second-stage larvae (0.5 µg/ml) was used for toxocaraisis [15].
Among these neglected helminthic diseases, toxocariasis is expected to have cosmopolitan distribution, since dogs and cats are companion animals with close contact with people in the world [7], [8].
Although there are no data on the prevalence of human toxocariasis in Cameroon, its prevalence in dogs in Cameroon is high [9].
70.Rationale: The passage does not provide any data on the prevalence of Toxocara seropositivity in the study population. It only mentions that a serological test for toxocariasis was performed. The claim about 'high prevalence' is not supported by the passage. Additionally, while the passage suggests that toxocariasis may be a cause of epilepsy, it does not provide evidence that it is the 'primary' cause in this region. The study was designed to investigate multiple potential causes of epilepsy, and the passage does not present any conclusive evidence about the relative contribution of toxocariasis.

71.Option: Despite high geographical and therapeutic coverage of ivermectin mass drug administration, the continuous transmission of Onchocerca volvulus in the Mbam drainage basin is maintained by abundant blackflies with high vector capacity and efficiency, which can acquire microfilariae from infected individuals who do not comply with treatment or harbor parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin.
Evidence: Following IVM-MDA, we collected blackflies within 9 months to analyse the changes of entomological indicators during the observation period.

In some of the communities, about 60% of individuals screened for onchocerciasis were still harbouring Mf in their skin [8]. The presence of infection in humans even after numerous rounds of IVM treatment means that there is continuous transmission by the vectors.
The entomological indicators for onchocerciasis transmission in this area before and following MDA are unknown.
The biting rate of Simulium vector for O. volvulus in the Mbam river basin fluctuates between 729 and 1,539 flies/man/day (Fig 2).

The infection rate was 25.1 per 1,000 parous flies before IVM-MDA and 66.8 per 1,000 parous flies at 30 days post control intervention (Fig 3). It later decreased gradually at 90 and 180 days post treatment but rose back to 57.2 per 1,000 parous flies at 270 days post treatment.

The monthly transmission potential of O. volvulus infective larvae from flies to humans dropped from 340 L3H/man/month before IVM-MDA to 229 L3H/man/month after 30 days of treatment. The MTP further decreased to 95 L3H/man/month at D90 but drastically increased to 205 and 471 L3H/man/month at six and nine months post treatment, respectively (Fig 4).
The high speed and volume of water plus the presence of rocks and vegetation in the river serves as excellent breeding site for blackflies in both the rainy and dry seasons of the year.
71.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. First, it explicitly states that despite over 20 rounds of annual CDTI, 60% of individuals in some communities still harbored microfilariae, indicating ongoing transmission. Second, the entomological data shows that the biting rate of Simulium vectors remained high (729-1,539 flies/man/day) and the infection rate in vectors fluctuated but remained above 19 per 1,000 parous flies at all time points. Third, the MTP increased to 471 L3H/man/month at 9 months post-MDA, suggesting sustained transmission potential. Finally, the description of the Mbam river drainage system with its dams and vegetation provides an ecological explanation for the abundance of blackflies. These factors together support the inference that continuous transmission is maintained by abundant vectors acquiring microfilariae from untreated or partially treated individuals.

71.Option: The construction of the Mape and Bamendjing dams has led to a permanent increase in blackfly breeding sites, making onchocerciasis elimination impossible in the Mbam drainage basin without large-scale dam removal.
Evidence: The analysis of the Mbam river drainage revealed a very high rate of water discharge during both rainy and dry seasons in Bafia (study site) due to the presence of two dams on the Mbam tributaries (Noun and Mape) at Bamendjing and Mape, respectively.
The high speed and volume of water plus the presence of rocks and vegetation in the river serves as excellent breeding site for blackflies in both the rainy and dry seasons of the year.
71.Rationale: While the passage describes how the dams contribute to blackfly breeding by maintaining high water discharge and creating suitable habitats, it does not state that dam removal is necessary for onchocerciasis elimination. The inference that dam removal is required is an overreach beyond the evidence presented. The passage only describes the ecological impact of the dams, not the feasibility or necessity of dam removal as a control strategy.

71.Option: The high monthly transmission potential (MTP) observed at 6 and 9 months post-MDA indicates that ivermectin treatment is ineffective in reducing microfilarial load in the human population, leading to sustained transmission by vectors.
Evidence: If IVM is administered in large scale, there will be a potential reduction of microfilaria (Mf) in the human population as it is known that a single standard dose (150 μg/kg body weight) of IVM can induce 98–99% reduction in skin microfilarial (Mf) load of O. volvulus within one month of treatment [16].
An evaluation of the impact of IVM-MDA showed a significant decrease (53.5% reduction) in the infection rate of the vector population within two months post treatment [17].

The monthly transmission potential of O. volvulus infective larvae from flies to humans dropped from 340 L3H/man/month before IVM-MDA to 229 L3H/man/month after 30 days of treatment. The MTP further decreased to 95 L3H/man/month at D90 but drastically increased to 205 and 471 L3H/man/month at six and nine months post treatment, respectively (Fig 4).
71.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that ivermectin is effective in reducing microfilarial load in humans (98-99% reduction within one month) and in reducing vector infection rates (53.5% reduction within two months). The increase in MTP at 6 and 9 months post-MDA is not attributed to ivermectin ineffectiveness but rather to ecological factors and ongoing transmission. The passage does not provide evidence that ivermectin is ineffective in reducing microfilarial load in this population.

71.Option: The detection of O. ochengi in two body pools at 9 months post-MDA suggests that animal Onchocerca species are a significant source of transmission in the Mbam drainage basin, contributing to the persistence of onchocerciasis despite ivermectin treatment.
Evidence: Using the triplex real-time PCR for the detection of O. ochengi parasites (larvae) in the Simulium damnosum, none of the 129 head pools were positive (0%) while two of the 129 body pools (1.6%) had positive signals. This gave us a zero infective rate and an infection rate of 1.4 (n = 129 pools of 100 flies with 127 negative pools) per 1,000 parous flies. The two pools that were positive for O. ochengi were collected at 9 months post MDA in April 2017.
71.Rationale: The passage reports the detection of O. ochengi in only two body pools (1.6% of 129 pools), resulting in an infection rate of 1.4 per 1,000 parous flies.
