This directly supports the option's claim that MDA programs should focus on achieving and maintaining worm burdens below this threshold to ensure sustained transmission suppression.

145.Option: The marked up-regulation of neutrophil chemokines and the number of neutrophils in the liver at 7 weeks p.i. was striking. Most striking was the marked up-regulation of S100A8 and S100A9 compared with uninfected controls at 6 (S100A8: 91-fold, p≤0.01; S100A9: 62-fold, p≤0.01) and 7 weeks p.i. (138-fold, p≤0.01; S100A9: 153-fold, p≤0.01).
145.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of neutrophil recruitment and chemokine up-regulation during S. japonicum-induced liver pathology. Neutrophils were first observed in the liver at 6 weeks p.i. and their numbers increased significantly by 7 weeks p.i., localizing to granulomas and fibrotic areas. The option correctly notes the ambiguity of the precise role of neutrophils in fibrosis, which is consistent with the passage's focus on describing patterns rather than mechanisms.

However, the passage does not provide evidence of a direct temporal relationship between TLR expression and these cytokines. 145.Option: The up-regulation of CCL7 and CCL12 during fibrosis is consistent with the role of the related chemokine CCL2 in other liver diseases [44]. CCL2 is a chemoattractant for HSCs [44] and its expression is associated with fibrogenesis in both human cholestatic liver disease and in the bile-duct-ligated rat model of cholestatic liver injury [45]. In contrast, there was no significant change in its expression during S. japonicum infection; instead, CCL21, CXCL1, CCL12 and particularly CCL7, may promote the initiation of S. japonicum induced fibrosis by recruiting fibrogenic effector cells and by directly inducing fibrogenic signalling pathways.

CCL12 is listed among chemokines with monocyte/macrophage chemotactic activity that are up-regulated in clusters 2 and 4, which are associated with fibrosis. The option correctly notes that CCL2 expression was not significantly changed in S. japonicum infection, in contrast to its role in S. mansoni, and highlights the role of CCL21, CXCL1, CCL12, and CCL7 in promoting fibrosis through recruitment of fibrogenic effector cells and direct signaling pathways.

Hierarchical clustering identified distinct gene expression clusters associated with different phases of infection. For example, Cluster 3 includes chemokines like CXCL13, CXCL16, CXCL1, and CCL3 that are consistently up-regulated throughout infection, with increased expression at 6–7 weeks p.i. Clusters 2 and 4 include chemokines like CCL7, CCL12, and S100A8 that peak at 6–7 weeks p.i., coinciding with fibrosis. The high rate of re-infection with the same ompA type (67%) suggests that protective immunity is not effective against homologous strains, since re-infection with the same strain occurred frequently. The option incorrectly states that this pattern suggests immunity is 'effective against heterologous strains,' when the data actually supports the opposite: that immunity is not effective against the same strain (hence the re-infection) but may be more effective against different strains. This contradicts the claim that the MOMP is 'highly conserved' and supports the idea that it is variable, which would make it a less reliable target for universal vaccine development. Of 21 children, 14 (67%, 95%CI 43–85%) had the same strain at both timepoints, which was significantly more than would be expected by chance (permutation P<0.001; Fig 3B).

Animal studies suggest that natural protective immunity mediated through MOMP may be serovar-specific, since experimental re-infection with the same serovar causes less clinical inflammation than the primary infection, whereas re-infection with a different serovar produces a similar degree of inflammation as the first infection.

This suggests that vaccines targeting only MOMP may not provide broad protection against different strains. The data supports the idea that immunity is not cross-protective, which implies that vaccines containing multiple antigenic epitopes (not just MOMP) would be more effective. Evidence: Infection rates of 68% were found for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50) after intrathoracic injection.

Evidence: Infection rates of 68% were found for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50) after intrathoracic injection.

Evidence: Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens.

SHUV titers of virus-infected Cx. p. pipiens body samples were almost all below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3C). This indicates that even when SHUV is injected into the thorax, there is no productive virus replication.

This contradicts the claim that the species is 'a competent vector capable of sustaining and spreading the virus in natural settings'. 150.Option: IL-32γ is associated with the induction of microbicidal molecules that contribute to control Leishmania species infection in human macrophages.
Evidence: Fig 5C shows a significant decrease of iNOS and nitrite production after 24 h of infection with both Leishmania species in cells silenced for IL-32 compared to control cells.
In addition, cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as antimicrobial peptide LL-37 concentration were strongly reduced in THP-1-derived macrophages depleted of IL-32 (Fig 5D and 5E).

In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6A and 6B).

Moreover cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as LL-37 peptide production were strongly increased when IL-32γ was overexpressed, especially in infection with L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6D and 6E).
150.Rationale: The evidence directly supports the conclusion that IL-32γ is associated with the induction of microbicidal molecules (iNOS, nitrite, cathelicidin, β-defensin 2, and LL-37) that contribute to controlling Leishmania species infection. The experimental data show that silencing IL-32γ reduces these microbicidal molecules and increases infection, while overexpression increases them and reduces infection. This demonstrates a clear functional role for IL-32γ in the host's defense mechanism against Leishmania.

150.Option: The induction of IL-32γ by L. (L.) amazonensis is primarily due to LPS contamination in the parasite culture, which confounds the interpretation of the results.
Evidence: L. (L.) amazonensis induced higher IL-32γ expression than L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 1A).
L. (L.) amazonensis induced considerably higher levels of TNFα, IL-1Ra and IL-10 than L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 1C).

The study used L. (L.) amazonensis (IFLA/BR/67/PH8) reference strain and MHOM/BR/2003/IMG L. (V.) braziliensis, a clinical isolate obtained from cutaneous lesion of LCL patient (Leishbank IPTSP/UFG) [34].
Parasites of L. (L.) amazonensis from stationary phase (6th—7th day) of growth were used to infect macrophages derived from THP-1 cells.

For L. (V.) braziliensis, parasites were collected from stationary phase (6th—7th day) of growth and metacyclic promastigotes were negatively selected using Bauhinia purpurea lectin according to the protocol described by [35].

Parasites were washed three times with sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) pH 7.4 (1,000 g, 10 min, 10°C). The suspensions were diluted into 0.4% formaldehyde in PBS for parasite quantification by hemocytometer.
100 ng/mL of E. coli LPS (O111:B4 Sigma-Aldrich) was used as a control. This commercial LPS was further purified based on [37].
150.Rationale: The evidence does not support the claim that LPS contamination is the primary reason for IL-32γ induction by L. (L.) amazonensis. The study used purified parasite cultures and a purified LPS control. The differential induction of IL-32γ by the two Leishmania species suggests a specific response to the parasites themselves rather than LPS contamination. The experimental design and purification steps make LPS contamination an unlikely explanation for the observed results.

150.Option: IL-32γ overexpression in THP-1 cells causes an increase in TNFα, IL-6, and IL-8 production, which is linked to enhanced cell death and reduced parasite control.
Evidence: In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6A and 6B).

Moreover cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as LL-37 peptide production were strongly increased when IL-32γ was overexpressed, especially in infection with L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6D and 6E).
Increased intracellular IL-32γ concentrations are associated with cell death [41]. However we observed LDH concentrations remained stable following Leishmania infection (S1C Fig).
150.Rationale: The evidence contradicts the claim that IL-32γ overexpression is linked to enhanced cell death and reduced parasite control. The study found that IL-32γ overexpression was associated with reduced infection and increased microbicidal molecules, not increased cell death. The LDH levels remained stable, indicating no significant cell death. Therefore, the option is incorrect as it misrepresents the findings.

150.Option: IL-32γ regulates the balance between pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators in a way that promotes chronic infection by suppressing immune activation.
Evidence: IL-32γ overexpression resulted in significant increases in TNFα and IL-8 mRNA expression and protein production after infection with either Leishmania species in comparison with egfp transfected control cells (Fig 4B).

In accordance with IL-32γ silencing results, no differences in IL-1β levels were observed whereas increased IL-1Ra mRNA and protein expression was detected only after infection with L. (L.) amazonensis (Fig 4C).

In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6A and 6B).

Moreover cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as LL-37 peptide production were strongly increased when IL-32γ was overexpressed, especially in infection with L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 6D and 6E).
150.Rationale: The evidence shows that IL-32γ enhances immune activation by increasing pro-inflammatory mediators (TNFα, IL-8) and microbicidal molecules, which reduces infection. This contradicts the claim that IL-32γ promotes chronic infection by suppressing immune activation. The data indicate that IL-32γ supports immune responses that control Leishmania infection rather than suppressing them.

The phrase 'perhaps the most likely zoonotic source' supports the idea that chickens could act as a potential source of zoonotic infection. Therefore, this inference can be drawn from the passage.

156.Option: The rapid systemic invasion and intense inflammatory response observed in chickens infected with ST313 suggest that this pathotype is evolving toward a 'stealth infection' strategy similar to S. Gallinarum and S. Pullorum.
Evidence: Genomic analysis of D23580, an MDR ST313 isolated in Blantyre, Malawi, revealed that ST313 has undergone genomic degradation including pseudogene formation and chromosomal deletion that is a feature of host-adapted serovars such as S. Typhi and S. Gallinarum, that cause systemic typhoidal disease in their hosts.

Both ST313 isolates were found to colonize the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
Infection with ST313 D23580 elicited a mean 11- fold increase in CXCLi1 gene expression and 20- fold increase CXCLi2 gene expression in the ileum at 3 days post-infection.
156.Rationale: The passage mentions that ST313 has genomic features similar to host-adapted serovars like S. Gallinarum, but it does not describe a 'stealth infection' strategy. The experimental findings show rapid systemic invasion and intense inflammatory response, which is the opposite of a stealth infection strategy. A stealth infection would typically avoid triggering a strong immune response. Therefore, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage.

157.Option: PORT is more likely to occur in patients with systemic immunosuppression, suggesting that immunosuppressed individuals should be prioritized for routine screening with multimodal imaging during posterior uveitis evaluations.
Evidence: Atypical presentations are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression.

The aim of the study is to describe clinical findings, serology, multimodal imaging, and visual prognosis in patients with PORT to facilitate further recognition of this pathology and thereby decrease the frequency of irreversible complications leading to legal blindness.
157.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that atypical presentations of ocular toxoplasmosis, including PORT, are more frequent in immunosuppressed patients. This directly supports the inference that immunosuppressed individuals are at higher risk for PORT. The study's stated aim to facilitate recognition through multimodal imaging further supports the recommendation for prioritizing immunosuppressed patients for such evaluations. The evidence provided in the passage directly supports this epidemiological inference.

157.Option: Since PORT lesions are often located in the macula and can lead to legal blindness, all patients with posterior uveitis should receive immediate intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy to prevent choroidal neovascularization.
Evidence: Complications encountered include choroidal neovascularization, serous retinal detachment, and macular cysts that may convert to what is known as huge outer retinal cystoid space (HORC).
157.Rationale: While the passage mentions that choroidal neovascularization is a complication of PORT, it does not support the inference that all posterior uveitis patients should receive immediate anti-VEGF therapy. The passage provides no evidence about the routine use of anti-VEGF therapy for all posterior uveitis cases, nor does it suggest that this is a standard management approach for PORT. The option makes an overgeneralization not supported by the passage.

157.Option: PORT represents a diagnostic challenge; therefore, clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.
Evidence: The aim of the study is to describe clinical findings, serology, multimodal imaging, and visual prognosis in patients with PORT to facilitate further recognition of this pathology and thereby decrease the frequency of irreversible complications leading to legal blindness.
PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
157.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the study aims to facilitate recognition of PORT through multimodal imaging. The description of PORT as having specific characteristics (multifocal grey-white lesions with minimal vitreous inflammation) suggests that it can be challenging to diagnose without appropriate imaging techniques. The evidence directly supports the inference that clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.

157.Option: The high rate of recurrence in PORT (25%) compared to classical OT (up to 79%) indicates that patients with PORT require more aggressive long-term immunosuppressive therapy to prevent reactivation.
Evidence: Recurrences when they appear, usually involve the adjacent retina.
157.Rationale: The passage does not provide any specific data on recurrence rates for PORT or classical OT. The option makes a specific numerical comparison (25% vs 79%) that is not mentioned in the passage. Additionally, the passage does not discuss immunosuppressive therapy as a treatment approach for PORT. The inference about requiring more aggressive immunosuppressive therapy is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

157.Option: The similarity between PORT and occult macular dystrophy (OMD) on OCT imaging suggests that patients with suspected posterior uveitis should undergo full-field and multifocal electroretinography to differentiate between the two conditions.
Evidence: The study used available images including OCT, fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence (FAF).
157.Rationale: The passage does not mention any similarity between PORT and occult macular dystrophy (OMD) on OCT imaging. While the study used OCT imaging, there is no evidence in the passage to suggest that electroretinography is necessary to differentiate PORT from OMD. The inference about using electroretinography is not supported by the evidence provided in the passage.

158.Option: The induction of autophagy promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis, as evidenced by increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher infection rates in Vero cells when autophagy is stimulated by rapamycin or spermidine.
Evidence: Our results showed that the percentage of MT differentiated up to 48 h was higher in both TAU medium at the first (TAU-F) and the total (TAU-T) period of metacyclogenesis than in controls (12.1 +/- 0.9% and 16.4 +/- 1.7% vs. 3.9 +/- 0,6%, Fig 6A).
Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
Conversely, Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%).

In another set of experiments, samples were allowed to infect Vero cells monolayers during 24 h and then fixed and processed for confocal microscopy. The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions, as previously described [22].
Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.
The presence of Wort at either the first (Wort-F) or the whole (Wort-T) period of differentiation in TAU significantly reduced the infection rate.
In concordance to the above result, the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).
158.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that autophagy induction promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis. The data show that TAU medium (which induces autophagy) leads to significantly higher metacyclic trypomastigote (MT) formation compared to controls. Rapamycin, an autophagy inducer, also increases MT formation and infection rates in Vero cells. Conversely, wortmannin, an autophagy inhibitor, reduces both MT formation and infection rates. These findings directly support the inference that autophagy induction promotes metacyclogenesis and subsequent infectivity.

158.Option: Spermidine and spermine exert their proautophagic effects in T. cruzi by directly inhibiting the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to transcriptional upregulation of ATG genes.
Evidence: The polyamine spermidine (Spd) has been recently described as a new modulator of autophagy since Spd inhibits the activity of histone acetyl transferase, leading to the upregulation of several ATG genes including ATG7, ATG11 and ATG15 [9].

When added to culture media, Spd is also able to directly induce autophagy in a transcription-independent manner. The mechanism has not been fully elucidated yet; however, this phenomenon could be due to the enhanced deacetylation of essential autophagy-related proteins such as ATG5 and ATG7 [10].
158.Rationale: While the passage does state that spermidine inhibits histone acetyl transferase and upregulates ATG genes in general, it also explicitly states that spermidine can induce autophagy in a transcription-independent manner. The passage does not provide evidence that this mechanism applies specifically to T. cruzi. The claim about histone acetyl transferase inhibition in T. cruzi is not supported by the evidence in the passage, which focuses on other mechanisms like PA availability and ODC activity in this parasite.

158.Option: The increased infection rate of Vero cells by T. cruzi parasites subjected to autophagy induction is primarily due to enhanced parasite motility rather than increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation.
Evidence: The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions, as previously described [22].
Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.
The presence of Wort at either the first (Wort-F) or the whole (Wort-T) period of differentiation in TAU significantly reduced the infection rate.
158.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the degree of Vero cell infection was used as an indirect measure of the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes (MT) present. The data show that autophagy induction increases both MT formation and infection rates, while autophagy inhibition reduces both. There is no evidence in the passage to suggest that the increased infection rate is due to enhanced motility rather than increased MT formation. The inference about motility is not supported by the evidence presented.

158.Option: The presence of wortmannin during the first or total period of differentiation in TAU medium significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes, indicating that autophagy inhibition leads to a complete block in parasite differentiation.
Evidence: Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
158.Rationale: The passage states that wortmannin significantly reduced the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes, but it does not claim that this represents a 'complete block' in differentiation. The evidence shows a reduction, not a complete inhibition. The option misrepresents the degree of effect described in the passage.

158.Option: Bafilomycin abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi due to its inhibition of the acidocalcisome acidification process, which is essential for PI3P synthesis and autophagosome biogenesis.
Evidence: Bafilomycin (Baf), which is another important autophagy inhibitor, is mainly used to study the autophagic flux [28]. The treatment of mammalian cells with Baf blocks the normal autolysosomal degradation, leading to accumulation of autophagic compartments at different maturation stages. Unexpectedly, a significant reduction in the autophagosome number was observed when parasites were incubated in TAU in the presence of Baf, as compared to TAU medium alone (Fig 4A and 4B).

The number of autophagosomes was lower in parasites subjected to Baf in control medium than in non-treated parasites, indicating that Baf inhibits both basal and induced autophagy. Unlike the effect observed in mammalian cells, Baf abrogated autophagosome formation in T. cruzi, resulting in a complete inhibition of the autophagic response.
158.Rationale: The passage states that bafilomycin abrogates autophagosome formation in T. cruzi, but it does not provide evidence that this is due to inhibition of acidocalcisome acidification or PI3P synthesis. The mechanism of action of bafilomycin in T. cruzi is not explained in the passage, making this inference unsupported by the evidence presented.

159.Option: The induction of both IFN-γ and IL-10 production in vaccinated mice suggests that the immune response is balanced and may be sufficient for long-term protection against visceral leishmaniasis.
Evidence: The above experiments demonstrate that immunization with recombinant L. tarentolae expressing A2-CPA-CPB-CTE confers a significant protection against L. infantum infection in mice.

Given that production of IFN-γ is considered an important requirement for protection against L. infantum and that the presence of a small amount of IL-10 could promote the induction of type-1 immunity
The production of IFN-γ in response to rA2-rCPA-rCPB antigens reached the highest level (524±16.4 pg/ml) at 4 weeks after challenge in G2 (Live/Live).
The production of IL-10 upon antigen stimulation at 4-week and especially at 8 weeks after challenge was lower in G1 and G2 vaccinated groups in comparison to the control groups (G3 to G5).
We further calculated the IFN-γ to IL-10 ratio for each vaccinated group as a clear indicator of successful immunization
159.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that vaccinated mice (G1 and G2) showed increased IFN-γ production and lower IL-10 levels compared to controls, resulting in a higher IFN-γ/IL-10 ratio. This balanced immune response is associated with significant protection against L. infantum infection. The text explicitly states that this ratio is a clear indicator of successful immunization, supporting the inference that this balanced response may be sufficient for long-term protection.

159.Option: The Live/Live prime-boost regimen using recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE provides superior protection against visceral leishmaniasis compared to the DNA/Live regimen and to control groups, as evidenced by significantly lower parasite burden in the spleen and improved preservation of splenic architecture.
Evidence: Immunization using a DNA A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G1) and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE (G2) prime-boosting regiments drastically (p<0.01) reduced the infection levels in both the liver and the spleen at 4 weeks after L. infantum challenge in contrast to the control groups (G3 to G5, n = 4 at each point/organ).
The highest parasite burden in control groups (G3, G4 and G5) was observed at 12 weeks after challenge and the organ remained chronically infected.

Only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2, which was immunized with Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE vaccine regimen, were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls (G1 with G4 and G2 with G3 and G5).
The spleen AUC of DNA/Live (G1) and Live/Live (G2) parasites indicate significant differences with the controls G4 and G5.
In spleen, splenic cords were normal and somewhat thickened in vaccinated groups but were thin and in some cases were disappeared in other groups.
Normal follicle formation of the spleen was noted in G1 and G2 in 4th and 8th week but the normal follicles were disappeared in the other groups (G3, G4 and G5).
We could not find parasites in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5).
159.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the superiority of the Live/Live regimen (G2) over the DNA/Live regimen (G1) and control groups. The Live/Live group showed significantly lower parasite burden in the spleen, preserved splenic architecture, and absence of parasites in the spleen, while control groups showed chronic infection and architectural disruption. These findings directly support the inference that the Live/Live regimen provides superior protection.

159.Option: The use of cationic solid-lipid nanoparticles (cSLN) as a delivery system for the DNA vaccine enhances antigen expression and is critical for the observed protective immunity in the DNA/Live regimen.
Evidence: cSLN as an effective delivery system has exhibited considerable low cytotoxicity, and it was able to protect pDNA in a DNase I challenge assay

The A2-CPA-CPB-CTE fragment was subcloned into the EcoRI and BamHI sites of vector pcDNA3.1(−) (Invitrogen, Germany) to generate pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE as a DNA vaccine. pcDNA-A2-CPA-CPB-CTE plasmid was purified by ion exchange chromatography with Endofree Mega kit (QIAGEN, Germany).
159.Rationale: While the passage mentions that cSLN is an effective delivery system with low cytotoxicity and DNA protection capabilities, it does not provide evidence that cSLN is critical for the observed protective immunity in the DNA/Live regimen. The study does not compare the DNA/Live regimen with and without cSLN, nor does it attribute the protective effect specifically to the cSLN delivery system. The inference that cSLN is critical for the observed immunity requires assumptions not supported by the passage.

159.Option: The persistence of a small number of live parasites is essential for maintaining durable immunity, and this is achieved by the recombinant L. tarentolae vaccine through controlled infection without disease progression.
Evidence: It has been reported that persistence of a small number of live parasites is essential for maintaining durable immunity
The only way to meet this requirement is by using attenuated live vaccines.
The use of L. tarentolae as a vaccine vector to deliver specific Leishmania antigens mimicking live infection has also been explored.
159.Rationale: The passage states that persistence of live parasites is essential for durable immunity and that attenuated live vaccines are the only way to meet this requirement. However, it does not provide evidence that the recombinant L. tarentolae vaccine achieves this through controlled infection without disease progression. The study does not explicitly state that live parasites persist in vaccinated mice or that this persistence is responsible for the observed immunity. The inference requires assumptions not directly supported by the passage.

159.Option: The combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE antigens is more protective than individual forms, indicating that the C-terminal extension enhances immunogenicity and is essential for vaccine efficacy.
Evidence: We have demonstrated that the combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE is more protective against visceral and cutaneous leishmanial infections than the individual forms
Immunization with CTE also displayed both type 1 and 2 immune signatures in experimental murine model of L. infantum infection and therefore is not protective as a vaccine candidate
159.Rationale: The passage states that the combination of CPA/CPB and CPA/CPB-CTE is more protective than individual forms, but it does not provide evidence that the C-terminal extension (CTE) enhances immunogenicity or is essential for vaccine efficacy. In fact, the passage explicitly states that immunization with CTE alone is not protective. The inference that CTE enhances immunogenicity and is essential for efficacy is not supported by the evidence provided.

160.Option: The fact that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were more attracted to infected dogs in the field suggests that these sand fly species have evolved a specific preference for L. infantum-infected hosts as part of their natural feeding behavior.
Evidence: The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7). Overall, there was a significant (ANOVA: F = 35.8120, DF = 2, P <0.001) difference in the numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the different cages. Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).

The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9). Overall, the difference between numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the cages with the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage was statistically significant (ANOVA: F = 94.715, DF = 2, P <0.001). Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).
160.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were more attracted to infected dogs in the field. However, the option's claim that this represents an 'evolved specific preference' as part of their 'natural feeding behavior' is not supported by the passage. The passage discusses the hypothesis that L. infantum may manipulate host odors to increase attractiveness to vectors, not that the sand flies have evolved a specific preference for infected hosts. The observed behavior is presented as evidence of parasite manipulation rather than an evolved trait of the sand flies.

160.Option: The significant attraction of both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi to infected dogs in the field suggests that the presence of infected dogs leads to a dilution of parasite transmission by increasing vector exposure to non-competent hosts.
Evidence: The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7). Overall, there was a significant (ANOVA: F = 35.8120, DF = 2, P <0.001) difference in the numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the different cages. Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).

The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9). Overall, the difference between numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the cages with the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage was statistically significant (ANOVA: F = 94.715, DF = 2, P <0.001). Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).
160.Rationale: This option misinterprets the data. The passage shows that infected dogs attract more sand flies than uninfected dogs, which would increase rather than dilute parasite transmission. The 'dilution effect' described in this option is the opposite of what the data shows. The evidence clearly indicates that infected dogs are more attractive to vectors, which would concentrate rather than dilute transmission.

160.Option: The higher number of male P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that male sand flies are more likely to transmit L. infantum to humans due to increased contact with infected reservoirs.
Evidence: The number of females trapped was significantly higher (30.07 ± 6.43) than the number of males (16.00 ± 4.02) (MCP: z = 2.933, P = 0.00335) (Fig 8). The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (34.11 ± 8.18) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393). Similarly, the number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (29.11 ± 7.31) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (11.78 ± 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393). The number of male P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (34.11±8.18) was significantly greater than the mean number of males trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (11.78 + 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034) (Fig 8). Similarly, the mean number of females P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 + 11.82) was significantly higher than the mean number of females trapped in cages baited with the uninfected dog (29.11 + 7.31) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).
160.Rationale: This option contains multiple errors. First, the passage states that female sand flies are significantly more numerous than males (30.07 ± 6.43 vs 16.00 ± 4.02). Second, the passage does not suggest that male sand flies transmit L. infantum to humans. In fact, only female sand flies feed on blood and are capable of transmitting the parasite. The option misrepresents both the data and the biological understanding of sand fly vector biology.

160.Option: The significantly higher number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog compared to the uninfected dog indicates that female sand flies are more likely to feed on infected dogs due to increased blood meal size or nutritional quality.
Evidence: The number of females trapped was significantly higher (30.07 ± 6.43) than the number of males (16.00 ± 4.02) (MCP: z = 2.933, P = 0.00335) (Fig 8). The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (34.11 ± 8.18) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393). Similarly, the number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (29.11 ± 7.31) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (11.78 ± 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393). The number of male P. perniciosus trapped in cages baited with the infected dog (34.11±8.18) was significantly greater than the mean number of males trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (11.78 + 4.70) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034) (Fig 8). Similarly, the mean number of females P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 + 11.82) was significantly higher than the mean number of females trapped in cages baited with the uninfected dog (29.11 + 7.31) (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).
160.Rationale: The passage shows that female P. perniciosus were more attracted to infected dogs, but it does not provide evidence that this is due to 'increased blood meal size or nutritional quality.' The study's hypothesis is that L. infantum may manipulate host odors to increase attractiveness to vectors, not that infected hosts provide better nutrition. The option introduces a causal explanation not supported by the passage.

160.Option: The enhanced attractiveness of infected dogs to both female and male Phlebotomus perniciosus and Phlebotomus perfiliewi under natural conditions suggests a zoopotentiation effect that increases the transmission success of Leishmania infantum.
Evidence: The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7). Overall, there was a significant (ANOVA: F = 35.8120, DF = 2, P <0.001) difference in the numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the different cages. Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).

The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9). Overall, the difference between numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the cages with the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage was statistically significant (ANOVA: F = 94.715, DF = 2, P <0.001). Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

We hypothesized that physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
160.Rationale: This option correctly identifies the zoopotentiation effect, which is the phenomenon where infected hosts become more attractive to vectors, thereby increasing transmission success. The passage provides direct evidence that infected dogs attract more P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi in the field. The hypothesis section explicitly states that this increased attractiveness enhances the parasite's transmission success. The term 'zoopotentiation' is a well-established concept in parasitology that accurately describes this phenomenon.

161.Option: The use of reagent strips alone for diagnosing urinary schistosomiasis could reduce survey costs by 2%, but this approach would result in considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment in Southern Sudan due to low specificity, making it unsuitable for implementation.
Evidence: Testing for urinary schistosomiasis infection could, for example, be conducted only with Hemastix reagent sticks (Bayer Diagnostics, Basingstoke, UK), instead of the current procedure whereby all samples with blood in urine and a sub-sample of negatives were cross checked using urine filtration. By cutting out the cost of these and other supplies for urine filtration, the cost per implementation unit surveyed would have decreased by 2% (Table 3). This small reduction in economic cost is explained by the fact that urine filtration can be easily conducted alongside other operations; personnel requirements (the major cost driver) and survey duration would thus remain unchanged.
161.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that using reagent strips alone would reduce costs by 2% and that this approach would not significantly affect personnel requirements or survey duration. There is no mention of 'considerable misclassification' or 'low specificity' as a concern. The option incorrectly introduces a negative consequence (misclassification) that is not supported by the passage. The passage actually suggests that this method may be reliable in some African countries and could be applicable in Southern Sudan after validation.

161.Option: Survey costs in Southern Sudan are likely to be higher than in other countries due to the need for international staff and post-conflict reconstruction challenges, which increase both personnel and operational expenses.
Evidence: It is common practice to include overheads in cost analyses [32]. To capture the indirect costs of project management and administration we applied an overhead of 25% to the financial cost estimate for all budget lines. This rate is higher than those applicable to operations in more stable settings of eastern Africa [e.g. [33], but in our experience provides an accurate reflection of the cost involved in implementing programmes in a land-locked country undergoing post-conflict reconstruction.

Due to severe human resource constraints affecting Southern Sudan, only two national laboratory technicians per team could be recruited, with additional technicians recruited as short-term consultants from the Vector Control Division, MoH, Uganda.
161.Rationale: The passage directly supports this conclusion. It explicitly states that Southern Sudan's survey costs include a 25% overhead rate, which is higher than in more stable settings, and attributes this to the challenges of post-conflict reconstruction. It also mentions the need to recruit international staff (technicians from Uganda) due to human resource constraints, which would increase costs. These factors together justify the inference that survey costs in Southern Sudan are likely to be higher than in other countries.

161.Option: Capital costs accounted for only 4.3% of total survey costs, indicating that infrastructure investment is not a major concern in integrated NTD surveys, and therefore should be deprioritized in future planning.
Evidence: Capital costs amounted to only 4.3% of the total survey costs and were largely comprised of vehicle costs.
161.Rationale: While the passage confirms that capital costs were only 4.3% of total survey costs, it does not suggest that infrastructure investment should be deprioritized in future planning. The low proportion of capital costs does not imply they are unimportant or should be ignored. The option makes an unsupported recommendation that is not present in the passage. The passage simply reports the cost distribution without making recommendations about prioritization.

161.Option: The major cost drivers of an integrated NTD survey in Southern Sudan were personnel and consumables, and this observation is likely applicable to most other settings, warranting detailed budgeting for these inputs during planning.
Evidence: The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2). Economic costs amounted to a total of USD 201,030 and were arrived at by including the imputed value of the time provided by MoH employees and other non-cash inputs, as well as the opportunity cost of capital items used in the survey. As for the financial costs, most economic costs were taken up by survey consumables and personnel, accounting for 27% and 38% of the total, respectively (Table 2).
161.Rationale: The passage clearly identifies personnel and consumables as the major cost drivers, accounting for over 60% of total costs (34.4% + 30.4% for financial costs, and 38% + 27% for economic costs). The option correctly notes that these are the primary cost drivers and suggests they should be prioritized in budgeting. While the passage does not explicitly state that this pattern is 'likely applicable to most other settings,' the general nature of these cost categories (personnel and consumables) as major expenses in health surveys makes this a reasonable inference that is supported by the evidence.

161.Option: The cost of survey consumables is likely to be similar in other African countries, as most of them are not produced in the region, while personnel costs might differ considerably between settings, particularly when comparing Southern Sudan to countries that have not been affected by decades of conflict.
Evidence: The total financial costs for the survey amounted to USD 182,067, the majority of which was spent on recurrent items. The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).
161.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence about the production location of survey consumables or their cost similarity across African countries. It also does not make any direct comparison between Southern Sudan and other countries regarding personnel costs. The option introduces assumptions about production and cost differences that are not supported by the passage. The passage only states the proportion of costs attributed to consumables and personnel but does not provide the basis for the claims about regional production or conflict-related cost differences.

162.Option: The seasonal variation in HFRS incidence is primarily driven by changes in air temperature, with outbreaks occurring during periods of high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September.

171.Option: Although the intracranial inoculation model induces consistent, lethal neurological disease, the lesions observed are not consistent with those seen in NiV-infected patients or in animals infected via natural routes, suggesting limited utility for studying NiV neuropathogenesis despite its potential for testing antiviral efficacy in the CNS.

Large numbers of neutrophils were observed within inflamed and necrotic vessels and the surrounding parenchyma (Fig 5A). Leptomeninges were segmentally thickened by fibrin and infiltrating inflammatory cells (Fig 5D). Multifocal to regionally extensive virus antigen and viral RNA positivity was detected throughout the leptomeninges on IHC and ISH, respectively (Fig 5D and 5G). In the cerebellum, virus antigen and RNA could often be observed tracking from the leptomeninges, across the molecular layer to positive cells within the granular layer (Fig 5F and 5I). Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K). In contrast, viral antigen was primarily observed within the parenchyma and less often observed within the leptomeninges and ependymal cells of animals inoculated via the intraperitoneal and intranasal route (Fig 6D). 171.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the histopathological changes observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters are in stark contrast with those observed in hamsters inoculated via natural routes (intraperitoneal or intranasal) and that these natural route lesions are similar to those described in humans and other animal models. Typhi clades in Santiago despite the interruption of hyperendemic transmission is also supported by the genomic evidence showing that the same genotypes have been present across both study periods. 174.Option: The detection of a novel variant of plasmid pHCM2 in two genotype 1.2.1 isolates from Santiago suggests that this plasmid may play a role in the persistence of S. Typhi in the human host.
Evidence: The other ancient clade present amongst the Santiago isolates was genotype 1.2.1. This was the only clade in our datasets (except from one recently imported H58 isolate) in which isolates harboured pHCM2 plasmids.
pHCM2 plasmids are found in ~15% of S. Typhi and are generally highly conserved (Fig 4A).

To our knowledge, based on the data available in the S. Typhi public collection on Pathogenwatch: (https://pathogen.watch/collection/1y3cdsjq55hf-public-genomes- accessed Dec 2021), pHCM2 plasmids have not been detected in ancient clades 0.0 to 1.2 previously.

Two pHCM2 plasmids were found among our isolates from genotype 1.2.1; one in a 1984 isolate (ERR2710544) and the other from 2013 (ERR4289185). These plasmids were highly similar to each other, but significantly different from the pHCM2 found on S. Typhi reference CT18 (accession no: AL513384), and from another novel pHCM2 found in a 1973 isolate from Thailand [37]. The pHCM2 sequences described here differed by >2000 SNPs (98.4% nucleotide identity, 89% coverage) from the reference (Fig 4).
174.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of the detection of a novel variant of plasmid pHCM2 in two genotype 1.2.1 isolates from Santiago, but it does not provide evidence that this plasmid plays a role in the persistence of S. Typhi in the human host. The passage only states that pHCM2 plasmids are found in ~15% of S. Typhi and are generally highly conserved. The detection of a novel variant of pHCM2 in Santiago isolates is interesting, but it does not provide evidence of a causal relationship between the plasmid and the persistence of S. Typhi in the human host. The passage does not mention any specific functions of the pHCM2 plasmid that would suggest a role in persistence.

174.Option: The global dominance of the H58 S. Typhi clade in other industrialized nations suggests that imported cases are the primary source of sporadic typhoid in Santiago, Chile.
Evidence: The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.

The genomic population structure of S. Typhi in Santiago appeared highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras (Fig 1). Both rare “ancient” genotypes 1.1 and 1.2.1, as well as more commonly found genotypes 2 and 3.5, were frequently present across both study periods.
The other genotypes present in Santiago were mostly found on long branches, indicating significant differentiation from their closest relatives in the global context phylogeny.
174.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that imported cases are one of the sources of sporadic typhoid in Santiago, but it does not suggest that imported cases are the primary source. The passage explicitly states that transmission from Chilean chronic carriers is also a source of sporadic cases. The genomic evidence also shows that the S. Typhi population in Santiago has remained highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras, with the same genotypes present across both periods. This suggests that the S. Typhi population in Santiago is not primarily derived from imported cases, but rather from a local source, such as chronic carriers.

174.Option: The low genetic diversity observed in the 2011–2012 genotype 1.1 cluster indicates that the S. Typhi population in Santiago has undergone a recent bottleneck event, leading to the loss of most ancestral genotypes.
Evidence: The genomic population structure of S. Typhi in Santiago appeared highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras (Fig 1). Both rare “ancient” genotypes 1.1 and 1.2.1, as well as more commonly found genotypes 2 and 3.5, were frequently present across both study periods.

The twenty isolates from 2011‒2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
The smallest SNP distance between a 1980s 1.1 isolate and a 2010s 1.1 isolate was 79 SNPs.
174.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the S. Typhi population in Santiago has not undergone a recent bottleneck event. The genomic population structure has remained highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras, with the same genotypes present across both periods. The low genetic diversity within the 2011–2012 genotype 1.1 cluster is explained by the fact that these isolates have a recent common ancestor, not by a bottleneck event that would have led to the loss of most ancestral genotypes. The passage also states that the smallest SNP distance between a 1980s 1.1 isolate and a 2010s 1.1 isolate was 79 SNPs, which is much greater than the SNP distance within the 2011–2012 cluster (8 SNPs), suggesting that the 2010s cluster is more recent and not the result of a bottleneck event.

175.Option: Ergot alkaloids are efficacious modulators of planarian regeneration and motility, and their structural-activity relationships suggest potential for developing novel chemotherapeutics targeting parasite muscle function.
Evidence: Ergot alkaloids are a historically important class of compounds that realize their effects because of the close structural similarity of the ergoline scaffold to bioaminergic transmitters.

Numerous ergot compounds yielded regenerative phenotypes, either phenocopying PZQ to promote bipolar (‘two-head’) regeneration or inhibiting head regeneration (‘no-head’, Fig 3A), all at doses lower than PZQ.

This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects: for example, all ergots that caused bipolarity were either alkylated on the indole nitrogen or halogenated at the adjacent 2-position (Fig 3A).

In schistosome contractility assays, the compounds that caused planarian bipolarity all inhibited schistosomule motility (just like PZQ), whereas the compounds that inhibited planarian head regeneration caused the opposite effect, stimulating contractile activity (Fig 3B).
Therefore, ergot alkaloids possess efficacy against schistosomules, with an action predictable by planarian polarity outcomes.
175.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that ergot alkaloids modulate planarian regeneration and motility, with structural-activity relationships identified. It also shows that these compounds inhibit schistosomule motility in a manner similar to PZQ. The conclusion that ergot alkaloids have potential for developing novel chemotherapeutics targeting parasite muscle function is directly supported by these findings.

175.Option: The structural-activity relationships identified in planarian regenerative screens are sufficient to guarantee the development of parasite-specific drugs with minimal off-target effects in humans.
Evidence: This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects: for example, all ergots that caused bipolarity were either alkylated on the indole nitrogen or halogenated at the adjacent 2-position (Fig 3A).
175.Rationale: While the passage describes structural-activity relationships in planarian screens, it does not provide evidence that these relationships guarantee parasite-specific drugs with minimal off-target effects in humans. The passage does not address human pharmacology or specificity, making this inference unsupported.

175.Option: Since PZQ’s mechanism of action is unresolved, the discovery that ergot alkaloids mimic its effects in planarians suggests that PZQ’s primary target is the serotonergic system in flatworms.
Evidence: The bipolarizing action of PZQ is reversed by 5-HT
RNAi of 5-HT receptors potentiates PZQ action
Serotonergic ligands modulate planarian regenerative polarity
The functional antagonism between serotonergic signals and PZQ action (Figs 1 and 4C).
175.Rationale: The passage shows that PZQ's effects are functionally antagonized by serotonergic signals, but it does not definitively establish that the serotonergic system is PZQ's primary target. The passage states that PZQ's mechanism of action remains unresolved, and while the evidence is suggestive, it does not prove that the serotonergic system is the primary target.

175.Option: The functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action suggests that PZQ may directly target flatworm bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic, which could be confirmed through heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs.

The structural studies have shown that the indole N1 hydrogen forms a key hydrogen bond with a conserved threonine residue T3.37 [27] within the orthosteric binding pocket of 5-HT GpCRs that is likely important for receptor activation [27,29].

175.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action, and describes structural features suggesting PZQ may act as an ergomimetic. 176.Option: The decision tree algorithm for prognostication achieved high positive predictive value in low-prevalence settings, making it ideal for identifying severe dengue cases in non-outbreak periods.

The claim about 'ideal for identifying severe dengue cases in non-outbreak periods' cannot be derived from the passage.

176.Option: The decision tree model for dengue diagnosis is more effective than traditional laboratory tests such as RT-PCR and ELISA in early detection of dengue infection.

The passage does not claim the decision tree is 'more effective' than these tests, and the sensitivity of 71.2% suggests it is not as accurate as the gold standard laboratory tests.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach can differentiate dengue from non-dengue febrile illness and predict disease outcome using simple haematological and virological parameters obtained within the first 72 hours of illness.
Evidence: We show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

The resulting diagnostic algorithm is shown in Figure 1. The first splitting parameter is a platelet count of 196,000/mm3 blood or less followed by the total white cell or lymphocyte counts, body temperature, haematocrit or neutrophil count and another platelet count at presentation.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3).
176.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the decision tree approach can distinguish dengue from non-dengue febrile illness and predict disease outcomes using haematological and virological parameters within the first 72 hours of illness. The evidence includes the description of the diagnostic algorithm and the prognostic algorithm, both of which use simple parameters like platelet count, white blood cell count, and RT-PCR results. This directly supports the option as a valid inference.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach allows for the integration of dynamic laboratory parameters into a single predictive model, enabling real-time clinical decision-making during dengue outbreaks.
Evidence: We show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

177.Option: Surveillance methods with greater sensitivity and more targeted sampling strategies to better detect residual hotspots than the current TAS methodology will be required.

177.Option: Because mosquito surveillance provides a snapshot of current infection and is less affected by the persistence of markers in the host, it should replace human-based surveillance in all post-validation settings.
Evidence: Mosquito surveillance studies
The majority of studies then described various methods for taking a random sample of households from which to sample mosquitoes, either indoors or outdoors.

The most common mosquito sampling method was the gravid trap (n = 9; 39.1%) followed by various baited traps (n = 6; 26.1%), human landing collection (n = 5; 21.7%) and pyrethrum space spray catches (n = 4; 17.4%).
177.Rationale: While the passage discusses mosquito surveillance methods and their potential utility, it does not provide evidence to support the claim that mosquito surveillance should replace human-based surveillance in all post-validation settings. The evidence does not support a blanket replacement of human-based surveillance with mosquito surveillance.

Compared to Binax Now or Alere ICT (the most commonly used tests at the time of most of these surveys) as the index test, Table 3 shows that antibody tests produce a higher proportion of positive results. Bm14Ab and Wb123Ab values are, on average, 5.1 and 6.7 times higher respectively than the corresponding BinaxNOW values, based on the median value of this ratio across the selected studies.

178.Option: The current WHO monitoring protocol for LF elimination is sufficient to support the detection of system-level changes in parasite dynamics, as it aligns with the time points at which significant reductions in model uncertainty occur.
Evidence: The four data streams considered for investigating the value of information gained from each survey were respectively: scenario 1—baseline mf prevalence data only, scenario 2—baseline and post-MDA 3 mf prevalence data, scenario 3—baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data, and scenario 4—baseline and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data. In addition to these four data streams, a fifth model-only scenario (scenario 0) was also considered where no site-specific data was introduced.

The results also show, however, that data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).

The best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).

The results of pairwise F-tests for variance, performed to compare the weighted variance in timelines to achieve 1% mf prevalence between model-only simulations (scenario 0) and the lowest entropy simulations (best scenario) (see Table 2), show that the predictions for the best scenarios are significantly different from the predictions for the model-only simulations.

The results indicate unequivocally that models constrained by data provided significantly more precise intervention predictions compared to model-only predictions (Table 2). Note that this was also irrespective of the two types of longitudinal data assimilation procedures (sequential vs. simultaneous) used by the different models in this study.
178.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the WHO monitoring protocol includes data collection at baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 time points. The evidence shows that data streams including post-MDA 5 (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty the most compared to shorter data streams (scenarios 1 and 2). This directly supports the inference that the WHO protocol aligns with the time points where significant reductions in model uncertainty occur. The passage also states that models constrained by these data streams provided significantly more precise predictions, confirming the protocol's sufficiency for detecting system-level changes.

The best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).

The results also show, however, that data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).

The best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).
178.Rationale: The passage directly contradicts this option by showing that additional longitudinal data (especially post-MDA 5) significantly reduced model uncertainty compared to model-only predictions and shorter data streams. The evidence demonstrates that while baseline data is important, additional longitudinal data (particularly later time points) have a substantial impact on reducing prediction uncertainty, not minimal.

178.Option: The use of longitudinal data from at least five years post-MDA is sufficient to constrain model predictions of the impact of LF interventions and detect qualitative changes in system behavior, with additional data beyond this point not leading to significant further improvements in predictive performance.
Evidence: The results also show, however, that data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).

The best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).

EPIFIL was also used to expand the number of calibration scenarios using a dataset with longer term post-MDA data from Villupuram district, India. This dataset contained two addition data streams: scenario 5 which included baseline, post-MDA 3, post-MDA 5, and post-MDA 7 mf data, and scenario 6, which included baseline, post-MDA 3, post-MDA 5, post-MDA 7, and post-MDA 9 mf data. Scenario 6 thus contained the most post-MDA data and was demonstrated to be the most effective for reducing model uncertainty, but this effect was not statistically significantly different from the reductions produced by assimilating data contained in scenarios 3 and 5 (Table 4). The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.

The best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).

EPIFIL was also used to expand the number of calibration scenarios using a dataset with longer term post-MDA data from Villupuram district, India. This dataset contained two addition data streams: scenario 5 which included baseline, post-MDA 3, post-MDA 5, and post-MDA 7 mf data, and scenario 6, which included baseline, post-MDA 3, post-MDA 5, post-MDA 7, and post-MDA 9 mf data. Scenario 6 thus contained the most post-MDA data and was demonstrated to be the most effective for reducing model uncertainty, but this effect was not statistically significantly different from the reductions produced by assimilating data contained in scenarios 3 and 5 (Table 4). The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
