Evidence: In five of six animals the overall population of CD4+ T cells increased over the course of the disease while in the survivor (8197), the CD4+ T cell population decreased, even when evaluated over only the acute phase of disease (Fig 5A–5C, S1 Table).
The population of CD8bright effector T cells increased markedly during convalescence of the survivor as one might expect for clearance of residual infected cells.
197.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the survivor had a decrease in CD4+ T cell populations during the acute phase, which is the opposite of what the option claims. The protective immune response in the survivor appears to be more related to CD8+ T cell responses rather than CD4+ T cells. This option is directly contradicted by the evidence presented in the study.

198.Option: The MP-12 vaccine candidate is safer than scMP-12 because it does not replicate in the central nervous system of immunocompromised hosts.
Evidence: However, intraperitoneal (i.p.) inoculation of young mice with MP-12 can result in efficient virus replication in the central nervous system (CNS) (J. Morrill et al, unpublished data).

Furthermore, i.p. inoculation of SCID mice with MP-12 results in the development of neurological signs and death of all mice [29]. These data suggest that MP-12 can invade the CNS and undergo efficient replication in immunocompromised animals, and may potentially do so in immunocompromised humans as well.
198.Rationale: The passage clearly states that MP-12 can replicate in the CNS of immunocompromised animals (young mice and SCID mice), which directly contradicts the claim that MP-12 is safer than scMP-12 in this regard. The option incorrectly reverses the safety profile of the two vaccine candidates.

198.Option: The scMP-12 vaccine candidate is more immunogenic than MP-12 because it elicits higher neutralizing antibody titers in mice following a single immunization.
Evidence: The mean PRNT80 titers of the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 and 105 PFU of VRP were 1∶238 and 1∶38, respectively; the difference in the PRNT80 titers was statistically significant. Thus, the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 elicited neutralizing antibody titers that were statistically higher than those immunized with 105 PFU of VRP and were comparable to those immunized with 104 PFU of MP-12.
198.Rationale: The data show that scMP-12 elicits neutralizing antibody titers comparable to 104 PFU of MP-12, not higher than MP-12. The option incorrectly claims scMP-12 is more immunogenic than MP-12, which is not supported by the evidence. The passage states that 105 PFU of MP-12 produced significantly higher titers than scMP-12.

198.Option: The scMP-12 vaccine candidate is superior to MP-12 in safety due to its lack of neurovirulence, despite being less immunogenic.
Evidence: All MP-12 infected mice died by 3 days p.i., whereas all mice inoculated with scMP-12 or HBSS survived and did not show any clinical signs of disease (Fig. 6), demonstrating the absence of detectable levels of neurovirulence in scMP-12.

The mean PRNT80 titers of the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 and 105 PFU of VRP were 1∶238 and 1∶38, respectively; the difference in the PRNT80 titers was statistically significant. Thus, the mice immunized with 105 PFU of scMP-12 elicited neutralizing antibody titers that were statistically higher than those immunized with 105 PFU of VRP and were comparable to those immunized with 104 PFU of MP-12.
198.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that scMP-12 is non-neurovirulent in neonatal mice, while MP-12 is highly neurovirulent. It also shows that scMP-12 is less immunogenic than MP-12 at the same dose (105 PFU), but comparable to a lower dose of MP-12 (104 PFU). This supports the inference that scMP-12 is safer despite being less immunogenic at the same dose.

198.Option: The scMP-12 vaccine candidate is less effective than MP-12 in preventing viremia and viral replication in target organs after wild-type challenge, as demonstrated by higher viral titers in immunized mice.
Evidence: scMP-12 immunization also prevented viremia and virus replication in the liver and spleen, while two mice, one having no detectable PRNT80 titer and the other having a PRNT80 titer of 1∶20, showed virus replication in the brain at day 9 p.i.
In contrast, mice immunized with VRP or MP-12 showed neither viremia nor virus replication in the liver, spleen or brain.
198.Rationale: The passage states that scMP-12 prevented viremia and replication in the liver and spleen, which are key target organs. The only exception was the brain in two mice with very low or no neutralizing antibody titers. This does not support the claim that scMP-12 is less effective overall. The option misrepresents the data by focusing on a minor exception while ignoring the overall effectiveness.

198.Option: The neurovirulence of MP-12 in neonatal mice indicates that scMP-12 may also be neurovirulent in immunocompromised humans, despite its safety in mice.
Evidence: All MP-12 infected mice died by 3 days p.i., whereas all mice inoculated with scMP-12 or HBSS survived and did not show any clinical signs of disease (Fig. 6), demonstrating the absence of detectable levels of neurovirulence in scMP-12.
However, neuroinvasiveness and neurovirulence is of concern when considering RVFV immunization of the general public, given the diversity of ages, health statuses and genetic backgrounds.
198.Rationale: The passage directly contradicts this option by showing that scMP-12 is non-neurovirulent in neonatal mice, while MP-12 is highly neurovirulent. The option incorrectly infers that scMP-12 would be neurovirulent in humans based on MP-12's properties, which is not supported by the evidence provided.

199.Option: The seasonal patterns of Shigella in Tanzania mirror the seasonality of malnutrition, previously described, suggesting that Shigella infections may be a direct cause of malnutrition in this population.
Evidence: The seasonality of Shigella infections differed by site (Fig 2). Peak prevalence was observed in May/June in Bangladesh, June in Nepal, July/August in India, November in South Africa, and February in Tanzania.

The majority of Shigella burden was associated with watery diarrhea, not dysentery [5]. A recent meta-analysis showed that the proportion of Shigella infections that present with dysentery has been decreasing, and that Shigella infections overall had a stronger association with mortality than Shigella-associated cases of dysentery [7]. Furthermore, even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, Shigella has been associated with impaired linear growth [6,8,9].
199.Rationale: The passage states that Shigella has a seasonal pattern in Tanzania (February peak) but does not mention any specific seasonality of malnutrition in Tanzania. While the passage does mention an association between Shigella and impaired linear growth, it does not establish a causal relationship between Shigella seasonality and malnutrition seasonality. The inference that Shigella infections are a direct cause of malnutrition based on seasonal patterns is not supported by the evidence provided.

199.Option: Because Shigella culture has low sensitivity, especially in young children, and because recent antibiotic use was not associated with reduced detection, the use of culture-based diagnostics should be abandoned in favor of molecular methods in all low-resource settings.
Evidence: Of 30,678 stools tested by both qPCR and culture, 3,372 (11.0%) were positive by qPCR and 280 (0.9%) were positive by culture. Considering qPCR the gold standard, the overall sensitivity of culture was 6.6% (Table 3). Specificity was uniformly high, at more than 99% at all sites.

Culture positivity was strongly associated with age, such that attributable diarrhea stools among younger children were less likely to be culture positive (Table 4). Presence of blood had the strongest association with culture positivity (aRR: 1.84, 95% CI: 1.28, 2.65), but culture still missed more than 70% of dysentery cases. Diarrhea severity was not associated with culture positivity, and caregiver-reported fever was inversely associated with culture positivity. Recent macrolide treatment was also associated with reduced detection by culture (aRR: 0.57, 95% CI: 0.30, 1.08; Table 4).
199.Rationale: The passage confirms that Shigella culture has low sensitivity and is less effective in young children. However, it does not provide evidence that recent antibiotic use was not associated with reduced detection. In fact, the passage states that recent macrolide treatment was associated with reduced detection by culture. The recommendation to abandon culture-based diagnostics in all low-resource settings is an overgeneralization not supported by the evidence, as the passage does not address cost, feasibility, or other factors that might influence diagnostic method choice in different settings.

199.Option: The presence of Shigella in non-diarrheal stools with no clinical symptoms indicates that most Shigella infections are subclinical and therefore pose no public health concern.
Evidence: The prevalence of Shigella was 11.5% (n = 4744) overall, and was higher in diarrheal stools than non-diarrheal stools (18.4% vs. 10.1%; p<0.0001). Shigella prevalence in non-diarrheal stools varied from 4.9%-17.8% across sites. Almost half (611/1407, 43.4%) of children with Shigella detected in one or more non-diarrheal stools never had a diarrhea episode in which Shigella was detected, and almost two-thirds (900/1407, 64.0%) never experienced a Shigella-attributable diarrhea episode.
Furthermore, even in the absence of diarrheal symptoms, Shigella has been associated with impaired linear growth [6,8,9].
199.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Shigella in non-diarrheal stools is associated with impaired linear growth, indicating that these subclinical infections do have public health significance. The option incorrectly concludes that subclinical infections 'pose no public health concern,' which directly contradicts the evidence presented about their association with growth impairment.

199.Option: The high burden of Shigella disease documented in MAL-ED highlights the potential utility of Shigella vaccines, as almost all children were exposed to Shigella by two years of age in most sites, suggesting a pathogen-specific population-based prevention strategy is warranted.
Evidence: By two years of age, 82.0% (n = 1407) of children had been infected with Shigella (Fig 1A), and 29.6% (n = 507) had at least one episode of Shigella-attributable diarrhea (Fig 1B).

The recent use of quantitative PCR for Shigella detection revealed a more than five times higher burden of Shigella-attributable diarrhea among children in low-resource settings than previously recognized using culture-based diagnostics [4–6].

Increasing rates of fluoroquinolone and macrolide resistance have highlighted the need for novel interventions, and particularly increased the urgency of the development of a Shigella vaccine [10], which may offer a more sustainable solution.
199.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence of the high burden of Shigella disease, with 82% of children infected by age 2 and a fivefold higher burden than previously recognized. It also explicitly states that increasing antibiotic resistance has increased the urgency for a Shigella vaccine. The inference that a pathogen-specific population-based prevention strategy (i.e., vaccination) is warranted directly follows from these data points.

199.Option: The high incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea in older children suggests that interventions should focus on improving hygiene practices in school-aged children rather than infants.
Evidence: The overall incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was 31.8 cases (95% CI: 29.6, 34.2) per 100 child-years. Incidence of Shigella-attributable diarrhea was higher in older age groups (incidence rate ratio: 1.75, 95% CI: 1.70, 1.81 per 6-month increase in age).

In univariable analysis, unimproved sanitation, crowding (2+ people living in a single room), <10 years of maternal education, having 3+ live children, initiating complementary foods before 3 months, recent diarrhea, antibiotic use (particularly fluoroquinolone use), and lower anthropometric measurements prior to sample collection were also associated with higher risk of Shigella infection (Table 5).
199.Rationale: While the passage does show higher incidence in older children, it also identifies risk factors that are relevant across all ages, including unimproved sanitation and crowding. The inference that interventions should focus only on school-aged children ignores the fact that early-life risk factors (like maternal education and sanitation) are critical for prevention. The passage does not support the conclusion that interventions should shift focus away from infants.

200.Option: The mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus is more sensitive to clk gene disruption than that of Ae. albopictus, suggesting that the molecular clock in nocturnal mosquitoes is more dependent on clk for rhythmic output.
Evidence: The mating rate of AalclkΔ293 at night (45.8%) was significantly higher than that of WT group (30.8%; GLM, χ2 = 5.65, d.f. = 1, P = 0.017), but there was no significant difference between AalclkΔ293 and WT group in daytime mating rates (GLM, χ2 = 1.61, d.f. = 1, P = 0.205) (Fig 2E).

The total mating rate of CxqclkΔ98 at night (0.0%) was significantly lower than that of WT group (13.3%; GLM, χ2 = 18.46, d.f. = 1, P < 0.0001). The total mating rate of CxqclkΔ98 during daytime (9.2%) was significantly higher than that of WT group (1.7%; χ2 = 5.23, d.f. = 1, P = 0.022) (Fig 2J).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that disruption of the clk gene in Cx. quinquefasciatus (CxqclkΔ98) caused a complete loss of nighttime mating activity (0.0% vs 13.3% in WT) and a significant increase in daytime mating (9.2% vs 1.7% in WT). In contrast, disruption of the clk gene in Ae. albopictus (AalclkΔ293) only caused a moderate increase in nighttime mating (45.8% vs 30.8% in WT) with no significant change in daytime mating. This indicates that the nocturnal mating rhythm of Cx. quinquefasciatus is more sensitive to clk gene disruption than the diurnal mating rhythm of Ae. albopictus, supporting the inference that nocturnal mosquitoes may be more dependent on clk for maintaining their rhythmic output.

200.Option: The distinct diel mating rhythms of Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus are regulated by the temporal expression of the desat1 gene, which is controlled by the core clock gene clk.
Evidence: The desat1 transcript levels in the heads and bodies of Ae. albopictus exhibited oscillations with expression peaks in both day and night (S3 Fig). The Cx. quinquefasciatus desat1 transcripts had a different oscillation trend with peak abundance at the moment of the light/dark transition (S3 Fig).

The abundance of desat1 transcripts in AalclkΔ293 males was high during light/dark transition at ZT12. The peak and trough values were out of phase compared to WT controls (Fig 3A). The desat1 transcripts profile in CxqclkΔ98 males was also out of phase and had lowest abundance during light/dark transition which was opposite to that of WT controls (Fig 3C).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that the temporal expression of desat1 is under the control of the core clock gene clk in both species. In Ae. albopictus, desat1 expression oscillates with peaks in both day and night, while in Cx. quinquefasciatus, desat1 expression peaks at the light/dark transition. Disruption of the clk gene in both species caused phase shifts in desat1 expression. This supports the inference that the distinct diel mating rhythms of these two mosquito species are regulated by the temporal expression of desat1, which is controlled by the core clock gene clk.

200.Option: The circadian clock is controlled genetically, and mutation in 'clock genes' can change rhythmic behaviour in animals, including insects, humans and other species.
Evidence: It has been confirmed in Drosophila melanogaster and mammals that these rhythms are regulated and maintained by molecular circadian clocks [5].
The clk gene knockouts suppressed the expression levels of the respective transcripts (Figs 2A and 2F and S1).

clk mutation led to a phase disruption in the diel mating rhythm under LD cycle, resulting in Ae. albopictus showing a nighttime mating peak, and Cx. quinquefasciatus showing mating during the daytime but not at night.
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that the circadian clock is genetically controlled, as demonstrated by the conserved molecular mechanisms in Drosophila and mammals. The study further demonstrates that mutation in the core clock gene clk in both Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus leads to significant changes in their mating rhythms. This supports the general inference that the circadian clock is genetically controlled and that mutations in clock genes can alter rhythmic behavior across species.

200.Option: The desat1 gene directly determines the timing of mating behavior in both mosquito species, independent of the core clock gene clk.
Evidence: The abundance of desat1 transcripts in AalclkΔ293 males was high during light/dark transition at ZT12. The peak and trough values were out of phase compared to WT controls (Fig 3A).
The desat1 transcripts profile in CxqclkΔ98 males was also out of phase and had lowest abundance during light/dark transition which was opposite to that of WT controls (Fig 3C).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that the temporal expression of desat1 is regulated by the core clock gene clk in both species. Disruption of the clk gene caused phase shifts in desat1 expression, indicating that desat1 expression is not independent of the core clock gene clk. Therefore, the claim that desat1 directly determines the timing of mating behavior independent of clk is not supported by the evidence.

200.Option: The phase difference in the temporal expression of desat1 between Ae. albopictus and Cx. quinquefasciatus is due to differences in the light sensitivity of their respective clock genes.
Evidence: The desat1 transcript levels in the heads and bodies of Ae. albopictus exhibited oscillations with expression peaks in both day and night (S3 Fig). The Cx. quinquefasciatus desat1 transcripts had a different oscillation trend with peak abundance at the moment of the light/dark transition (S3 Fig).
The abundance of desat1 transcripts in AalclkΔ293 males was high during light/dark transition at ZT12. The peak and trough values were out of phase compared to WT controls (Fig 3A).
200.Rationale: The evidence shows that the phase difference in desat1 expression between the two species is due to differences in the regulation by their respective clock genes, not differences in light sensitivity. The study demonstrates that the core clock gene clk regulates the temporal expression of desat1 in both species, and disruption of clk leads to phase shifts in desat1 expression. There is no evidence provided in the passage to support the claim that the phase difference is due to differences in light sensitivity of the clock genes.

201.Option: The increase in CFA positivity from 2 to 8 cases in TAS 2, despite passing the threshold, indicates that the WHO criteria for stopping MDA are too lenient and should be revised to prevent resurgence.
Evidence: TAS 1 was conducted in 2015 using a cluster survey of 30 primary schools, and two children were identified as antigen-positive using the rapid filariasis test strip (FTS) (Alere, Scarborough, ME), which detects circulating filarial antigenemia (CFA) for adult worms. This was below the critical cutoff of 2% for regions with Culex vectors, and MDA in the department was stopped. Nippes underwent TAS 2 in 2017 and passed again, however the number of CFA-positive children had increased to eight.

Of the 1,927 participants who completed FTS testing, 29 were CFA-positive, for a total positivity rate of 1.5% (Table 2). Of these, 21 (72%) were from Miragoane, and each index case in this commune led to the identification of CFA-positive participants, with the number found per index case ranging from 6 to 8 (Fig 2).
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of an increase in CFA positivity from 2 to 8 cases in TAS 2, but it does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that the WHO criteria for stopping MDA are too lenient. The passage states that the WHO criteria were met in TAS 1 and TAS 2, and the increase in CFA positivity does not necessarily indicate that the criteria are too lenient. The passage also mentions that the increase in CFA positivity may be due to other factors, such as the sensitivity of the survey methods or the presence of hotspots of transmission. Therefore, the inference that the WHO criteria are too lenient and should be revised is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

201.Option: The fact that random sampling in neighboring EAs identified only a low number of CFA-positive cases suggests that transmission in Miragoane is isolated and unlikely to spread to surrounding communes.
Evidence: Random sampling in a neighboring EA identified only 3 positive participants for a positivity rate of 0.6% (Table 2).

Among the 6 index cases that yielded any CFA-positive cases in this investigation, purposive sampling found positives for 5 index cases in 3 geographic zones (Miragoane, rural and urban Anse-a-Veau), and random sampling in the index EA found positives for 4 index cases in 3 geographic zones (Miragoane, L’asile, urban Anse-a-Veau) (S1 Table). Both of these sampling methods missed cases in one zone each. Positivity rate by geographic zone ranged from 1.3–2.7% for purposive sampling and 2.0–9.6% for random sampling. Random sampling in a neighboring EA found one additional CFA-positive participant for 3 of the 6 index cases.
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that random sampling in neighboring EAs identified only a low number of CFA-positive cases, but it does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that transmission in Miragoane is isolated and unlikely to spread to surrounding communes. The passage mentions that the low number of CFA-positive cases in neighboring EAs may be due to the sensitivity of the survey methods or the presence of hotspots of transmission. The passage also mentions that the study was conducted in a single department and does not provide evidence about the spread of transmission to surrounding communes. Therefore, the inference that transmission in Miragoane is isolated and unlikely to spread to surrounding communes is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

201.Option: The higher CFA prevalence in Miragoane (2.4%) compared to the 2% threshold used to initiate TAS suggests ongoing transmission despite the cessation of MDA, and the presence of CFA-positive children under 7 years old indicates recent infection, which may require targeted intervention.
Evidence: Of the 1,927 participants who completed FTS testing, 29 were CFA-positive, for a total positivity rate of 1.5% (Table 2). Of these, 21 (72%) were from Miragoane, and each index case in this commune led to the identification of CFA-positive participants, with the number found per index case ranging from 6 to 8 (Fig 2).
Two CFA positives were found among participants aged <10 years (positivity 0.8%), both of which resided in Miragoane and were between the ages of 4 and 6 years.
Miragoane also had the highest rate of CFA positivity, with 2.4% of participants testing positive compared to the range of 0.0%–1.8% in other geographic zones (Chi squared P value = 0.03).
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the CFA prevalence in Miragoane (2.4%) is higher than the 2% threshold used to initiate TAS, which suggests ongoing transmission despite the cessation of MDA. The passage also provides evidence that there are CFA-positive children under 7 years old in Miragoane, which indicates recent infection. The presence of recent infection in children under 7 years old is a strong indicator of ongoing transmission, as children of this age are of sufficient age to develop a mature parasite but young enough for a current infection to indicate recent transmission. Therefore, the inference that ongoing transmission may require targeted intervention is supported by the evidence in the passage.

201.Option: The presence of CFA-positive children under 7 years in Miragoane, combined with the high seropositivity in older age groups, suggests that the population has experienced a prolonged period of low-level transmission, leading to widespread immune exposure.
Evidence: Two CFA positives were found among participants aged <10 years (positivity 0.8%), both of which resided in Miragoane and were between the ages of 4 and 6 years.

Normalized mean OD values from ELISA testing ranged from 0.04 to 4.5, with a mean of 0.11. The cutoff for serology positivity determined by FMM methods was 0.18. Using this cutoff, there were 87 serology-positive participants, for a seropositivity rate of 4.5%. Concordance with CFA antigen results was 95%, with 6 participants positive by both methods, 1,813 negative by both methods, 23 CFA-positive only, and 81 seropositive only.

The highest number of seropositive participants was found in Miragoane (43, 5.1% positive), but the highest percent seropositive was found in Petit-Trou de Nippes (6.0% positive), which had no CFA positives.
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of CFA-positive children under 7 years in Miragoane and high seropositivity in older age groups, but it does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that the population has experienced a prolonged period of low-level transmission leading to widespread immune exposure. The passage mentions that seropositivity can detect both current and past infection, but it does not provide evidence that the high seropositivity in older age groups is due to a prolonged period of low-level transmission. The passage also mentions that the highest percent seropositive was found in Petit-Trou de Nippes, which had no CFA positives, which suggests that seropositivity may not be solely due to recent transmission. Therefore, the inference that the population has experienced a prolonged period of low-level transmission leading to widespread immune exposure is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

201.Option: The high seropositivity rate among males and older adults in Miragoane suggests that these groups are more likely to have been exposed to LF during the period of active transmission, indicating a higher risk of reactivation of latent infection.
Evidence: Seropositivity was higher among male (5.8%) than female (3.7%) participants (P = 0.02) and was higher among participants who did not sleep under a bed net (5.1%) than those who did (2.3%) (P = 0.02) (Table 2 and Figs 4 and S3).

The highest number of seropositive participants was found in Miragoane (43, 5.1% positive), but the highest percent seropositive was found in Petit-Trou de Nippes (6.0% positive), which had no CFA positives.
Seropositivity was similar among participants who did or did not participate in MDA for LF in the past.
201.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high seropositivity rates among males and older adults in Miragoane, but it does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that these groups are more likely to have been exposed to LF during the period of active transmission or that they are at higher risk of reactivation of latent infection. The passage mentions that seropositivity can detect both current and past infection, but it does not provide evidence that the high seropositivity in males and older adults is due to reactivation of latent infection. The passage also mentions that seropositivity was similar among participants who did or did not participate in MDA for LF in the past, which suggests that MDA participation is not a significant factor in seropositivity. Therefore, the inference that males and older adults are at higher risk of reactivation of latent infection is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

202.Option: The higher abundance of haemoglobin subunit beta-2 in CBA/Ca mice is directly responsible for their resistance to Ascaris by enhancing oxygen delivery to the liver.
Evidence: The most abundantly expressed proteins observed in CBA/Ca control samples are haemoglobin subunit beta-2, S-methylmethionine, and putative hydroxypyruvate isomerase. The log2 fold change for haemoglobin subunit beta-2 was the highest observed in the dataset, being 13.6.
202.Rationale: While the passage notes that haemoglobin subunit beta-2 is highly abundant in CBA/Ca mice, it does not establish a causal relationship between this protein and resistance to Ascaris. The inference that haemoglobin subunit beta-2 directly enhances oxygen delivery to the liver and thereby contributes to resistance is not supported by the evidence presented. The passage lacks any direct evidence linking haemoglobin subunit beta-2 to Ascaris resistance or to liver oxygenation.

202.Option: The downregulation of ribosomal proteins under infection in both strains indicates that Ascaris actively suppresses host translation to evade immune detection.
Evidence: Ribosomal proteins were more abundant in both CBA/Ca controls and infected with respect to their C57BL/6J counterparts (S1A and S1C Fig). Under infection however, both CBA/Ca and C57BL/6J mice had significantly lower abundances for many ribosomal proteins (Fig 2). KEGG analysis further confirmed this finding demonstrating that CBA/Ca had more intrinsically abundant ribosomal proteins than C57BL/6J, a difference that became more pronounced under infection (S2B Fig).
202.Rationale: The passage describes a decrease in ribosomal protein abundance under infection in both strains, but it does not provide evidence that Ascaris actively suppresses host translation. The inference that this downregulation is a strategy by Ascaris to evade immune detection is not supported by the data. The passage does not establish a causal link between ribosomal protein downregulation and immune evasion by the parasite.

202.Option: The higher abundance of mitochondrial proteins and presumed reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels in CBA/Ca mice may create a prohibitive oxidative environment that restricts Ascaris larval migration, contributing to their resistance to infection.
Evidence: Mitochondrial associated proteins were consistently differentially abundant among all samples. Of the 2,307 proteins identified and reported here, 324 were associated with the mitochondrion. A higher abundance of mitochondrial proteins was generally observed in CBA/Ca mice both with and without infection (Table 1; Fig 1C) in comparison to C57BL/6J mice, although an increase in mitochondrial protein abundances was observed for both strains under infection (Fig 2).

KEGG analysis also indicated that there was a generally lower abundance of ETC proteins in C57BL/6J infected mice, compared to their CBA/Ca counterparts. However Complex I proteins, were less abundant in C57BL/6J infected mice, when compared to their controls (S2A Fig).

Proteins of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle were intrinsically more abundant in CBA/Ca control mice, when compared to their C57BL/6J counterparts. This difference between the two strains was observed also under infection. When infected, both mouse strains had increased abundance of these proteins. However, C57BL/6J infected mice had higher abundances for the entire TCA cycle (S3 Fig).
202.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that CBA/Ca mice have a higher abundance of mitochondrial proteins, including those involved in the electron transport chain and TCA cycle, both in control and infected states. This suggests a higher metabolic activity and potential for ROS production in CBA/Ca mice. The inference that this higher metabolic activity and presumed ROS levels create an oxidative environment that restricts Ascaris larval migration is supported by the data. The passage also notes that C57BL/6J mice have lower abundances of these proteins, which could contribute to their susceptibility. This causal relationship between mitochondrial protein abundance, ROS levels, and resistance to Ascaris is a logical inference from the evidence presented.

202.Option: The resistance of CBA/Ca mice to Ascaris infection is primarily due to their higher intrinsic expression of antioxidant enzymes, which directly neutralize Ascaris larvae in the liver.
Evidence: KEGG analysis identified an increase in glutathione metabolic proteins of C57BL/6J control samples, compared to their CBA/Ca counterparts (S4 Fig). This inherent difference was also observed when comparing both strains under infection. Additionally, there was an increase in abundance of glutathione metabolism proteins in C57BL/6J mice when infected, compared to their controls. This increase was less pronounced in CBA/Ca mice.
202.Rationale: The passage indicates that C57BL/6J mice have higher glutathione metabolism protein levels compared to CBA/Ca mice, both in control and infected states. This suggests that C57BL/6J mice have a higher capacity for ROS reduction, which is the opposite of what the option claims. The inference that CBA/Ca mice have higher intrinsic expression of antioxidant enzymes is not supported by the evidence. The data actually suggest that C57BL/6J mice have a greater antioxidant capacity, which would be expected in a susceptible strain if antioxidant activity were the primary resistance mechanism.

202.Option: The lower abundance of immune-associated proteins in CBA/Ca mice compared to C57BL/6J mice suggests that a weaker innate immune response is responsible for their resistance to Ascaris infection.
Evidence: Of these, 20 immune associated proteins were differentially abundant (relative fold difference > 1.5) between infected and control samples (Table 2). Eight proteins: s100-A8; s100-A9; dipeptidyl peptidase 1; coronin-1A; galectin; vitronectin; moesin and plasminogen were more abundantly expressed in both C57BL/6J and CBA/Ca infected mice with respect to their uninfected counterparts indicating a potential conservation of response to Ascaris in both mice strains. The majority of SSDA immune proteins were observed in C57BL/6J Ascaris-infected mice (Table 2). A number of immune proteins demonstrated higher intrinsic abundances in C57BL/6J compared to CBA/Ca (in both infected and uninfected comparisons) and included dipeptidyl peptidase 1, complement C4-B, galectin-3 and the MHC class I molecules H2-D1 and H2-K1 (Fig 2C and 2D).
202.Rationale: The passage indicates that C57BL/6J mice have higher intrinsic abundances of immune-associated proteins compared to CBA/Ca mice. The inference that a weaker innate immune response is responsible for CBA/Ca resistance is contradicted by the data. The evidence suggests that C57BL/6J mice have a stronger immune response, which is consistent with their susceptibility rather than resistance. Morphological analysis of the intestinal mucosa revealed an increase in the number of eosinophils and neutrophils starting at 10 days and persisting throughout the acute phase, up to the 22nd day after infection. An increase in the number of lymphocytes was observed at 17 days, and both lymphocytes and macrophages increased at 22 days in the acute phase. In the chronic phase, at 35 days, an increase in the number of eosinophils and macrophages was observed (Table 1). In the submucosal analyses, all the cell counts increased starting at 10 days after infection, persisting throughout the acute and chronic phases (Table 2). With respect to the quantification of enterocytes and goblet cells, we observed a significant increase in the number of these cells starting at the 10th and 17th days after infection, respectively, and remaining throughout the acute and chronic phases (Fig 6 and Table 3).

For G. p. gambiensis, there was no evidence for isolation by distance in any site along rivers. But this may be due to the very short length of river portions covered in each site.

This surface S = 100,000 m2 was thus used to divide effective population sizes to compute densities.
The surface defined above S∼0.01 km2 leads to an effective density of G. p. gambiensis De = Ne/S≈8000 (for Ne = 80) or De = 15000 (for Ne = 150) G. p. gambiensis per km2 in the study area.

211.Option: The recurrence of EV-A71 epidemics in Malaysia is due to the co-circulation of multiple subgenotypes, which increases genetic diversity and facilitates sustained transmission across different age groups.
Evidence: EV-A71 can be divided into three major genotypes, A, B and C, based on a cut-off nucleotide divergence value of 17–22% [4]. Genotypes B and C can be further subdivided into subgenotypes B0-B5 and C1-C5 respectively based on cut-off nucleotide divergence of 10–14% [4,5].

Many subgenotypes were co-circulating during EV-A71 epidemics. Subgenotypes B3, B4, C1 and C2 were present during the 1997 epidemic, but only subgenotypes B4 and C1 continued to circulate till 2001 and 2003, respectively (Fig 4C). After 2003, subgenotype B5 became the sole genotype circulating in Malaysia.

The decline in the effective population seen after the 1997 epidemic may coincide with purifying selection against subgenotypes B3 and C2. The decline in the effective population observed after the 2000 and 2003 epidemics may indicate purifying selection against subgenotypes B4 and C1, respectively.
211.Rationale: While the passage does describe the co-circulation of multiple subgenotypes and genetic diversity, it also shows that after 2003, only subgenotype B5 was circulating. The evidence suggests that subgenotype diversity decreases after each epidemic due to purifying selection, not that co-circulation facilitates sustained transmission. The passage emphasizes that the cyclical pattern is more likely due to changes in population immunity rather than subgenotype diversity.

The association between a 10% decrease between the preceding and current months and odds of an epidemic was 1.13 (95% CI 0.95–1.35) in those aged 1–6 years and 1.05 (95% CI 0.82–1.33) in those aged 7–12 years. The model incorporating the interaction terms of monthly seroprevalence with age groups and changes between seroprevalence of preceding and current months with age groups was tested, but showed non-significant interactions (P = 0.30). The monthly seroprevalence was positively associated with the odds of an epidemic period in the univariate analysis (OR for every 10% increase in seroprevalence, 1.40; 95% CI 1.18–1.65; P<0.001) and multivariate analysis after adjusting for plausible confounding factors such as age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance (adjusted OR for every 10% increase in seroprevalence, 1.45; 95% CI 1.24–1.69; P<0.001).
211.Rationale: While the study did collect climatic data and adjusted for these factors in the analysis, the results show that the association between seroprevalence and epidemics remained significant even after adjusting for temperature, rainfall, and other climatic variables. The association between a 10% decrease between the preceding and current months and odds of an epidemic was 1.13 (95% CI 0.95–1.35) in those aged 1–6 years and 1.05 (95% CI 0.82–1.33) in those aged 7–12 years.

The monthly seroprevalence was positively associated with the odds of an epidemic period in the univariate analysis (OR for every 10% increase in seroprevalence, 1.40; 95% CI 1.18–1.65; P<0.001) and multivariate analysis after adjusting for plausible confounding factors such as age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance (adjusted OR for every 10% increase in seroprevalence, 1.45; 95% CI 1.24–1.69; P<0.001).

The passage specifically mentions S. stercoralis as one of the confirmed helminth infections. 213.Option: The lower T. cruzi PCR positivity rate in non-helminth-infected patients (35.2%) indicates that the PCR test is less sensitive in individuals without helminth co-infection.

213.Option: The presence of helminth infection in chronic Chagas disease patients may lead to increased parasitaemia due to a suppressed Th1 immune response, which could explain the higher T. cruzi PCR positivity observed in this study.

"the percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021)."

The Th1/Th2 polarization mechanism is a well-established immunological principle in parasitology. The observed association between helminth infection and increased T. cruzi PCR positivity, combined with the known immunomodulatory effects of helminths, supports the inference that helminth-induced Th1 suppression could lead to increased parasitaemia and thus higher PCR detection rates.

"the percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021)."

The study found an association between helminth infection and T. cruzi PCR positivity within the Bolivian cohort, not between different geographic origins. The passage does not mention or suggest that regional differences in T. cruzi strains are responsible for the observed association, making this inference unsupported by the evidence presented.

Evidence: The estimated JE CFR in 2000–2018 for Brunei was 20%, Sri Lanka 7%, Pakistan 20%, Papua New Guinea 15%, and Timor-Leste 20%. Even though the projected JE CFR in 2019–2030 increased considerably from the previous two decades in Bangladesh and Vietnam (Table 4), the associated confidence intervals were fairly wide, and the projected estimates were not statistically different from the predicted estimate in 2000–2018.
