This supports the inference that temperature extremes (specifically high temperatures) may prevent the establishment of new MSF foci in warmer regions. The biological principle that higher mortality rates reduce population persistence is well-established, making this a valid epidemiological inference from the data presented.

251.Option: The reactivation phenomenon observed in R. conorii conorii-infected ticks at elevated temperatures explains why infected ticks are more likely to survive winter conditions in temperate climates.
Evidence: The same experiment was performed with several batches of eggs, larvae and unfed nymphs of infected and non-infected ticks. None of these stages, either infected or non-infected, could tolerate low (4°C) or high (37°C) temperatures for one month (data not shown)

In the first batch of adults, which was maintained at 4°C for one month and then transferred at 25°C for rabbit attachment, 10–40% infected ticks died compared to a lack of death for non-infected ticks (30/120 vs. 0/80, p = 0.0001)
251.Rationale: The passage does not mention any reactivation phenomenon at elevated temperatures. Instead, it shows that infected ticks have higher mortality rates at both high (37°C) and low (4°C) temperatures compared to non-infected ticks. The claim about increased survival in winter conditions is directly contradicted by the evidence showing higher mortality in infected ticks at low temperatures. This option introduces a concept (reactivation) not supported by the passage and makes an incorrect inference from the data.

252.Option: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
Evidence: All groups receiving CHIKV antigens generated neutralizing antibody titers that are significantly different (P<0.01) from both control groups (PBS and Ac-GFP) but not significantly different from one another (Fig. 5A). The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).

The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
252.Rationale: This option is a direct quotation from the passage and does not represent an inference. It merely restates the data presented in the passage without drawing a new conclusion about the protective efficacy of the VLP vaccine. The question asks for an inference about protective efficacy, not a restatement of the antibody titer data.

252.Option: The complete CHIKV structural cassette was cloned downstream the strong polyhedrin promoter of the AcMNPV baculovirus (Ac-S27). Western analysis on cell and medium fractions of Sf21-cells that were infected with Ac-S27 indicated that glycoprotein processing (glycosylation, furin cleavage and surface localization) is efficient and that the functionality of E1 as fusion protein is retained.
Evidence: A recombinant baculovirus (Ac-S27) was generated to produce CHIKV VLPs by expressing the complete CHIKV-S27 structural polyprotein (C, E3, E2, 6K, E1) (Fig. 1A). The coding sequence of the structural polyprotein was cloned downstream the polyhedrin promoter in an AcMNPV backbone, after which Sf21 cells were infected with a MOI = 10 TCID50/ml. Glycoprotein expression in the cell fraction as well as in the medium fraction was analyzed by WB using α-E1 (Fig. 1B) and α-E2 (Fig. 1C) polyclonal antibodies.

To test whether CHIKV E1 retains its functionality as a fusion protein, a pH-dependent syncytia formation assay was performed. Sf9-ET cells were infected with Ac-S27, Ac-6KE1 and Ac-GFP (negative control). Syncytia formation at pH = 6.4, pH = 5.8, pH = 5.5 was readily observed in cells expressing CHIKV structural proteins (Ac-S27) (Fig. 3, right) and in the positive control (Ac-6KE1) (Fig. 3, middle) but not in the negative control (Ac-GFP) (Fig. 3, left).
252.Rationale: This option describes the methodology and results of the VLP production and characterization, but does not address the protective efficacy of the VLP vaccine in the challenged mouse model. The question specifically asks about inferences regarding protective efficacy, not about the production or characterization of the VLPs.

252.Option: The 1 µg non-adjuvanted VLP vaccine elicited a similar neutralizing antibody response as compared to 10 µg of inactivated virus (Fig. 5A), yet induced lower IgG2c titers (Fig. 5C). This might suggest that chemical inactivation results in generation of antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity, although further experimentation would be required to confirm this.
Evidence: The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
At the 1 µg dose, VLPs produced ∼10–20 fold, significantly lower antibody titers (IgG1 P<0.05, IgG2c P<0.01) than 10 µg inactivated CHIKV.
The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).
252.Rationale: This option introduces a hypothesis not supported by the passage. The passage states that the 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable to 10 µg inactivated CHIKV, but it does not suggest that chemical inactivation results in antibodies with reduced neutralizing activity. The passage does not make this causal claim, and the option goes beyond the evidence presented.

252.Option: A single low-dose immunization with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
Evidence: All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
The 1 µg dose of VLPs (with or without Quil A) provided complete protection against foot swelling, while the 0.1 µg dose reduced the peak foot swelling from 60–70% to 20–30% (Fig. 6B).
These results illustrate that non-adjuvanted VLPs can provide complete protection against CHIKV-induced arthritis.
252.Rationale: This option directly states a conclusion that can be drawn from the passage. The passage explicitly states that all animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia and foot swelling, and that non-adjuvanted VLPs can provide complete protection against CHIKV-induced arthritis. This is a direct inference about the protective efficacy of the VLP vaccine based on the outcomes observed in the challenged mouse model.

252.Option: The VLPs induced a balanced IgG1/IgG2c response, in contrast with CHIKV infection where IgG2c responses dominate [12]. Whether this would have an important effect on protection is unclear.
Evidence: CHIKV-specific IgG1 and IgG2c titers were determined by ELISA, and broadly similar titers were seen for IgG1 and IgG2c (Fig. 5B, C), which contrasts with natural CHIKV infection where IgG2c dominates [12].
At the 1 µg dose, VLPs produced ∼10–20 fold, significantly lower antibody titers (IgG1 P<0.05, IgG2c P<0.01) than 10 µg inactivated CHIKV.
The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).
252.Rationale: This option is a direct statement from the passage and does not represent an inference about protective efficacy. It merely describes the antibody isotype response without drawing a conclusion about the protective efficacy of the VLP vaccine. The question asks for an inference about protective efficacy, not about the antibody isotype response.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after MDA is due to the high rate of reinfection following clinical cure, which is more common in children than in adults.
Evidence: The infectivity of H. ducreyi is high [7], and reinfections following clinical and microbiological cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common [8].

Of 17 cultures obtained from asymptomatic contacts, 8 (47%) yielded no growth and 9 (53%) yielded small gram negative rods or coccobacilli. Six of the gram negative organisms were tested by PCR and 2 were confirmed to be H. ducreyi.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
253.Rationale: While the passage does mention high infectivity and common reinfections after clinical cure of genital ulcers, it does not provide evidence that this is more common in children than in adults. The study focused on children, but the passage does not compare reinfection rates between children and adults. The persistence of H. ducreyi is attributed to multiple factors including asymptomatic colonization and environmental reservoirs, not solely to reinfection rates in children.

253.Option: The presence of H. ducreyi DNA in flies and bed linens indicates that these vectors are the primary source of new infections in the community.
Evidence: Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
Nevertheless, there is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.
253.Rationale: The passage shows that H. ducreyi DNA was found in flies and bed linens, but it explicitly states there is no clear evidence of transmission by these vectors. The presence of DNA does not prove these are the primary sources of new infections. The passage suggests these could be potential sources but does not establish them as the primary source of new infections in the community.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after mass azithromycin treatment may be explained by asymptomatic skin colonization, environmental reservoirs such as flies and bed linens, and incomplete penetration of azithromycin to the skin surface.
Evidence: Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
The infectivity of H. ducreyi is high [7], and reinfections following clinical and microbiological cure of H. ducreyi genital ulcers are common [8].

The demonstration of bacterial colonization or carriage usually requires a positive culture as an indication of presence and multiplication of microorganisms. However, culture of H. ducreyi is expensive and technically difficult and T. p. pertenue cannot be grown in vitro.

The persistence of H. ducreyi skin ulcers after mass azithromycin treatment [12,13] raises the possibility that the bacteria may exist in a natural reservoir. If H. ducreyi adheres and survives on the healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers, this could enhance its persistence in the community and its transmission, because skin-colonizing bacteria might escape systemic azithromycin treatment and could infect the skin after a minor abrasion.

Of 17 cultures obtained from asymptomatic contacts, 8 (47%) yielded no growth and 9 (53%) yielded small gram negative rods or coccobacilli. Six of the gram negative organisms were tested by PCR and 2 were confirmed to be H. ducreyi.
253.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this explanation. It discusses asymptomatic skin colonization as a potential reservoir, notes the presence of H. ducreyi DNA in flies and bed linens as environmental reservoirs, and suggests that azithromycin may not fully penetrate the skin surface to eliminate the bacteria. The study found H. ducreyi DNA on asymptomatic children's skin, in flies, and on bed linens, and the passage explicitly raises the possibility of asymptomatic colonization and environmental reservoirs as explanations for persistence after MDA.

253.Option: The high prevalence of H. ducreyi in asymptomatic children suggests that the organism is more transmissible through sexual contact than through non-intact skin contact.
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Of 78 asymptomatic participants evaluated, 16 (21%) had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
253.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that both infections are transmitted through non-intact skin contact, not sexual contact. The high prevalence in asymptomatic children is attributed to skin contact transmission, not sexual transmission. The option incorrectly suggests a mode of transmission that is not supported by the passage.

253.Option: The limited impact of azithromycin on H. ducreyi is likely due to widespread resistance to macrolides in the H. ducreyi population.
Evidence: Cutaneous strains of H. ducreyi are also susceptible to macrolides in vitro [11].

A single oral dose of 30 mg/kg azithromycin is highly effective for treatment of yaws [9], while 15 mg/kg generally provides effective treatment and prophylaxis against H. ducreyi genital ulcer strains [10].

In the context of yaws eradication, the use of mass drug administration (MDA) with oral azithromycin might be expected to be effective for both yaws and H. ducreyi. However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].
253.Rationale: The passage states that cutaneous strains of H. ducreyi are susceptible to macrolides in vitro and that azithromycin is effective against genital ulcer strains. The limited impact on H. ducreyi after MDA is not attributed to resistance but rather to factors like asymptomatic colonization and environmental reservoirs. The passage does not provide evidence of widespread resistance to macrolides in the H. ducreyi population.

254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
Evidence: Over 70% of people reported bat use of their buildings for >5 years (n = 72), with bat presence for 5–10 years most commonly reported (χ2 = 36.52, P < 0.01, Fig 1). Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round (n = 90).

Survey participants described frequent exposure to bats that would support pathogen transmission through two main routes: direct and indirect (fecal/oral) contact, with indirect contact between bats and humans reported more frequently than direct contacts (χ2 = 24.77, P < 0.01, Fig 2A).

Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten. People on the property (children, spouses, custodians) other than the respondent engaged in these interactions as well.

Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98). Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B) and children, spouses, house guests, and custodians were also involved in these interactions.
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Interestingly, 13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property, most often their own animals.
254.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. First, it establishes that bat presence in buildings is both frequent and prolonged, with over 70% of people reporting bat use of their buildings for more than 5 years and 88% reporting year-round presence. This creates sustained opportunities for human-bat interactions. Second, the study documents both direct and indirect contact routes: 39% of participants reported direct contact (touching, bites) and 90% reported indirect contact (exposure to feces/urine) with daily occurrences for 78% of participants. Third, the passage describes common attempts to remove bats (80% of participants) that often result in direct contact (34 cases). Finally, the study notes that domestic animals (dogs, cats, chickens) were observed consuming dead bats, which could serve as intermediate hosts for bat-borne pathogens. All these factors together create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure across multiple transmission routes, consistent with the principles of zoonotic spillover dynamics.

254.Option: The high frequency of indirect contact with bat excreta and the common practice of disposal of dead bats by throwing them over property lines suggest that bat-borne pathogens are likely to spread widely within communities through environmental contamination.
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98). Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B) and children, spouses, house guests, and custodians were also involved in these interactions.

Nearly 65% reported dead bats on properties (n = 51). Most removed dead bats, usually by throwing them over property lines (n = 30) or swept them outside (n = 7). Some also reported burning (n = 6), feeding domestic cats (n = 2), and burying bat carcasses (n = 1).
254.Rationale: While the passage does describe high frequency of indirect contact with bat excreta and common disposal practices for dead bats, it does not provide evidence to support the inference that bat-borne pathogens are likely to spread widely within communities through environmental contamination. The passage documents the existence of potential transmission routes but does not provide data on actual pathogen spread or environmental contamination patterns. The inference about widespread community transmission requires additional evidence not presented in the passage, such as data on pathogen shedding in excreta, environmental persistence of pathogens, or evidence of community-level transmission events. The passage only establishes the potential for environmental contamination, not its actual occurrence or extent.

255.Option: The use of household water treatment methods such as boiling significantly reduces the risk of infection from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in drinking water, as demonstrated by the 6 log10 reduction in viable parasites.
Evidence: To account for the effects of boiling water (the only point of use treatment method used by the study population) on parasite viability, we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling (i.e. 6 log10 = 99.9999% reduction) and used household survey data collected from study households (n = 355) to estimate the fraction of households that boiled their drinking water (9% and 16% of deep and shallow tubewell users, respectively, reported boiling; see Table 1, B, boiling rates) (Appendix B.5 in Supporting Information).

The full equation used to estimate the dose, d, of the number of pathogens ingested per day per individual able to cause an infection in humans is: (4) where C (# 20 L-1) is a random sample from the gamma distribution describing the observed concentration of parasites (by year, tubewell type, and protozoa species) adjusted for non-detects, MR is the method recovery fraction determined by spiking trials, V is the fraction of viable parasites able to cause host infection, Sp is the fraction of parasites in the dose which are a species infectious-to-humans, LR is the reduction of parasites from boiling (1 if no boiling occurred), B is a dichotomous term that indicates if the household boiled (i.e. yes or no), and L is the volume of water ingested in liters per day.

Boxplots of daily risk are shown in Fig 3 in log10 scale and reveal the mean risk of waterborne protozoal infection per day, accounting for boiling rates, varied between 0.06% and 1.5% (between about 1/1000 and 15/1000 children infected daily) (see Fig F for risk profiles in S1 Supporting Information).
255.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that boiling water reduces parasite viability by 6 log10 (99.9999%) and incorporates this into the model equation as a key parameter. The study also collected data on boiling rates (9% and 16% for deep and shallow tubewell users) and explicitly accounted for this in the risk calculations. The boxplots of daily risk show that boiling rates were factored into the risk estimates. This directly supports the conclusion that household water treatment methods like boiling significantly reduce infection risk.

255.Option: The spatial proximity of tubewells to household latrines is the main determinant of whether detected parasites are from human or non-human sources, directly influencing disease burden estimates.
Evidence: Fraction of detected parasites of a species infectious-to-humans.As tubewell samples may have contained a mixture of human and non-human shed parasites, we simulated three different scenarios to estimate the fraction of parasites detected in a tubewell sample that was a species infectious-to-humans (Sp), with Sp determined as follows: (2) where H is the fraction of human shed parasites and  is the fraction of non-human shed parasites of a species infectious-to-humans (i.e. zoonotic fraction), with H calculated for each year (2012–2013), tubewell type (deep and shallow), and parasite (Cryptosporidium and Giardia) when appropriate.

Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human host (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15]. Livestock included cattle, buffalo, sheep, and goats and domestic animals included dogs, as these represented the majority of animal species in the study region.

Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55]. MST uses molecular methods to detect enteric bacteria specific to a host to identify the likely source of fecal contamination.
255.Rationale: While the study does consider the source of parasites (human vs. non-human), it does not mention spatial proximity to latrines as a determinant. Instead, it uses environmental load calculations (ENV scenario) and microbial source tracking (MST scenario) to estimate the fraction of human vs. non-human parasites. The option incorrectly introduces a spatial proximity factor not discussed in the passage.

255.Option: The concentration of Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water varies significantly between years, which is the primary driver of differences in estimated disease burden between 2012 and 2013.
Evidence: Protozoal concentrations in tubewells.As previously reported [15], Cryptosporidium and Giardia concentrations in deep and shallow tubewells were measured once in 206 tubewells in Odisha Sanitation Trial villages, during the monsoon season (June-August) of either 2012 or 2013. A two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicated concentrations were significantly different between years (P-value = < 0.001).

Estimated child diarrhea daily point prevalence is shown in Fig 4 and reveals a wide range of prevalence, from a median as high as 6.5% to as low as < 1% at the population level considering estimated levels of water treatment by boiling. Across all tubewell types and years, assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV) for Puri District (see Table H in S1 Supporting Information). Comparing years, 2012 had a much higher estimated prevalence of diarrhea compared to 2013 under all scenarios. In 2012, deep tubewell users were estimated to have higher diarrhea prevalence than shallow tubewell users, with Cryptosporidium often causing more symptomatic infections than Giardia across human pathogenicity scenarios.

The simulated 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence from waterborne infections are compared to the all-cause observed levels reported in the Odisha Sanitation Trial during the monsoon season (~12% in 2012 and ~ 9% in 2013) [21] in Table 4. The fraction of the observed all-cause diarrhea during each monsoon season that can be explained by the estimated tubewell drinking water protozoa infections under each human pathogenicity scenario (HUM, MST, ENV) is presented. The results indicate that as much as 65.8% or as little as 2.9% of the all-cause diarrhea burden in < 5 children can be attributed to waterborne infections from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water. Depending on the scenario examined (HUM, MST, ENV), we see a wide range of estimated attributable fractions, but a clear trend that a far greater fraction of diarrhea can be attributed to Cryptosporidium and Giardia contamination in drinking water during the monsoon season in 2012, when child diarrhea prevalence rates were higher (~12% 7-day recall period) compared to 2013 (~9% 7-day recall period).
255.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that concentrations of Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewells were significantly different between 2012 and 2013 (p < 0.001). The results also show that 2012 had much higher estimated diarrhea prevalence than 2013 across all scenarios. The higher observed diarrhea prevalence in 2012 (~12%) compared to 2013 (~9%) further supports the conclusion that concentration differences between years are a primary driver of disease burden variation.

255.Option: The morbidity ratio of symptomatic infections for Cryptosporidium and Giardia is the most sensitive parameter in the model, leading to the greatest variation in estimated disease burden.
Evidence: Results from the sensitivity analysis for the HUM scenario combined by sample year and tubewell type are shown in Fig 5. Of the parameters examined, Cryptosporidium 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence estimates were most sensitive to the rate parameter used in the dose-response model, the fraction of detected parasites assumed to be viable, and the method recovery of parasites from tubewell samples. For Giardia, diarrhea estimates were most sensitive to model parameters representing viability, method recovery, and the concentration of Giardia observed in tubewell samples. For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to the volume of water ingested, the fraction of the population that treated water by boiling, the morbidity ratio, and recall bias as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.

To determine if a susceptible child transitioned into the infected state on a given day, first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child. If P(i) = 1, the probability that the infection resulted in a diarrheic symptom (P(s)) was modeled based on published studies of the diarrhea morbidity ratio for children diagnosed with Cryptosporidium [45, 47] or Giardia [9, 46] in developing countries. These included two large scale, multi-country, case-control studies designed to identify primary pathogens responsible for causing diarrhea in children in developing countries (the GEMS and MAL-ED) and two smaller scale regional studies conducted in settings with inadequate access to clean water and sanitation. Variability in the morbidity ratio across studies was modeled as a Bernoulli process with parameter p represented by a uniform distribution between of 0.28–0.68 and 0.49–0.59 for Cryptosporidium and Giardia respectively (see P(s) in Table 1).
255.Rationale: The sensitivity analysis explicitly states that the morbidity ratio had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2 for both parasites, indicating it was not a major driver of variation in disease burden estimates. The passage also describes the morbidity ratio as being modeled with a uniform distribution, but this parameter was not among the most sensitive in the analysis. The option incorrectly claims it is the most sensitive parameter.

255.Option: The fraction of parasites in drinking water that are from humans versus non-human animal hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans has important implications for infection risk and diarrhea disease burden estimates.
Evidence: Fraction of detected parasites of a species infectious-to-humans.As tubewell samples may have contained a mixture of human and non-human shed parasites, we simulated three different scenarios to estimate the fraction of parasites detected in a tubewell sample that was a species infectious-to-humans (Sp), with Sp determined as follows: (2) where H is the fraction of human shed parasites and  is the fraction of non-human shed parasites of a species infectious-to-humans (i.e. zoonotic fraction), with H calculated for each year (2012–2013), tubewell type (deep and shallow), and parasite (Cryptosporidium and Giardia) when appropriate.

Scenario one (HUM) assumed humans shed all of the detected parasites and 100% of both parasites were species infectious-to-humans. Therefore, H = 1 always and  is irrelevant in the calculation of Sp. The HUM scenario equated to the case with the highest level of parasites infectious-to-humans in tubewells and thus represented the scenario with the highest risk of infection.

Scenario two (ENV) assumed the probability of a parasite shed by a human host (H) was directly proportional to the ratio of the estimated environmental load of parasites shed by the population of humans to the total estimated environmental load shed by the population of humans, livestock, and domestic animals in Puri District [15]. Livestock included cattle, buffalo, sheep, and goats and domestic animals included dogs, as these represented the majority of animal species in the study region.

Scenario three (MST) used microbial fecal source tracking (MST) results by year and tubewell type for the same protozoa-tested tubewell water samples [55]. MST uses molecular methods to detect enteric bacteria specific to a host to identify the likely source of fecal contamination.

Estimated child diarrhea daily point prevalence is shown in Fig 4 and reveals a wide range of prevalence, from a median as high as 6.5% to as low as < 1% at the population level considering estimated levels of water treatment by boiling. Across all tubewell types and years, assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV) for Puri District (see Table H in S1 Supporting Information).
255.Rationale: The passage explicitly discusses three scenarios (HUM, ENV, MST) to estimate the fraction of parasites that are infectious-to-humans. It shows that assuming all parasites are from humans (HUM scenario) significantly increases diarrhea prevalence estimates compared to other scenarios. The results demonstrate that the source of parasites (human vs. non-human) and their infectivity to humans has a major impact on disease burden estimates. This directly supports the conclusion that this factor is important for infection risk and disease burden estimates.

256.Option: The fact that 74.9% of patients reported trismus indicates that surgical release of ankylosis is ineffective and should be avoided in Noma survivors.
Evidence: The acute phase of the disease has a high mortality, and the evolution is exceptionally rapid, causing the loss of soft and bony facial tissues within days [17,18].
The surgical phase is usually initiated 6 to 18 months after a period of quiescence [20].

Even after modern and sophisticated interventions (particularly reconstructive and plastic surgeries), Noma is associated with considerable morbidity and mortality, and, understandably, psychosocial impacts on the patients remain substantial [21].

Of these, 32.5% (n = 53), 25.2% (n = 41), and 13.5% (n = 22) have reported grade-1, grade-2, and grade-3 mouth opening limitations, respectively. Furthermore, a single locked jaw (grade-4) case has been reported.
256.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence regarding the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of surgical release of ankylosis in Noma survivors. While it mentions that 71.8% of patients reported grade-2 to grade-4 mouth opening limitations (trismus), it also states that surgical interventions are typically initiated 6 to 18 months after the acute phase. The passage further notes that even with modern interventions, Noma is associated with considerable morbidity and mortality. The claim that surgical release is ineffective and should be avoided cannot be derived from the passage, as the passage does not evaluate the outcomes of surgical interventions.

256.Option: The study's findings indicate that Noma is primarily a disease of malnutrition and poor sanitation, and therefore, improving public health infrastructure alone would eliminate the disease in Ethiopia.
Evidence: The major risk factors of ANUG/Noma in children are malnutrition, diarrheal diseases, measles infection, lack of proper sanitation, and poor living conditions.

Ethiopia is one of the countries in the Noma-belt region where people, particularly children, are profoundly affected by the disease. However, attention and resources dedicated to this devastating condition remains inadequate [24].
256.Rationale: While the passage identifies malnutrition and poor sanitation as major risk factors for Noma in children, it does not provide evidence that improving public health infrastructure alone would eliminate the disease. The passage also notes that Ethiopia is in the Noma-belt region, suggesting that geographic and possibly other factors contribute to the disease's prevalence. The claim that public health improvements alone would eliminate Noma is an overgeneralization not supported by the passage.

256.Option: The presence of dental anarchy in 14 patients suggests that Noma survivors are at increased risk of developing oral cancer due to chronic inflammation and tissue damage.
Evidence: Out of the 109 medical records, 14 have reported different levels of disoriented teeth.
The disease is associated with very high morbidity and mortality [9]. Associated septicemia, intracranial infection, and pneumonia are the leading causes of death.
256.Rationale: The passage mentions that 14 patients reported disoriented teeth (dental anarchy) but does not provide any evidence linking this to an increased risk of oral cancer. The passage focuses on the immediate and long-term functional and physical consequences of Noma, such as trismus and facial disfigurement, but does not discuss the risk of cancer. The inference about oral cancer is not supported by the data presented.

256.Option: The high prevalence of combined facial defects and severe functional limitations in Noma survivors, particularly trismus and dental anarchy, indicates that the disease leads to substantial long-term disability and social marginalization.
Evidence: The study was initiated to assess the sequela and severity of Noma in Ethiopia. The findings of this study can be used as a baseline for further investigation and help to fill the current knowledge gap.

The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13]. In those with more advanced Noma, the lesions and contractures often lead to growth disturbance and result in further facial disfigurement and functional impairment [14].

Even after modern and sophisticated interventions (particularly reconstructive and plastic surgeries), Noma is associated with considerable morbidity and mortality, and, understandably, psychosocial impacts on the patients remain substantial [21]. Often, those patients that survive are not only severely disfigured, but also rejected from family and society [22].
256.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of the long-term disability and social marginalization experienced by Noma survivors. It states that the study aimed to assess the sequela and severity of Noma, and the findings show that the disease leads to significant functional limitations such as trismus and dental anarchy. The passage also explicitly mentions that survivors are often rejected by family and society, indicating social marginalization. The high prevalence of combined facial defects and functional limitations supports the inference that Noma leads to substantial long-term disability and social marginalization.

256.Option: The high prevalence of ANUG in children under 10 years in Ethiopia suggests that early intervention for ANUG could prevent the progression to Noma and reduce the overall disease burden.
Evidence: In many cases, the disease begins to develop from acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG) [2].
The major risk factors of ANUG/Noma in children are malnutrition, diarrheal diseases, measles infection, lack of proper sanitation, and poor living conditions.

The study was initiated to assess the sequela and severity of Noma in Ethiopia. The findings of this study can be used as a baseline for further investigation and help to fill the current knowledge gap.
256.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Noma often begins as ANUG, and it identifies the risk factors for ANUG in children. This suggests a causal relationship between ANUG and Noma. The inference that early intervention for ANUG could prevent progression to Noma is supported by the passage's description of the disease's progression. The study's focus on assessing the sequela and severity of Noma in Ethiopia also implies that understanding the early stages (ANUG) is important for reducing the disease burden.

257.Option: The high level of non-synonymous polymorphisms in gp43 is due to sequencing errors and methodological artifacts, which artificially inflated the dN/dS ratio and led to false detection of positive selection.
Evidence: No differences were found between the linear and the quadratic models, neither for virulence factors (LRT = 2.134, p = 0.144), nor the housekeeping genes (LRT = 0.112, p = 0.7378), nor for the pooled data (LRT = 1.631; p = 0.2015) indicating that the lineal model is more appropriate to explain the relationship between dN and dS. Therefore, mutational saturation is not responsible for the elevated dN/dS ratios observed in the virulence factors.
Because no such pattern is observed in Figure 2, we consider this explanation unlikely.
257.Rationale: The passage explicitly rules out sequencing errors and methodological artifacts as explanations for the observed dN/dS ratios. The authors performed statistical tests (LRT) and visual analysis of mutational saturation patterns, finding no evidence to support these alternative explanations. The non-linear relationship between dN and dS that would be expected from sequencing errors is not observed in the data.

257.Option: The gp43 gene is under strong purifying selection, as indicated by the low levels of genetic variation and the absence of significant positive selection in the M-K test.
Evidence: Using the one-ratio model (M0), the average value of ω for the gp43 gene was 1.168 - significantly higher than for any of the housekeeping genes [48].

Models of positive selection (discrete, selection, beta and ω models) that allow for sites with ω greater than 1 fit the gp43 data significantly better than the corresponding neutral models (one-ratio, neutral and beta models) (Table 5).
We found evidence of variation in ω among lineages, as well as substantial variation in ω between sites in the data set.
257.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that the gp43 gene is not under strong purifying selection. The ω ratio (dN/dS) is significantly greater than 1 (1.168), and models allowing for positive selection fit the data significantly better than neutral models. The M-K test was not significant for gp43 (P = 0.40), but this absence of significance does not indicate purifying selection - it simply means the test did not detect an association between polymorphism and divergence in this case.

257.Option: The long time to the most recent common ancestor (TMRCA) of gp43 alleles indicates that the gene has undergone recent positive selection, leading to rapid fixation of beneficial mutations in the P. brasiliensis lineage.
Evidence: The TMRCAs for S1 and PS2 were estimated based on genetic variation at the gp43 locus and seven other nuclear loci. The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the long TMRCA for gp43 is evidence for balancing selection, not recent positive selection. A long TMRCA suggests that genetic diversity has been maintained over a long period, which is characteristic of balancing selection rather than recent positive selection that would typically result in a shorter TMRCA as beneficial alleles sweep through the population.

257.Option: The gp43 gene has evolved under positive directional selection, as evidenced by the significant results of the M-K test and the fixation of advantageous amino acid replacements in P. brasiliensis.
Evidence: For the gp43 case, the M-K test yielded no significant results between H. capsulatum and P. brasiliensis (Fischer's exact test, P = 0.40, Table 3).
257.Rationale: The M-K test for gp43 was not significant (P = 0.40), contradicting the claim in this option. While the passage does show evidence of positive selection through other methods (codon-based likelihood analyses with significant LRTs), the M-K test was not significant for this gene. The option incorrectly attributes significance to the M-K test results for gp43.

257.Option: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection.
Evidence: The TMRCAs for S1 and PS2 were estimated based on genetic variation at the gp43 locus and seven other nuclear loci. The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.

The values of the parameters under the discrete model (M3) indicated that 59.3% of the sites in the gp43 gene were under purifying selection (ω = 0), whereas 37.07% belonged to a site class with ω = 1.63, and 3.6% had an ω equal to 18.53, indicating that the two latter classes are under positive selection.

The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. The long TMRCA indicates that the polymorphism in gp43 has been maintained over a long evolutionary period, consistent with balancing selection. The codon-based likelihood analyses show that a significant proportion of sites in gp43 are under positive selection (ω>1), while others are under purifying selection. The persistence of non-synonymous polymorphisms within and between lineages, combined with evidence of both positive and purifying selection, supports the conclusion that these polymorphisms have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection pressures.

258.Option: Heterotypic neutralizing antibodies, particularly against DENV-4, were more strongly associated with protection against DENV-2 infection than homotypic antibodies, suggesting that cross-protection may be more effective than serotype-specific immunity in some cases.
Evidence: For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
258.Rationale: The passage states that for DENV-2 clusters, models using heterotypic DENV-4 NTs had a higher AUC (0.880) than homotypic DENV-2 NTs (0.740). This suggests that DENV-4 heterotypic antibodies were more predictive of protection against DENV-2 than homotypic DENV-2 antibodies. However, this does not prove that cross-protection is more effective than serotype-specific immunity overall. The study only shows that DENV-4 heterotypic NTs were more predictive in this specific case, not that cross-protection is generally more effective than homotypic immunity. The option overgeneralizes the finding.

258.Option: Pre-existing homotypic neutralizing antibody titers measured by PRNT were positively associated with protection against infection by DENV-1, -2, and -4, with the level of protection varying by serotype.
Evidence: When all serotypes were combined, PCR status was significantly associated with the log of homotypic NT both alone and adjusted for age (Table 2).
For DENV-1 clusters, age-adjusted models to predict PCR status were better (based on AIC and AUC) when using homotypic DENV-1 NTs (AUC = 0.833 [95% CI 0.590, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
For DENV-4 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using homotypic DENV-4 NTs (AUC = 0.889 [95% CI 0.667, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
258.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that homotypic neutralizing antibody titers were positively associated with protection against DENV-1, -2, and -4. The AUC values for homotypic NTs were higher than for heterotypic NTs in DENV-1 and DENV-4 clusters, and even in DENV-2 clusters, homotypic NTs showed some predictive value. The variation in AUC values across serotypes (0.833 for DENV-1, 0.889 for DENV-4, 0.740 for DENV-2) directly supports the claim that the level of protection varies by serotype. This conclusion is directly supported by the statistical findings presented in the passage.

258.Option: The protective effect of neutralizing antibodies is consistent across all dengue serotypes, with similar titers required for protection regardless of the infecting serotype.
Evidence: Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.
For DENV-1 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.554).
For DENV-4 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥16 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 0.842).
For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that the protective titer cutoffs vary significantly across serotypes (11 for DENV-1, 16 for DENV-4, and 323 for DENV-2). This directly contradicts the claim that the protective effect is consistent across all serotypes with similar titers required. The variation in cutoff values indicates that the level of neutralizing antibodies needed for protection differs by serotype, making this option incorrect.

258.Option: The lack of clinical efficacy against DENV-2 in the recent dengue vaccine trial in Thailand may have partly been due to insufficiently high DENV-2 NTs, suggesting that vaccine-induced antibody levels should be optimized to match natural infection thresholds.
Evidence: Recently, the first human efficacy trial of a dengue vaccine candidate was completed in Thailand showing good neutralizing antibody response to all four DENV serotypes after vaccination, but no clinical efficacy against DENV-2 infection [4].
Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.
For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The passage states that the dengue vaccine trial in Thailand showed good neutralizing antibody responses but no clinical efficacy against DENV-2. The study found that for DENV-2, the homotypic NT cutoff for protection was 323, which is much higher than the cutoffs for DENV-1 (11) and DENV-4 (16). This suggests that the vaccine-induced DENV-2 NTs may not have reached the threshold level (323) required for protection, which could explain the lack of clinical efficacy against DENV-2. This inference is directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

258.Option: The study design using geographic clusters with matched exposed but uninfected individuals provides definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection against dengue virus infection.
Evidence: The study design using geographic cluster studies in which high DENV transmission activity has been demonstrated [18].
We showed an association between homotypic NTs and the likelihood of subsequent infection with DENV-1, -2 and -4.

The study design using geographic clusters with matched exposed but uninfected individuals provides definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection against dengue virus infection.
258.Rationale: The passage shows an association between neutralizing antibody titers and protection from infection, but it does not provide definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection. The study design demonstrates a correlation, not causation. Other factors could also contribute to protection, and the study does not rule out other mechanisms. The option overstates the findings by claiming 'definitive evidence' when the study only provides an association.

259.Option: Sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients due to the higher parasite density associated with clinical illness, as shown by the higher PCR prevalence in fever patient surveys.
Evidence: The proportion of individuals with symptoms ranged from 0.0% to 11.4%, averaging 2.8%. This means that between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.

In 13 surveys of clinical malaria suspects conducted in different transmission settings (1997–2010), the prevalence of P. vivax infections determined by LM varied widely from 0.7% to 86.0% of the patients. Although PCR detected more P. vivax infections in some studies, overall it did not have a significantly higher prevalence than LM in fever patients (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, P = 0.278, Fig. 8).
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the vast majority (97.1% on average) of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are asymptomatic. Additionally, in fever patient surveys, PCR did not detect significantly more P. vivax infections than LM. This directly contradicts the claim that sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients. The option misrepresents the data by suggesting a higher prevalence in symptomatic patients, which is not supported by the evidence.

259.Option: The high proportion of asymptomatic sub-microscopic P. vivax infections in the community is primarily due to recent antimalarial drug use, which rapidly reduces parasite density and prevents symptom onset.
Evidence: Only one survey reported that recent antimalarial treatment (<4 weeks prior to survey) was associated with a significant increase in risk of having SM P. vivax infection [33].
259.Rationale: The passage states that only one survey found an association between recent antimalarial treatment and increased risk of sub-microscopic P. vivax infection. This is not sufficient evidence to conclude that antimalarial drug use is the primary cause of the high proportion of asymptomatic sub-microscopic infections. The option overstates the role of antimalarial drugs as the primary factor, which is not supported by the data presented.

259.Option: The use of whole blood for DNA extraction in PCR assays significantly increases the risk of false-positive results in detecting sub-microscopic P. vivax infections, which undermines the reliability of PCR-based surveillance.
Evidence: The average prevalence of SM P. vivax in community surveys determined by PCR using whole blood was significantly higher than that using blood from filter papers (Mann Whitney test, P = 0.004).
259.Rationale: The passage indicates that whole blood extraction results in higher detection rates of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections compared to filter paper, but this is interpreted as increased sensitivity rather than false positives. The option incorrectly frames this as a problem of false positives undermining reliability, which is not supported by the passage. The data suggest improved detection capability, not compromised reliability.

259.Option: The prevalence of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher in areas with high transmission intensity compared to low transmission intensity, as indicated by the higher number of PCR-positive cases in these regions.
Evidence: The negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P<0.001, Deviance  = 7.32, df  = 29). The regression equation describing this relationship is: proportion SM P. vivax  =  exp (−0.162–4.163× LM P. vivax prevalence).
259.Rationale: The passage describes a negative relationship between LM prevalence (a proxy for transmission intensity) and the proportion of sub-microscopic infections. This suggests that sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are more common in areas with lower transmission intensity, not higher. The option incorrectly claims the opposite pattern, which is directly contradicted by the statistical relationship presented in the passage.

259.Option: The relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in the same study settings, and this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity.
Evidence: The average relative proportion of SM was significantly higher in P. vivax infections compared to P. falciparum in the same study (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, Median difference  = 5.2%, P = 0.045, Fig. 5B).

The negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P<0.001, Deviance  = 7.32, df  = 29). The regression equation describing this relationship is: proportion SM P. vivax  =  exp (−0.162–4.163× LM P. vivax prevalence).
259.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of P. falciparum in the same study settings (5.2% median difference, p=0.045). Additionally, the negative relationship between LM prevalence (a proxy for transmission intensity) and the proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections indicates that this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity. This option accurately reflects both the comparative proportion and the transmission intensity relationship described in the passage.

260.Option: The high level of awareness about NTDs among medical and nursing students in Egypt is capable of achieving significant and long-lasting results in controlling these diseases.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage clearly states that student knowledge about NTDs is limited, with only 26% knowing the meaning of NTDs and 5% knowing specific prevention activities. The claim of 'high level of awareness' directly contradicts the evidence presented. The passage does not provide any information about students' ability to achieve long-lasting results in controlling these diseases.

260.Option: The lack of explicit mention of the term 'neglected tropical diseases' (NTDs) in the epidemiology course materials at Cairo University is associated with a significant knowledge gap among medical and nursing students regarding NTDs and their control activities.
Evidence: Neither the term 'neglected tropical disease' nor the abbreviation 'NTDs' was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage establishes a direct correlation between the absence of the term 'NTDs' in course materials and the observed knowledge gaps. The lack of explicit terminology in the curriculum is temporally and logically associated with the low knowledge levels demonstrated by students. The evidence supports an epidemiological inference that curriculum content significantly influences student knowledge.

260.Option: The lack of NTDs in the curriculum is not a significant factor in the low knowledge levels among students, as most students learned about NTDs through social media and mass media.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
The main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio)
The epidemiology course was mentioned as a single source of knowledge by 8 students (4.3%), while it was mentioned with other sources by 6 students (3.3%)
260.Rationale: While the passage mentions that some students obtained knowledge through social media and mass media, the overall knowledge level remains low (26% understanding the meaning of NTDs). The data shows that the formal curriculum (epidemiology course) was a very minor source of knowledge for most students. This supports the inference that the lack of NTDs in the curriculum is indeed a significant factor in the low knowledge levels, contradicting the option's claim.

260.Option: The inclusion of NTDs in the epidemiology course curriculum would likely lead to a significant increase in student willingness to participate in NTDs control activities.
Evidence: More than one third of them (34%) expressed their willingness to participate in these control activities
No statistically significant difference (P value = 0.061) was found between medical and nursing students regarding their willingness to participate in the control activities for NTDs
260.Rationale: The passage shows that willingness to participate in control activities is already present in 34% of students, and there is no evidence that this willingness is related to curriculum content. The option makes a causal claim about curriculum inclusion increasing willingness that is not supported by the data presented. The study does not establish a relationship between curriculum content and willingness to participate.

260.Option: The poor public awareness of NTDs in Egypt is primarily due to insufficient media coverage of control programs, despite adequate knowledge among healthcare students.
Evidence: Nearly half of the students agreed on the poor level of awareness regarding NTDs in Egypt
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence about media coverage of control programs. The claim that healthcare students have 'adequate knowledge' is contradicted by the data showing low knowledge levels among students. The option makes assumptions about media coverage and student knowledge that are not supported by the evidence in the passage.

261.Option: The method is capable of detecting the effect of benznidazole on T. cruzi epimastigotes at concentrations as low as 0.0195 μg/mL, indicating high sensitivity to drug action.
Evidence: The effect of varying amounts of benznidazole on the activity of the parasites was tested and compared to the pattern obtained from the LIT medium with benznidazole and without parasites.

Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 show the curves obtained for the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 4x104 parasites per well, at times of 1, 61, 128 and 204 minutes with six concentrations of benznidazol ranging from 0.0195 to 20μg/mL, and as a comparison, the curves obtained with benznidazole without parasites.

The activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of benznidazole concentration shows curves that fit similar Polynomial regressions at all the tested times (Fig 13) with a decrease of the activity as a function of concentration and a clustering of the values (mean of the clustered low concentration values of 48.39±2.91, see S1 Data for details) of the four time curves up to a concentration of benznidazole of 1.25μg/mL.

The results of Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 suggest that benznidazole produces an instantaneous effect at 1min on T. cruzi that occurs at all the tested concentrations, with a profile that remains almost the same up to at least 61min, as shown by the polynomial tendencies (Fig 13) and the resulting profile of Fig 15.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the method tested varying concentrations of benznidazole from 0.0195 to 20μg/mL and detected effects at the lowest concentration of 0.0195μg/mL. The results show a statistically significant difference in Biospeckle activity between parasite samples with and without benznidazole at this concentration. This directly supports the inference that the method is sensitive enough to detect drug effects at low concentrations.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern is sensitive to changes in parasite motility and aggregation, which may be influenced by drug-induced taxis, suggesting that the method detects early behavioral responses to benznidazole.
Evidence: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites present in the assay well and remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes.

The results of Figs 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 suggest that benznidazole produces an instantaneous effect at 1min on T. cruzi that occurs at all the tested concentrations, with a profile that remains almost the same up to at least 61min, as shown by the polynomial tendencies (Fig 13) and the resulting profile of Fig 15.
261.Rationale: While the passage mentions an instantaneous effect of benznidazole at 1 minute, it does not provide evidence that this effect is due to changes in motility or aggregation, nor does it mention drug-induced taxis. The term 'taxis' refers to directed movement in response to stimuli, which is not discussed in the passage. The inference about behavioral responses requires assumptions not supported by the evidence provided.

261.Option: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites present in the assay well and remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes.
Evidence: The effect of parasite quantity on the activity Biospeckle pattern was evaluated by conducting two experiments (constant density and variable density) and analyzing in each case, the same video with the three approaches for handling of image data.

In the experiment of constant density 5 different test wells had 4x104 parasites/mL but the volume in each well varied from 13.3 to 133μL and the parasite number varied from 0.532 to 5.32x104 parasites per well. As is shown in Fig 2, the three approaches are able to detect an increase in the activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of parasite quantity. Moreover, the plots are nearly linear using the three approaches.

The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways. Fig 11 shows the desiccation in terms of remaining volume, after 0, 55, 120 and 270 minutes have elapsed. In the same Figure two assays (Assay 1 and Assay 2) show the activity of the Biospeckle pattern in separate wells with and without parasites and processed under R/SAGA and Eq (3). The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to parasite quantity in both constant and variable density experiments, and that the pattern remains stable despite desiccation up to 200 minutes. These findings directly support the inference about proportionality and stability.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern remains stable over time because the VDRL plate's flat-bottomed design minimizes optical distortion and prevents evaporation-induced signal drift.
Evidence: The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways. Fig 11 shows the desiccation in terms of remaining volume, after 0, 55, 120 and 270 minutes have elapsed. In the same Figure two assays (Assay 1 and Assay 2) show the activity of the Biospeckle pattern in separate wells with and without parasites and processed under R/SAGA and Eq (3). The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.

The VDRL plate was recommended as a specific and reproducible system by the World Health Organization in 1980 [11]. The design and dimensions of the VDRL flat test wells are adapted to optical microscopes and magnifiers and they are available in most health centers worldwide.
261.Rationale: The passage states that the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to 200 minutes despite desiccation, but it does not attribute this stability to the flat-bottomed design of the VDRL plate. The flat-bottomed design is mentioned as being adapted to optical equipment, but the specific claim about minimizing optical distortion and preventing evaporation-induced signal drift is not supported by the evidence provided.

262.Option: Tungiasis is an important health problem in Vihiga County occasioned by the low economic status of the people affected, and factors that point to poverty contribute to the occurrence of tungiasis in Vihiga County.
Evidence: The poverty levels are high (41%) and the GDP per capita income was reported as US $ 1,103 in 2013 [21].

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).

Tungiasis also referred to as jigger infestation is a neglected tropical disease due to the fact that it typically occurs in populations living in poverty, with poor housing, inadequate sanitation and in close contact with domestic animals akin to other neglected diseases.
262.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence linking low economic status to the occurrence of tungiasis. It explicitly states that the disease is a neglected tropical disease associated with poverty, poor housing, and inadequate sanitation. The study in Vihiga County confirms high poverty levels (41%) and low GDP per capita. The multivariate analysis further shows a strong association between low economic status (monthly income <1000) and tungiasis. These findings directly support the inference that poverty-related factors contribute to the disease's occurrence in the region.

262.Option: The high prevalence of tungiasis among children aged 5–14 years is primarily due to their frequent exposure to infected domestic animals, especially rats, in shared household spaces.
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).
262.Rationale: While the passage does mention an association between having rats in the compound and tungiasis, it does not establish that this is the primary driver of high prevalence among children aged 5–14. The multivariate analysis shows that low economic status and age are the most significant factors. The option makes an unsupported causal claim by asserting that exposure to infected domestic animals is the 'primary' reason for high prevalence in this age group, which cannot be derived from the evidence provided.

262.Option: The lack of knowledge about tungiasis transmission among 45% of participants suggests that community education campaigns should be prioritized to eliminate stigma and improve treatment adherence.
Evidence: When it came to knowledge and practice associated with tungiasis, 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on its transmission.
262.Rationale: The passage states that 45% of participants had no knowledge about tungiasis transmission, but it does not provide evidence about stigma or treatment adherence. The option introduces new concepts (stigma and treatment adherence) that are not mentioned in the passage. While community education campaigns might be a reasonable intervention, the specific claim about eliminating stigma and improving treatment adherence cannot be derived from the evidence provided.

262.Option: The absence of spatial autocorrelation in tungiasis cases suggests that environmental factors such as soil type and elevation are the primary drivers of disease transmission in the study area.
Evidence: Visual investigation of the distribution of cases did not reveal any obvious spatial pattern in terms of the changes in elevation. There is similar distribution in both the low and high altitudes (Fig 2).
The semivariogram revealed no spatial autocorrelation which could indicate that tungiasis prevalence in the study area might be random.
The soil type in the three sub locations did not vary, it was mainly observed to be sandy clay (Fig 3).
262.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that there was no spatial autocorrelation and that the soil type was uniform across the study area. The absence of spatial autocorrelation and uniform soil type actually contradict the claim that environmental factors are the primary drivers. The option misinterprets the evidence by suggesting that the absence of spatial patterns indicates environmental factors are important, when in fact the evidence suggests the opposite.

262.Option: The use of GIS and spatial interpolation techniques such as inverse distance weighting (IDW) allows for accurate prediction of tungiasis hotspots in small-scale endemic areas, enabling targeted public health interventions.
Evidence: GIS analysis was carried out using Arc GIS 10.5 from Esri. The basemap, from the topographical map of the area at a scale of 1:50,000, was projected to the Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) projection system and used to digitize layers i.e. roads, rivers, towns.

The tungiasis case concentration map was produced by interpolating the number of cases recorded per site using the inverse distance weighting (IDW) algorithm tool found in ArcGIS Spatial Analyst tool—a spatial autocorrelation method that interpolates unknown values/cases based on values/cases from nearby known locations [26].
262.Rationale: The passage describes the use of GIS and IDW for mapping tungiasis cases, but it does not provide evidence that these techniques allow for 'accurate prediction' of hotspots. In fact, the study found no spatial autocorrelation, which would be necessary for accurate spatial prediction. The option overstates the capabilities of the methods described in the passage and makes a claim about accuracy that is not supported by the evidence.

263.Option: The increasing dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008, coupled with a stable median age of infection, suggests that the force of infection has declined, leading to fewer infections in children and a higher proportion of symptomatic cases in adults.
Evidence: In the quasi-Poisson model there was no statistically significant yearly increase in age adjusted incidence when all years were included (table 1). Since dengue characteristically produces large epidemics interspersed by many years, these unusually large epidemic years may mask underlying trends in the data. Therefore we also ran the quasi-Poisson model with the two epidemic years of 1998 and 2009 excluded. In this analysis of years 1999-2008, the age adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually (table 1).

The unadjusted median and mean age of notified cases were 23 and 26.3 years respectively, and 53% of cases were male. The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A). Although the average age of notified dengue cases increased over the study period, from a median (mean) age of 20 (22.4) years in 1998 to 24 (26.8) years in 2009, this was not statistically significant after adjusting for changes in the population age structure (figure 2B).
263.Rationale: The passage states that age-adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually from 1999 to 2008. However, the option claims the force of infection has declined, which contradicts the observed increase in incidence. Additionally, the median age of infection did not decrease but rather increased slightly, which is inconsistent with the claim that fewer children are being infected. The passage does not provide evidence to support the claim that the force of infection has declined.

263.Option: Despite a significant increase in age-adjusted dengue incidence in Hanoi from 1999 to 2008, the median age of notified cases did not decrease, suggesting that the force of infection may not have increased and that clinical case reporting patterns may be influenced by factors such as adult susceptibility to symptomatic disease.
Evidence: In the quasi-Poisson model there was no statistically significant yearly increase in age adjusted incidence when all years were included (table 1). Since dengue characteristically produces large epidemics interspersed by many years, these unusually large epidemic years may mask underlying trends in the data. Therefore we also ran the quasi-Poisson model with the two epidemic years of 1998 and 2009 excluded. In this analysis of years 1999-2008, the age adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually (table 1).

The unadjusted median and mean age of notified cases were 23 and 26.3 years respectively, and 53% of cases were male. The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A). Although the average age of notified dengue cases increased over the study period, from a median (mean) age of 20 (22.4) years in 1998 to 24 (26.8) years in 2009, this was not statistically significant after adjusting for changes in the population age structure (figure 2B).
263.Rationale: The passage confirms a significant increase in age-adjusted dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008. However, the median age of notified cases did not decrease, which is consistent with the idea that the force of infection may not have increased. The option correctly suggests that clinical case reporting patterns may be influenced by factors such as adult susceptibility to symptomatic disease, which is supported by the observation that the majority of cases occurred in individuals over 15 years of age.

263.Option: The increase in dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008 is likely due to improved case detection and reporting, as evidenced by the rising proportion of IgM-positive cases over time.
Evidence: ELISA results were available from 3678 cases notified between 2002 and 2009. The proportion of cases with an available ELISA result varied from 68.2% in 2002 to 4.5% in 2009. The overall, proportion of ELISA tests that were anti-dengue virus specific IgM positive was 23.7%, ranging from 13.6% in 2006 to 49.8% in 2003. The lowest annual test positivity rates were observed in 2006 (13.6%), 2007 (17.6%), and 2008 (16.1%).
263.Rationale: The passage indicates that the proportion of cases with available ELISA results decreased over time, from 68.2% in 2002 to 4.5% in 2009. Additionally, the proportion of IgM-positive cases varied significantly, with the lowest rates observed in 2006, 2007, and 2008. This suggests that case detection and reporting may have declined rather than improved over time, contradicting the option's claim.
