278.Option: The lack of neutrophilic infiltrates in antibiotic-treated patients indicates that mycolactone-mediated cytotoxicity prevents the recruitment of neutrophils during early infection.
Evidence: In the other four patients massive cellular infiltration was observed. Three major types of mixed infiltrates, differing in cellular composition, architecture and localisation were found in all four specimens: (i) highly organized epithelioid granulomas of different size and state of differentiation, primarily located in deeper dermal tissue (Figure 3A); (ii) less organized diffuse infiltrates representing the most abundant type, present in all areas of the dermal connective and adipose tissue (Figure 3B); (iii) dense lymphocyte clusters in proximity to vessels, occasionally found in superficial connective tissue (Figure 3C).

In contrast to granulomas, regions of diffuse cellular infiltration contained sparsely distributed elastase+ PMNL (Figure 7A) with sporadic focal clusters near ulcerative and necrotic areas (not shown). CD56+ NK cells showed a similar distribution with even lower cell counts (not shown). Foci of PMNL and NK cells with signs of apoptosis were located within necrotic tissue (Figure 7Ainsert and 7B, respectively).
278.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that neutrophilic infiltrates are absent in antibiotic-treated patients. In fact, it describes the presence of sparsely distributed elastase+ PMNL (neutrophils) in regions of diffuse cellular infiltration. The option incorrectly assumes a complete lack of neutrophilic infiltrates. Additionally, the passage does not establish a direct link between mycolactone-mediated cytotoxicity and the prevention of neutrophil recruitment during early infection. The observed sparsity of neutrophils may be due to other factors not discussed in the passage.

278.Option: The presence of extracellular mycobacterial clusters in the lesion of one antibiotic-treated patient suggests that antibiotic treatment may accelerate the progression of early plaques and oedemas to ulcerative lesions.
Evidence: The excised lesion of one of the five antibiotic treated patients was almost devoid of cellular infiltrates. Only minor superficial vasculitis-associated infiltrates of the dermis consisting of lymphocytes and macrophages/monocytes were found (Figure 2A). Extensive connective tissue necrosis and epidermal hyperplasia was observed (Figure 2A). The adipose regions displayed fat cell ghosts, extensive calcification and massive necrosis, associated with nearly complete absence of intact cell structures (Figure 2B; calcified areas stained purple). While the ulcerative centre of the lesion harboured numerous large clusters of rod-shaped extra-cellular acid fast bacilli (Figure 2C), margins contained only very few small bacterial microcolonies (not shown).
278.Rationale: The passage describes the presence of extracellular mycobacterial clusters in one antibiotic-treated patient, but it does not provide evidence that antibiotic treatment accelerates the progression of early plaques and oedemas to ulcerative lesions. The option makes a causal claim that is not supported by the passage. The presence of extracellular clusters in this patient may be due to other factors, such as individual variability in immune response or treatment resistance, which are not discussed in the passage.

278.Option: A complex and highly organized cellular immune response is crucial for the elimination of a chronic M. ulcerans infection.
Evidence: In the other four patients massive cellular infiltration was observed. Three major types of mixed infiltrates, differing in cellular composition, architecture and localisation were found in all four specimens: (i) highly organized epithelioid granulomas of different size and state of differentiation, primarily located in deeper dermal tissue (Figure 3A); (ii) less organized diffuse infiltrates representing the most abundant type, present in all areas of the dermal connective and adipose tissue (Figure 3B); (iii) dense lymphocyte clusters in proximity to vessels, occasionally found in superficial connective tissue (Figure 3C).

The highest burden of mycobacterial material was found in deep dermal regions (Figure 5A and 5E) with a declining gradient from ulcerative areas towards the excision margins. Accumulations of mycobacterial material were primarily observed in areas of mixed cellular infiltration, but only very rarely in granulomas.

A panel of antibodies specific for leukocyte markers (Table 1) were used for immunohistochemical characterisation of infiltrates. Serial sections revealed that the outer layer of granulomas was mainly composed of CD3+ T lymphocytes (Figure 6A) with CD4+ T cells (Figure 6B) always outnumbering CD8+ T cells (Figure 6C). These lymphocyte belts were interspersed with S100+ dermal dendrocytes (dDCs) (Figure 6D). Occasionally dDCs were present in the centre of granulomas (not shown). Focal clusters of CD20+ B cells appeared at the outer margins of the T lymphocyte layer (Figure 6E). Cytoplasmic CD68+ antigen-presenting cells (APCs) in particular Langhans' giant cells (Figure 6Finsert) and epithelioid macrophages formed the centre of granulomas (Figure 6F). Staining of the membrane protein CD14 confirmed the macrophage/monocyte origin of giant cells (Figure 6G, arrow). Furthermore, large amounts of soluble CD14 were observed within the belt of T lymphocytes (Figure 6G, arrowhead). A great proportion of lymphocytes turned out to be activated as evidenced by their CD45RO+ phenotype (Figure 6H). Variable numbers of Ki67+ (proliferating) cells (Figure 6I) presented a lymphocyte phenotype (Figure 6Iinsert). Neither elastase+ polymorphonuclear neutrophilic leucocytes (PMNL) nor CD56+ natural killer (NK) cells could be detected in granuloma formations.
278.Rationale: The passage provides detailed evidence of a complex and highly organized cellular immune response in four of the five antibiotic-treated patients. This includes the formation of granulomas with specific cellular composition (CD3+ T lymphocytes, CD4+ T cells, CD20+ B cells, CD68+ APCs), the presence of activated lymphocytes (CD45RO+), and proliferating cells (Ki67+). These findings suggest that a robust immune response is associated with the presence of mycobacterial material in areas of mixed cellular infiltration. The contrast with the one patient who had a lack of cellular infiltrates and extensive necrosis supports the inference that a complex immune response is crucial for controlling the infection.

278.Option: The presence of CD14+ macrophages and dendritic cells in necrotic areas suggests that antigen presentation occurs independently of granuloma formation in antibiotic-treated patients.
Evidence: In contrast to granulomas, regions of diffuse cellular infiltration contained sparsely distributed elastase+ PMNL (Figure 7A) with sporadic focal clusters near ulcerative and necrotic areas (not shown). CD56+ NK cells showed a similar distribution with even lower cell counts (not shown). Foci of PMNL and NK cells with signs of apoptosis were located within necrotic tissue (Figure 7Ainsert and 7B, respectively). CD3+ T lymphocyte (Figure 7C) and foamy histiocytes (not shown) were the most prominent cell types of mixed infiltrates in the dermal connective tissue. The CD4+/CD8+ cell ratio varied widely between different regions of a lesion, but usually CD8+ cells were more abundant than CD4+ cells (not shown). Small clusters of CD20+ B lymphocytes were scattered within infiltrates (Figure 7D). Counts of CD14+ macrophages/monocytes were particularly high in adipose tissue and around necrotic areas (Figure 7E). Additionally, vast amounts of sCD14 shed from those cells were revealed (Figure 7Einsert). Dendritic cells could be detected in both epidermis and dermis. The frequency of epidermal CD1a+ Langerhans cells was strongly elevated (Figure 7F). S100+ dDC were distributed throughout diffuse mixed infiltrates with increasing density towards the margins of necrotic areas (Figure 7G). Remarkably, dendritic cell appendices reached into the damaged tissue (Figure 7H). Similar to the findings in granulomas, a large proportion of lymphocytes stained positive for the activation marker CD45RO (Figure 7I) and proliferating Ki67+ cells were scattered throughout the infiltrate (Figure 7J).
278.Rationale: The passage describes the presence of CD14+ macrophages and dendritic cells in necrotic areas, which are associated with antigen presentation. The evidence shows that these cells are present in regions outside of granulomas, particularly in areas of diffuse cellular infiltration and necrotic tissue. The description of dendritic cell appendices reaching into damaged tissue and the presence of activated lymphocytes (CD45RO+) and proliferating cells (Ki67+) supports the inference that antigen presentation occurs independently of granuloma formation. This is further supported by the observation that mycobacterial material is primarily found in areas of mixed cellular infiltration rather than in granulomas.

278.Option: The development of highly organized structures, such as granulomas, is not required for the resorption of destroyed tissue, which is consistent with the observed lack of major inflammatory symptoms in the enrolled patients.
Evidence: The excised lesion of one of the five antibiotic treated patients was almost devoid of cellular infiltrates. Only minor superficial vasculitis-associated infiltrates of the dermis consisting of lymphocytes and macrophages/monocytes were found (Figure 2A). Extensive connective tissue necrosis and epidermal hyperplasia was observed (Figure 2A). The adipose regions displayed fat cell ghosts, extensive calcification and massive necrosis, associated with nearly complete absence of intact cell structures (Figure 2B; calcified areas stained purple). While the ulcerative centre of the lesion harboured numerous large clusters of rod-shaped extra-cellular acid fast bacilli (Figure 2C), margins contained only very few small bacterial microcolonies (not shown).

In the other four patients massive cellular infiltration was observed. Three major types of mixed infiltrates, differing in cellular composition, architecture and localisation were found in all four specimens: (i) highly organized epithelioid granulomas of different size and state of differentiation, primarily located in deeper dermal tissue (Figure 3A); (ii) less organized diffuse infiltrates representing the most abundant type, present in all areas of the dermal connective and adipose tissue (Figure 3B); (iii) dense lymphocyte clusters in proximity to vessels, occasionally found in superficial connective tissue (Figure 3C).
278.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that the development of granulomas is not required for the resorption of destroyed tissue. In fact, the patient with a lack of cellular infiltrates (and presumably no granulomas) had extensive necrosis and calcification, suggesting that the absence of granulomas may be associated with impaired tissue resorption. The option incorrectly assumes that the lack of major inflammatory symptoms is consistent with the absence of granulomas, but the passage does not establish a direct link between granuloma formation and the resorption of destroyed tissue. The presence of granulomas in the other four patients may be related to the immune response and tissue repair processes, which are not discussed in the passage.

279.Option: The rising number of leishmaniasis cases in Austria is primarily driven by increased international travel to endemic regions, particularly among younger populations who are more vulnerable to infection.
Evidence: In the vast majority (93.8%) of the 146 CL cases, PCR was performed on material derived from the skin lesions. This method was highly sensitive with 131 (95.6%) of the 137 tested samples being positive, although for one sample the test result was not documented.

In 83 (56.8%) of the 146 CL cases, the travel history was available and 48 countries or regions were visited prior to development of disease (Fig 3). 39 (81.3%) of the destinations are considered endemic for CL according to the database of the WHO [9], the remaining non-endemic. The countries most frequently reported were Syria (13.3%), Spain (10.8%), Tunisia (8.4%), Afghanistan and Belize (each 6.0%).

The analysis of the 146 CL patients´ characteristics revealed a preponderance of the male sex at a ratio of 2.7 (Table 2). Primarily children, adolescents and adults up to the age of 30 were affected.
279.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that the rising number of leishmaniasis cases in Austria is primarily driven by increased international travel to endemic regions. The data shows that 56.8% of CL cases had a documented travel history to 48 countries, with 81.3% of these destinations being CL-endemic. The most frequently reported countries were Syria, Spain, Tunisia, Afghanistan, and Belize. Additionally, the analysis reveals that the majority of CL cases were in younger populations (children, adolescents, and adults up to age 30), with a male predominance (2.7:1 ratio). These findings directly support the option that increased international travel, particularly among younger populations, is the primary driver of the rising leishmaniasis cases in Austria.

279.Option: The emergence of congenital leishmaniasis in Austria indicates that local sandfly vectors are now capable of transmitting the L. donovani/infantum complex, leading to autochthonous transmission.
Evidence: Congenital leishmaniasis is rare, especially in European countries [5,41]. In 2021, a two-year-old boy presented to the dermatology outpatient clinic with pruritic skin lesions on the arms, legs, cheeks, and buttocks (Fig 4A–4C) that had been present for approximately six months. The boy was born in Austria and had never left the country, and abnormalities at birth or previous illnesses were denied by the parents. Screening of the patient’s asymptomatic family revealed anti-leishmanial IgG antibodies in the mother’s blood, whereas the remaining family members, including the older siblings, aged 6, 8 and 9 years were negative. Only the 5-year-old brother had a borderline-positive serology, but Leishmania DNA was not detectable in his blood. Upon questioning, the mother recalled traveling to the Romanian Black Sea coast, a region endemic for VL [9], in 2015 and 2016, before the birth of the patient and around the birth of his older brother, but denied having had any symptoms indicative of leishmaniasis.
279.Rationale: The passage describes a case of congenital leishmaniasis in Austria but does not provide evidence that local sandfly vectors are now capable of transmitting the L. donovani/infantum complex. The case is explained as a result of vertical transmission from the mother, who had a travel history to an endemic region (Romanian Black Sea coast) before the birth of the child. The mother was asymptomatic but had anti-leishmanial IgG antibodies, indicating a past infection acquired during her travels. There is no evidence in the passage that the infection was acquired locally or that local sandflies are now transmitting the parasite. Therefore, the option suggesting autochthonous transmission via local sandflies is not supported by the evidence provided.

279.Option: The high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in Austrian military personnel indicates that leishmaniasis is now endemic in Austria due to widespread asymptomatic transmission.
Evidence: A total of 154 patients had a borderline-positive serological result, the value of which remains debatable.
Moreover, a relatively high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies was shown in several serological surveillance studies conducted in Austrian military personnel [30–33].

However, reports of serological surveillance studies conducted in healthy, asymptomatic Austrian military personnel [30–33] were excluded, as these individuals were healthy and asymptomatic and could not be conflated with the study cohort, which consists of patients who had presented at their respective physicians with symptoms, pathologies or, at least, because of their fear to suffer from a disease, but not for research-based screening purposes.
279.Rationale: The passage mentions a high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in Austrian military personnel but explicitly states that these individuals were healthy and asymptomatic. The study excluded these serological surveillance studies from the analysis because they were not part of the symptomatic patient cohort. The presence of antibodies alone does not indicate that leishmaniasis is now endemic in Austria or that there is widespread asymptomatic transmission. The passage does not provide evidence of local transmission or a shift in the disease's endemic status in Austria. Therefore, the option suggesting that leishmaniasis is now endemic in Austria due to widespread asymptomatic transmission is not supported by the evidence provided.

279.Option: The increasing detection of L. infantum in cutaneous leishmaniasis cases in Austria suggests that the species has developed greater virulence and transmissibility in human hosts over time.
Evidence: L. infantum was identified in 4 cases (4.0%) and two of these had occurred in 2021 (Fig 2D).

Members of the L. donovani/infantum complex were the cause of 29 (29.0%), members of the group L. tropica/major/mexicana the cause of 19 (19.0%) cases. Members of the L. braziliensis complex were isolated in 12 (12.0%) cases (Fig 2G). One case was attributed to an unidentified member of the L. guyanensis complex [33], in 7 (7.0%) cases no further specification of the species was given (Fig 2H).
279.Rationale: The passage provides data on the increasing detection of L. infantum in cutaneous leishmaniasis cases in Austria but does not provide evidence that the species has developed greater virulence and transmissibility in human hosts over time. The increase in detection could be due to various factors such as increased travel to endemic regions, improved diagnostic methods, or changes in reporting practices. The passage does not mention any changes in the virulence or transmissibility of L. infantum. Therefore, the option suggesting that the species has developed greater virulence and transmissibility is not supported by the evidence provided.

279.Option: The increasing detection of the L. donovani/infantum complex as a cause of cutaneous leishmaniasis in Austria reflects a shift in the epidemiological pattern of the disease, with this species complex now being recognized as a significant cause of CL in non-endemic regions.
Evidence: Members of the L. donovani/infantum complex were the cause of 29 (29.0%), members of the group L. tropica/major/mexicana the cause of 19 (19.0%) cases. Members of the L. braziliensis complex were isolated in 12 (12.0%) cases (Fig 2G).
Particularly since 2016, increasing numbers of members of the L. donovani/infantum complex (Fig 2E) as well as of the group L. tropica/major/mexicana (Fig 2F) were identified as the causative agents.

In 100 of the 146 CL cases information on the causative Leishmania species was available (Fig 2A and S1 Table). The most frequent species identified was L. major in 15 (15.0%) cases and its incidence has increased by 11-fold in 2010–2019 as opposed to 2000–2009 (Fig 2B). L. tropica was the second most common species, detected in 9 (9.0%) cases (Fig 2C). L. infantum was identified in 4 cases (4.0%) and two of these had occurred in 2021 (Fig 2D).
279.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the L. donovani/infantum complex is increasingly being detected as a cause of cutaneous leishmaniasis in Austria. The data shows that members of this complex were responsible for 29.0% of CL cases, and their detection has increased since 2016. This suggests a shift in the epidemiological pattern of the disease, with the L. donovani/infantum complex now being recognized as a significant cause of CL in non-endemic regions like Austria. The increase in detection could be due to factors such as increased travel to endemic regions where this species is prevalent, improved diagnostic methods, or changes in reporting practices. The passage supports the option that there is a shift in the epidemiological pattern of the disease with the L. donovani/infantum complex becoming a more significant cause of CL in non-endemic regions.

280.Option: The attenuated virulence of GFP-PA2G4 transgenic parasites is primarily due to impaired differentiation into amastigotes, as shown by the failure of L. donovani transgenic lines to differentiate under axenic conditions.
Evidence: We monitored the axenic amastigotes 24 and 48 h after differentiation (Fig. 5B). As judged by the expression of the amastigote-specific A2 protein family [31], transgenic parasites carrying GFP-PA2G4 are bona fide amastigotes at 48 h and no differences are observed when compared with WT. This result suggests that the virulence attenuation is potentially due to defects in cell proliferation.
280.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that transgenic L. donovani lines carrying GFP-PA2G4 are able to differentiate into amastigotes under axenic conditions, as evidenced by the expression of amastigote-specific A2 protein. The virulence attenuation is instead attributed to defects in cell proliferation, not differentiation. The option incorrectly attributes the virulence defect to impaired differentiation, which contradicts the evidence presented.

280.Option: LmaPA2G4 functions as a transcriptional repressor in Leishmania, as suggested by its ability to bind double-stranded RNA and the observed defects in gene expression in overexpressing lines.
Evidence: dsRNA-binding domains characterize an expanding family of proteins involved in different cellular processes, ranging from RNA editing and processing to translational control. Human homolog Ebp1 interacts with double stranded RNA [34] and thus it was tempting to study whether GFP-PA2G4 is able to bind a synthetic double stranded RNA Poly (I∶C).

The results from last section suggest a defect in protein translation in transgenic parasites, potentially due to the non-physiological accumulation of intermediates of translation initiation and elongation.
280.Rationale: While the passage confirms that LmaPA2G4 can bind double-stranded RNA, it does not provide evidence that it functions as a transcriptional repressor. The observed defects are in translation (initiation and elongation), not transcription. The option mischaracterizes the biological process affected by LmaPA2G4 overexpression.

280.Option: LmaPA2G4 is a phosphoprotein that is phosphorylated by MPK7, as suggested by its isolation through a phospho-enrichment procedure (IMAC) and its role in regulating translation initiation and elongation.
Evidence: Spot ID 207 was over-represented in GFPK7 (2.97 fold change and p = 0.00056) and excised from the gel and analyzed by MS/MS (Fig. 1). LmjF19.0160 (Tritryp gene ID) is a putative aminopeptidase with a predicted MW of 43 kDa. Recombinant LmjF19.0160 was not phosphorylated in vitro by active GFPK7 using an in vitro kinase assay (Fig. S1). Phosphotransferase activity of recombinant GFPK7 was assessed by phosphorylation of MBP.

The results from last section suggest a defect in protein translation in transgenic parasites, potentially due to the non-physiological accumulation of intermediates of translation initiation and elongation.
280.Rationale: The passage states that LmaPA2G4 was isolated through a phospho-enrichment procedure but also explicitly shows that recombinant LmaPA2G4 was not phosphorylated by MPK7 in vitro. The role in translation is mentioned, but the claim that it is phosphorylated by MPK7 is directly contradicted by the evidence. The option makes an incorrect inference about phosphorylation status.

280.Option: The overexpression of LmaPA2G4 leads to a significant reduction in de novo protein synthesis, which is likely responsible for the observed growth and virulence defects in Leishmania parasites.
Evidence: Percentage of de novo protein synthesis is shown normalized to the controls L. donovani and L. major WT parasites (Fig. 8, lower panel). These data suggest than indeed de novo protein synthesis is greatly reduced in pro- and amastigotes over-expressing LmaPA2G4. Defects in biosynthesis will likely impact cell proliferation and ultimately may be responsible for the phenotype observed.
280.Rationale: The passage directly states that de novo protein synthesis is significantly reduced in parasites over-expressing LmaPA2G4 and explicitly links this reduction to the observed growth and virulence defects. This inference is directly supported by the experimental evidence presented.

280.Option: The essentiality of LmaPA2G4 in L. major is due to its role in ribosome biogenesis, as evidenced by the over-representation of ribosomal proteins and translation initiation factors in the proteomic analysis of GFP-PA2G4 overexpressing parasites.
Evidence: Five spots with significant expression differences in GFP-PA2G4 (p-value<0.005) were selected. Gels were stained with the fluorescent stain SYPRO Ruby and the five spots of interest were excised and identified by MS/MS. Interestingly, the homologs of identified eukaryotic translation initiation factor 5 (LmjF25.0720, 6.1-fold change), 60S ribosomal protein (LmjF29.2460, 5.9-fold change) and 40S ribosomal protein (LmjF28.0960, 5.3-fold change) are implicated in translation initiation and elongation [32].
The overexpression of LmaPA2G4 results in accumulation of intermediates of translation initiation and ultimately leads to growth and virulence defects.
280.Rationale: While the proteomic analysis shows changes in ribosomal proteins and translation factors, the passage does not directly link these changes to ribosome biogenesis. The essentiality of LmaPA2G4 is attributed to its role in translation processes, not ribosome biogenesis specifically. The option makes an inference that is not directly supported by the evidence provided.

281.Option: The lack of association between malaria infection and low birth weight in this study suggests that placental histopathological changes are not clinically relevant in this setting.
Evidence: The babies from both groups were also similar in terms of gestational age at delivery and birth weight (Table 1).
281.Rationale: The passage states that the babies from both infected and uninfected groups were similar in terms of birth weight. However, this does not imply that placental histopathological changes are not clinically relevant. The study found significant placental histopathological changes (e.g., increased barrier thickness and monocyte numbers) in infected women. The lack of association with low birth weight could be due to other factors, but it does not negate the clinical relevance of the observed histopathological changes. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the evidence provided.

281.Option: The increase in placental barrier thickness observed in women with multiple malaria infections may be primarily driven by P. vivax due to its higher prevalence in the region, despite the lack of parasite sequestration in the placenta.
Evidence: The increase in the thickness of the placental barrier observed is at least partially driven by P. vivax, while the increase in the percentage of intervillous monocytes seems independent of it (S1 Fig).
This region presents a higher prevalence of P. vivax infections than P. falciparum infections [27].
281.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the increase in placental barrier thickness is at least partially driven by P. vivax, and the region has a higher prevalence of P. vivax. This supports the inference that P. vivax is a primary driver of the observed placental changes, even in the absence of parasite sequestration. The evidence directly links the prevalence of P. vivax to the histopathological changes, making this a valid conclusion.

281.Option: The higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in women with active malaria infection indicates a systemic shift toward angiogenic activation that may counteract placental barrier thickening.
Evidence: we observed a decrease of peripheral Ang-1 in women with a current infection and a significantly higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in both the total women who had MiP or only the women who had a current infection, relative to the uninfected women (Table 3).

Though the numbers are small, if we segregate current malaria in pregnancy by P. vivax and P. falciparum the data shows that both the decrease of Ang-1 and the increase in the ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 are more closely associated with P. falciparum than with P. vivax (Ang-1 (median [min, max]): Uninfected (n = 88): 12.67 [3.71, 32.16], vivax (n = 4): 9.19 [9.14, 9.85], p = 0.975, falciparum (n = 5): 8.89 [8.56, 9.51], p = 0.098; ratioTie2:Ang-1: Uninfected (n = 88): 0.89 [0.20, 2.56], vivax (n = 4): 0.76 [0.50, 1.51], p = 0.766, falciparum (n = 5): 1.42 [0.86, 2.65], p = 0.096).
281.Rationale: The passage indicates that the higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in women with active malaria infection is associated with a decrease in Ang-1, not an increase in angiogenic activation. The data also suggest that this ratio is more closely associated with P. falciparum than P. vivax. The inference that this ratio indicates a systemic shift toward angiogenic activation that counteracts placental barrier thickening is not supported by the evidence, which instead points to a different mechanism.

281.Option: The elevated levels of IL-10 in the placenta of women with malaria during pregnancy suggest a compensatory anti-inflammatory response that may protect against placental barrier thickening.
Evidence: we observed a decrease of peripheral Ang-1 in women with a current infection and a significantly higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in both the total women who had MiP or only the women who had a current infection, relative to the uninfected women (Table 3).

The women with evidence of a current malaria infection had increased levels of cytokines in the periphery and of IL-10 in the placenta, which was also seen in vivax-only infected women (Table 3 and S2 and S3 Figs).
281.Rationale: The passage mentions elevated levels of IL-10 in the placenta of infected women but does not provide evidence that this is a compensatory anti-inflammatory response protecting against placental barrier thickening. The study found an increase in placental barrier thickness in women with multiple infections, which is not contradicted by the presence of IL-10. Therefore, the inference about a protective role of IL-10 is not supported by the evidence provided.

281.Option: In this area where diagnosis and treatment of malaria are readily available and where the impact of malaria during pregnancy is mild, we were able to detect a link between decreased levels of Ang-1 and an increase in the placental barrier thickness of Plasmodium-infected women.
Evidence: The region where our study was conducted has been described elsewhere. This region presents a higher prevalence of P. vivax infections than P. falciparum infections [27].

we observed a decrease of peripheral Ang-1 in women with a current infection and a significantly higher ratio of Tie-2:Ang-1 in both the total women who had MiP or only the women who had a current infection, relative to the uninfected women (Table 3).

The increase in the thickness of the placental barrier observed is at least partially driven by P. vivax, while the increase in the percentage of intervillous monocytes seems independent of it (S1 Fig).
281.Rationale: The passage does not mention that diagnosis and treatment of malaria are readily available or that the impact of malaria during pregnancy is mild in the study area. The observed link between decreased Ang-1 and increased placental barrier thickness is noted, but the inference about the availability of diagnosis and treatment is not supported by the evidence provided. Therefore, this option includes an unsupported claim and is incorrect.

282.Option: The high cure rates observed in the complete case analysis, particularly for the Milt+PM regimen, suggest that the treatment regimens are more effective in patients with less severe baseline clinical characteristics.
Evidence: In the complete case analysis, cure rates were SDA 95.5% (95% CI 93.9–96.8), AmB+Milt 95.5% (95% CI 92.7–97.5) and Milt+PM 99.6% (95% CI 98.6–99.9) (Table 2).

Although not significantly consistent, patients treated with SDA and Milt+PM (a majority of whom were treated at district hospitals) were younger, more likely to be female, and to present with severe wasting than those treated with AmB+Milt.
282.Rationale: The complete case analysis shows the highest cure rates for Milt+PM at 99.6%, followed by SDA and AmB+Milt at 95.5%. The passage also notes that patients treated with SDA and Milt+PM were more likely to have severe wasting, a marker of clinical severity. This suggests that the high cure rates in these regimens are achieved despite more severe baseline characteristics, not because of them. The inference that treatment effectiveness is higher in patients with less severe baseline characteristics is therefore not supported by the evidence.

282.Option: The use of combination regimens such as AmB+Milt and Milt+PM is associated with a higher risk of treatment failure in patients with prolonged illness duration, as indicated by the higher relapse rates in those with illness lasting 8 weeks or more.
Evidence: Relapse rates varied by drug regimen and were higher for children than for those older than 12 years (Tables 3 and 4). Those with illness that had lasted 8 weeks or less were also more likely to have relapse at 6 months.
282.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that relapse rates were higher for patients with illness duration of 8 weeks or less. This directly supports the inference that prolonged illness duration is associated with lower relapse rates, and by extension, lower treatment failure rates in combination regimens. The statement in the option is the inverse of what the evidence shows, making it correct as an inference based on the data.

282.Option: The high rate of treatment compliance observed in this study, particularly in the Milt+PM and AmB+Milt arms, indicates that the regimens are more feasible in primary health care settings despite the logistical burden of daily visits.
Evidence: A total of 1,761 patients were recruited, 534 (30.3%) children (≤12 years) and 1,227 (69.7%) adults (>12 years).

Patients treated at the district hospital were admitted to a VL ward for the 10 days of treatment, whereas patients enrolled at PHC level were managed on an outpatient basis, returning each day for the injection. Patients who failed to return for ambulatory treatment were actively traced by telephone and, if necessary, in person to ensure maximum compliance.
282.Rationale: The passage does not provide any direct evidence about treatment compliance rates in the Milt+PM and AmB+Milt arms. While it mentions that patients in PHC settings were managed on an outpatient basis with active tracing to ensure compliance, there are no specific data on compliance rates. The inference about high compliance and feasibility in PHC settings is not supported by the evidence provided.

282.Option: The safety profile of the SDA regimen is compromised by the high incidence of anaphylactic reactions and serious adverse events related to AmBisome, particularly in patients with severe wasting and elevated liver enzymes.
Evidence: Serious adverse events were infrequent in all study arms. There were 5 serious adverse events (SAE) in the SDA arm. Anaphylactic reaction occurred during treatment in one patient and was related to the study drug AmBisome. There were four SAEs after completion of treatment and discharge from hospital: one asymptomatic atrial ectopic possibly related to AmBisome and three other SAEs that were unrelated to the study drugs: TB empyema, hospitalization due to dehydration and elevated creatinine, and lower respiratory tract infection. All SAEs resolved completely with no sequela.
ALT levels were higher in the AmB+Milt arm, whereas AST levels were higher in the SDA and AmB+Milt arms than in the Milt+PM arm (Table 1).
282.Rationale: The passage states that serious adverse events were infrequent in all study arms, including the SDA arm. Only one anaphylactic reaction was directly related to AmBisome in the SDA arm, and all SAEs resolved completely. The option incorrectly claims a 'high incidence' of anaphylactic reactions and serious adverse events, which is not supported by the evidence. Additionally, there is no mention of a specific association between SDA and patients with severe wasting or elevated liver enzymes.

282.Option: Despite a range of clinical severity, presentations, and patient demographics, all of the treatments showed excellent safety profiles.
Evidence: Serious adverse events were infrequent in all study arms. There were 5 serious adverse events (SAE) in the SDA arm. Anaphylactic reaction occurred during treatment in one patient and was related to the study drug AmBisome. There were four SAEs after completion of treatment and discharge from hospital: one asymptomatic atrial ectopic possibly related to AmBisome and three other SAEs that were unrelated to the study drugs: TB empyema, hospitalization due to dehydration and elevated creatinine, and lower respiratory tract infection. All SAEs resolved completely with no sequela.

The most common adverse events were gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain) and back pain (Table 5). Adverse events leading to treatment interruption were rare (<1% for all three drug regimens).
282.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that serious adverse events were infrequent across all treatment arms, with only 5 SAEs in the SDA arm and no long-term sequelae. Additionally, adverse events leading to treatment interruption were rare (<1%) for all three regimens. This supports the inference that all treatments showed excellent safety profiles despite varying clinical severity and patient demographics.

283.Option: The guinea pig model of Lujo virus infection exhibits severe, rapidly progressive, and uniformly lethal disease characterized by high viral loads, disseminated organ damage, and hallmarks consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation, despite a lack of significant early pro-inflammatory cytokine upregulation.
Evidence: All animals infected with either the wtLUJV (N = 28 total; full-duration disease monitoring group N = 10; serial euthanasia group N = 18) or with recLUJV (N = 13 total; full-duration disease monitoring group N = 10; serial euthanasia group N = 3) experienced an onset of progressive illness starting approximately 5 days PI.

The illness was characterized clinically by pyrexia, loss of body weight, bilateral, encrusted, light tan-colored ocular discharge, dehydration (as demonstrated by sunken eyes and tacky mucous membranes), ruffled fur, piloerection, lethargy, hematuria, frank external genitourinary hemorrhage (in 1 animal), and eventual moribundity followed rapidly by death (Fig. 1A).
Animals were either found dead (wtLUJV N = 1; recLUJV N = 2) or euthanized when moribund (wtLUJV N = 9; recLUJV N = 8) 11–16 days PI (Fig. 1A).

Within 48 h PI, a dramatic rise in viral load, measured by qRT-PCR and reported as tissue culture infective dose 50 (TCID50) equivalents (eq), was detected in all tissues and fluids analyzed (whole blood: 4.64×102 eq; liver: 1.83×105 eq; spleen: 8.13×104 eq; lung: 6.22×103 eq; kidney: 5.24×103 eq; urine: 2.20×103 eq) (Fig. 2).

Over the duration of the experiment, a temporal pattern emerged, suggesting the liver and spleen as initial sites of high-level viral replication (2–9 days PI), with increasing amounts of virus detected in lungs and kidneys 12 and 14 days PI, and in all terminal cases exceeding 1.0×106 eq.
The most consistently observed lesions among LUJV-infected animals were hepatocyte and myocardial necrosis.

The most severely affected animals developed other histologic lesions, including focal mild cortical cell necrosis in the adrenal gland, hemosiderin-laden macrophages, and erythrophagocytosis in mesenteric lymph nodes (Fig. 6D).

Generally, we saw significant differences in the magnitude of gene fold-changes across tissue types, with lung, kidney, and liver tissues all demonstrating greater fold-changes compared to spleen tissues regardless of the time PI. Broadly, we observed no change or decrease (2 to 5-fold) in several pro-inflammatory mRNA levels (IL-1b, RANTES), and modest up-regulation of TNFa (2 to 7-fold), primarily in kidneys.
283.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of severe, rapidly progressive, and uniformly lethal disease in the guinea pig model. All infected animals developed progressive illness starting around day 5 post-infection, with clinical signs including pyrexia, weight loss, dehydration, and hemorrhage, leading to death between days 11-16. Viral loads were dramatically high across multiple tissues within 48 hours post-infection, with liver and spleen as initial sites of replication. Histological findings showed hepatocyte and myocardial necrosis, along with other lesions consistent with disseminated organ damage. The correct interpretation is that Lujo virus has unique pathogenic properties, not that it is inherently less virulent overall.

This result indicates that the role of ALD1 may not be confined to the ability to establish an infection in either an insect or mammalian host.

Evidence: Skin flushing (including facial flushing, reddening of neck and upper chest), oliguria, arthralgia, periorbital pain and edema, and hypotension appeared in a relatively low rate in HFRS (15–49%), but not in SFTS.
The clinical manifestation and laboratory test results of the 4 misdiagnosed SFTS patients were hard to differentiate from the confirmed HFRS patients (Table 1)
289.Rationale: While the passage notes that these symptoms appear in HFRS but not SFTS, it also states these symptoms appear in only 15-49% of HFRS cases. This low frequency means they cannot reliably exclude SFTS. Furthermore, the passage explicitly states that the 4 misdiagnosed SFTS patients were clinically indistinguishable from HFRS patients, suggesting that even in the absence of these specific symptoms, SFTS can still be misdiagnosed as HFRS. The claim of 'reliable exclusion' is therefore not supported by the evidence presented.

289.Option: The high sensitivity and specificity of the HFRS-IgM and SFTSV-IgM ELISA kits used in this study suggest that routine serological testing in clinical settings could eliminate misdiagnosis of SFTS as HFRS.
Evidence: ELISA results showed that 57.0% (73/128) of patients were seropositive to hantavirus; that among the 55 sera which were IgM negative to hantavirus IgM antibodies, 4 (7.3%) were positive to SFTSV IgM antibodies.
The acute-phase of patients’ sera was tested for anti-hantavirus IgM and anti-SFTSV IgM antibodies with ELISA.
289.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the sensitivity or specificity of the ELISA kits used. It only reports the results of their application in this study. The 57% seropositivity rate for hantavirus and 7.3% for SFTSV in the tested population does not indicate the kits' diagnostic performance characteristics. The claim about 'high sensitivity and specificity' is therefore not supported by the evidence in the passage. Additionally, the study found 4 cases of SFTS among clinically diagnosed HFRS patients, suggesting that even with testing, misdiagnosis can still occur.

289.Option: Because both SFTS and HFRS are caused by viruses in the Bunyaviridae family, they are likely to share similar transmission routes and should be managed with identical public health interventions.
Evidence: SFTSV is transmitted by tick bite and frequently spread from person-to-person [2–4]

Hantaviruses are carried and transmitted by rodents. Humans acquire infection after direct contact with infected rodents or their excreta, which most likely occurs by inhaling virus-contaminated aerosols [18]
289.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that SFTSV is transmitted by tick bites and person-to-person, while Hantavirus is transmitted by rodents and their excreta. These are clearly different transmission routes. The fact that both viruses belong to the same family does not imply similar transmission routes or management strategies. The claim that they 'should be managed with identical public health interventions' is therefore not supported by the evidence in the passage and contradicts the specific transmission information provided.

289.Option: The seasonal patterns of SFTS and HFRS differ, with SFTS primarily occurring in summer and HFRS having two peaks in spring and fall, which may help clinicians distinguish between the two diseases in endemic regions.
Evidence: Temporal distribution by month of disease onset was calculated and no seasonal pattern was observed for the confirmed HFRS patients (Fig 1) and the four SFTS patients occurred in May and June

289.Rationale: The passage states that no seasonal pattern was observed for confirmed HFRS patients in this study. While it mentions that the four SFTS cases occurred in May and June, this is only four cases and insufficient to establish a seasonal pattern. The claim about HFRS having two peaks in spring and fall is not supported by the passage. The evidence provided does not support the conclusion that there are distinct seasonal patterns that can help distinguish between the two diseases.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections compared to other soil-transmitted helminths, likely due to the combined reporting of two distinct species with different drug sensitivities.
Evidence: The latter two species are indistinguishable by microscopic examination of the eggs and are therefore frequently reported collectively as “hookworms”.
the TPMS 9 (albendazole plus ivermectin first round; no treatment second round) [13]
290.Rationale: The passage states that Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale are reported collectively as hookworms due to indistinguishable eggs. However, the passage does not provide evidence that the model's lower accuracy for hookworms is due to different drug sensitivities between the two species. The TPMS9 reference shows that combined treatments are considered, but no evidence is given that species-specific drug sensitivity differences impact model accuracy.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the pharmacological interventions used in the control programmes were less effective against hookworms compared to other soil-transmitted helminths.
Evidence: the impact of diethylcarbamazine on STH is considered absent [1].

When ivermectin with albendazole is provided during the first round of treatment and albendazole or mebendazole is provided during the second round of treatment the impact is similar to TPMS1/TPMS2 with a marginal additional efficacy against hookworm infection [13].
290.Rationale: The passage discusses drug efficacy differences, noting that ivermectin with albendazole provides marginal additional efficacy against hookworms. However, it does not state that pharmacological interventions are less effective against hookworms overall. The passage actually suggests that albendazole and mebendazole are used for hookworm control, and that combined treatments may have marginal additional efficacy, not reduced efficacy.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the standard TPMS used in SM2 were derived from datasets with low baseline prevalence, leading to poor extrapolation at high prevalence levels.

the standard modification becomes active after 5 years of follow up; for lower initial prevalence the standard modification becomes active after 3–4 years of follow up.
290.Rationale: The passage shows that hookworm baseline prevalence was 45% on average, with a range up to 99%, indicating that datasets with high prevalence were included. The standard modification for high prevalence is applied after 5 years, but this is not presented as a limitation of the model. The evidence does not support the claim that TPMS for hookworm were derived from low-prevalence datasets.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the Kato-Katz method used in the studies has poor sensitivity for detecting low-intensity hookworm infections.
Evidence: We understand the poor precision of the diagnostic methods traditionally used to monitor STH control programmes (i.e. Kato-Katz) especially when intensities of infection are low [1].

In general the models preformed best for T. trichiura (43% of predictions within 1 percentage point) and least well for hookworm (only 26% of predictions within 1 percentage point). Linear trend is included.
290.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Kato-Katz has poor precision for low-intensity infections. It also directly compares model performance across parasites, showing that hookworm predictions were least accurate (26% within 1 percentage point) compared to T. trichiura (43% within 1 percentage point). This directly supports the inference that the diagnostic method's poor sensitivity for low-intensity hookworm infections contributes to reduced model accuracy for hookworms.

290.Option: The model's predictive capacity was lower for hookworm infections because the transition probabilities for hookworms were not updated annually, leading to a loss of stationarity over time.
Evidence: The Markov model assumes the “stationarity” of the TP, in the sense that if the intervention remains the same (e.g. albendazole is administered once a year) the TPMS will also remain constant throughout the duration of the control programme.

For example, Fig 1 shows that if 37% of heavy-intensity hookworm infections become light-intensity infections after the first year of preventive chemotherapy, the same proportion (37%) of the remaining heavy-intensity infections will become light-intensity infections after the second year of the intervention.
290.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the model assumes stationarity of transition probabilities, meaning they remain constant if the intervention remains the same. The evidence shows that the model applies the same transition probabilities over time for hookworms, but does not indicate that this assumption is violated or that it leads to reduced accuracy for hookworms specifically.

291.Option: The use of visual analogue scores in the LeishCOM_LCL tool ensures that both clinician and patient perspectives are captured in a reliable and repeatable manner, which enhances the tool's validity for assessing treatment response in LCL.
Evidence: To measure the impact of the treatment on the “skin problem” at the time of assessment a Visual Analogue Score (VAS) ranging from 0–10 (0 = “completely clear skin”, 10 = “severely affected skin”) was described for both investigator (VASi) and patient (VASp) (CRF Section 6.3).
Each line was 10 cm long. Each score is to be allocated according to the nearest whole cm.
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the use of Visual Analogue Scores (VAS) for both investigator (VASi) and patient (VASp) assessments. The VAS is described as a 10 cm line with scores allocated based on the nearest whole cm, which provides a standardized method for capturing both clinician and patient perspectives. This dual-perspective approach directly supports the claim that the tool enhances validity by incorporating both viewpoints in a reliable and repeatable manner.

291.Option: The development of the LeishCOM_LCL tool represents the first standardized and validated approach to assess core outcome domains in localized cutaneous leishmaniasis, enabling consistent evaluation of treatment response across clinical trials and routine care.
Evidence: The overall aim of this work was to adopt a standardized and validated approach to assess agreed clinical aspects of LCL and response to treatment in a measurable manner for use in clinical trials and routine patient care which were then reflected in a practical tool.

The development of a validated scoring system for LCL based on harmonized methodologies would allow assessment of treatment response in routine clinical settings and enable comparison of the efficacy of existing or new drugs as well as novel alternative interventions and would subsequently support meta-analysis.
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the goal was to create a standardized and validated approach for assessing LCL in both clinical trials and routine care. The tool is described as a practical instrument that enables consistent evaluation of treatment response. The mention of harmonized methodologies and the ability to support meta-analysis further supports the claim that this is the first standardized and validated approach for LCL.

291.Option: The LeishCOM_LCL tool was developed through a multi-stakeholder consensus process that included representation from all major LCL-endemic regions, ensuring global applicability and cultural relevance.

291.Rationale: While the passage mentions that the tool was developed with input from experts in Sri Lanka, India, Brazil, and the UK, it does not claim that all major LCL-endemic regions were represented. The option incorrectly generalizes the stakeholder representation to include all major endemic regions, which is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

291.Option: The inclusion of open-ended questions on HRQoL in the LeishCOM_LCL tool allows for the systematic capture of patient-reported outcomes, which is essential for developing a patient-centered approach to LCL management.
Evidence: The impact of the HRQoL had not been previously considered in LCL and therefore two open-ended questions were included as a preliminary step to try and capture the most important aspects for the patient with a view to informing a novel tool at a later date.
The two open-ended questions were “How does your skin problem affect you?” and “What are the three worst aspects of having your skin problem?”
291.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that open-ended questions were included to capture patient-reported outcomes related to health-related quality of life (HRQoL). These questions were designed to systematically gather patient perspectives, which is a key component of a patient-centered approach to LCL management. The evidence directly supports the claim in the option.

291.Option: The LeishCOM_LCL tool enables the assessment of both clinical efficacy and long-term sequelae, which allows for the evaluation of treatment impact on patient quality of life and psychological well-being.
Evidence: The Core Outcome Domains identified during the development and judgment process are given in Table 1.
