The study focuses on seroprevalence patterns and seroconversion rates, but does not establish a direct link between asymptomatic infection and transmission dynamics. The option makes a causal claim about transmission that is not supported by the data presented in the passage.

411.Option: The discrepancy between DAT and rK39 seropositivity is primarily due to differences in test sensitivity, with rK39 being less sensitive in detecting asymptomatic infection.
Evidence: Agreement between the two assays was weak with a Kappa of 0.30 (95% CI 0.28–0.32).
ROC analysis showed that for the rK39 ELISA, 14 percentage points was the value with the highest Youden index.

The alternative cutoff for rK39 ELISA of 23 pp resulted in 206 positive subjects (1.6%). Agreement with results of the DAT improved only marginally when using this higher cut off for rK39 in combination with a cutoff of 1∶3200 for DAT. (Kappa 0.31, 95% CI 0.30–0.33).
411.Rationale: The passage describes weak agreement between DAT and rK39 tests but does not attribute this discrepancy to differences in test sensitivity. The study actually performed ROC analysis to determine optimal cutoffs for both tests. The option makes an unsupported claim about test sensitivity that is not directly stated in the passage. The data show discrepancies but do not provide evidence for the specific explanation given in the option.

411.Option: The stability of DAT titers over time indicates that individuals who have recovered from VL maintain lifelong immunity against reinfection.
Evidence: Average titers among ex-VL cases were clearly higher than those observed among subclinically infected subjects. The median DAT titer was >25,600 among ex-VL cases and 3,200 among subclinically infected; for rK39 the median titers were 33 pp and 18 pp respectively (figure 3 and 4). Whereas rK39 titers appeared to decline over time, DAT titers were fairly stable.
The decline in rK39 titers was marginally statistically significant (p = 0.045).
411.Rationale: The passage notes that DAT titers are stable over time but does not make any claims about lifelong immunity. The stability of DAT titers could be due to persistent antigenemia rather than immunity. The option makes an unsupported inference about immunity that is not directly stated in the passage. The data on titer stability alone cannot confirm lifelong immunity against reinfection.

411.Option: The age-related increase in sero-conversion rates reflects a higher susceptibility to initial infection among older individuals due to age-related immune decline.
Evidence: The incidence of sero-conversion increased with age from 1.9% in the 2–9 years old to 7.0% in those aged 70 and above, chi squared for trend was highly significant (p<0.001) (table 2).

We modeled DAT and rK-39 positivity and the probability of active VL and sero-conversion against age as independent variable. The probability for being a VL case was highest at 25 years, with a steep decline from that age onwards. For sero-positivity and sero-conversion there was a steady increase with age, until 59 years for sero-conversion, until 67 years for rK39 positivity and beyond that age for DAT positivity (figure 5).
411.Rationale: The passage shows an age-related increase in sero-conversion rates but does not provide evidence that this is due to age-related immune decline. The option makes an unsupported causal claim about immune function that is not discussed in the passage. The data show a pattern but do not establish the mechanism proposed in the option.

412.Option: The high abundance of Aedes vexans mosquitoes in 1987 and 2003, as predicted by the model, suggests that this species alone is sufficient to trigger Rift Valley fever outbreaks in Northern Senegal.
Evidence: The two major RVFV circulation events in northern Senegal and southern Mauritania were recorded in 1987 [25] and 2003 [28]. For these two years the model predicted high ISA values of Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations.

The Index of Simultaneous Abundance (ISA) expresses the synchronicity of the Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations and higher values are obtained when the two mosquito populations are both abundant at the same time.
412.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the two major RVFV events in 1987 and 2003 coincided with high ISA values, which measure the synchronicity of both Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations. The option incorrectly claims that Aedes vexans alone is sufficient, but the evidence shows that the model links outbreaks to the simultaneous abundance of both species. The ISA index specifically measures their synchronicity, not the abundance of one species alone.

412.Option: RVF outbreaks in Northern Senegal are more closely associated with the timing and pattern of rainfall that supports the simultaneous abundance of both Aedes vexans and Culex poicilipes mosquitoes than with total annual rainfall.
Evidence: The model shows that the peak of abundance of Ae. vexans populations generally occurs before the peak of Cx. poicilipes populations, depicting Aedes-before-Culex population cycles. Variations of the ISA reveal the variations in the temporal lag between Ae. vexans and Cx poicilipes populations.

The two major RVFV circulation events in northern Senegal and southern Mauritania were recorded in 1987 [25] and 2003 [28]. For these two years the model predicted high ISA values of Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations.

The Index of Simultaneous Abundance (ISA) expresses the synchronicity of the Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations and higher values are obtained when the two mosquito populations are both abundant at the same time.
412.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the model links RVF outbreaks to the timing and pattern of rainfall that supports the simultaneous abundance of both mosquito species. The ISA index, which measures their synchronicity, is explicitly associated with the two major RVFV events in 1987 and 2003. The study also notes that the model's predictions are driven by rainfall data, and the ISA reflects the temporal dynamics of both species rather than total annual rainfall. This supports the conclusion that the timing and pattern of rainfall leading to synchronized mosquito populations is more critical than total rainfall.

412.Option: The model's failure to simulate high Ae. vexans abundances in 1991 and 1996 suggests that Rift Valley fever outbreaks in Northern Senegal are primarily driven by human behavior rather than environmental factors.
Evidence: The comparison of observed and simulated mosquito abundances from 1991 to 1996 confirmed the capacity of the model to assess the inter-annual variability of Cx. poicilipes populations (Figure 4). For instance, the year of highest abundance of Cx. poicilipes observed during this six years period (1993) was clearly identified by the model. However, it failed to simulate the high abundances of Ae. vexans populations observed in 1991 and 1996 (Figure 4), suggesting that the model would only detect very high inter-annual variations in Ae. vexans abundances, like between the years 2002 and 2003.
412.Rationale: The passage states that the model failed to simulate high Ae. vexans abundances in 1991 and 1996 but does not attribute this to human behavior. The failure is interpreted as the model's inability to detect very high inter-annual variations in Ae. vexans, not as evidence that human behavior drives RVF outbreaks. The option introduces an unsupported causal claim about human behavior that is not present in the passage.

412.Option: The co-occurrence of Aedes vexans and Culex poicilipes populations in high densities during the rainy season is a necessary condition for Rift Valley fever transmission, regardless of the timing of rainfall or the presence of transhumant herds.
Evidence: The two major RVFV circulation events in northern Senegal and southern Mauritania were recorded in 1987 [25] and 2003 [28]. For these two years the model predicted high ISA values of Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations.

The Index of Simultaneous Abundance (ISA) expresses the synchronicity of the Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations and higher values are obtained when the two mosquito populations are both abundant at the same time.
412.Rationale: The passage links RVF outbreaks to the co-occurrence of both mosquito species but does not claim this is a 'necessary condition regardless of the timing of rainfall or the presence of transhumant herds.' The study explicitly models mosquito population dynamics in relation to rainfall and pond dynamics, and the ISA index is derived from rainfall-driven patterns. The option incorrectly generalizes the findings by removing the context of rainfall and livestock, which are central to the study's conclusions.

412.Option: The lack of correlation between total rainfall and RVF outbreaks in West Africa indicates that rainfall is not a relevant factor in predicting Rift Valley fever in Northern Senegal.
Evidence: In West Africa, there is strong evidence that the disease is endemic [18]: different RVF outbreaks were reported in ruminants since the severe outbreak in the Senegal River basin in 1987 [25], [26], [27], [28], and RVFV was isolated from mosquitoes [21], [29] (Figure 1a). However, using a statistical approach, the correlation found in East Africa is not valid in the semi-arid regions of West Africa [30], [31] where the drivers of RVFV transmission dynamics remain poorly understood. There, temporary water bodies (ponds) constitute the main oviposition sites of different mosquito species [32], [33] and mosquito population dynamics are assumed to mainly depend on water availability and on pond dynamics, themselves driven by rainfall [34].

The model was run over a 63-year period, from 1961 to 2003, using rainfall historical records provided by the meteorological station of Linguère. As output, we considered the dynamics of each mosquito species expressed in relative values, as well as the product of the two temporal series. The latter index expresses the synchronicity of the Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations and higher values are obtained when the two mosquito populations are both abundant at the same time. It is subsequently referred as the Index of Simultaneous Abundance (ISA).
412.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that in West Africa, mosquito population dynamics depend on water availability and pond dynamics driven by rainfall. The model itself was driven by rainfall data to predict mosquito populations and RVF outbreaks. The option incorrectly claims that rainfall is not a relevant factor, but the study clearly demonstrates that rainfall is a key driver of mosquito population dynamics and, by extension, RVF transmission in Northern Senegal.

413.Option: The lack of consistent association between arbovirus notifications and hospitalizations for chronic non-communicable diseases suggests that arboviral infections do not exacerbate pre-existing conditions in vulnerable populations.
Evidence: While there is evidence that the risk of severe dengue might be related to non-communicable comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, hypertension, respiratory, cardiovascular or renal diseases) [13–15], whether DENV or other arboviral infections may exacerbate underlying conditions and lead to excess hospitalizations for noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) remains unknown.
Chikungunya and dengue incidence were not associated with any indirect cause of hospitalization.
At the municipality level, we found no increases in all-cause hospitalization rates associated to arboviruses notifications.
413.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that whether arboviral infections exacerbate underlying conditions and lead to excess hospitalizations for NCDs remains unknown. The study found no association between chikungunya and dengue incidence with indirect causes of hospitalization, but this does not prove that arboviruses do not exacerbate pre-existing conditions. The lack of evidence is not evidence of absence. The option makes a definitive claim that cannot be supported by the study's findings.

413.Option: The association between Zika notifications and hospitalizations for inflammatory polyneuropathy, including Guillain-Barré Syndrome, suggests that Zika infection may trigger neurological complications in a significant proportion of infected individuals.
Evidence: Zika incidence was associated with small increases in hospitalizations for endocrine, nutritional, and metabolic diseases (RR 1.0003, 95% Crl 1.0001–1.0004), diabetes (RR 1.0004, 95% Crl 1.0002–1.0006) and inflammatory polyneuropathy (including Guillain-Barré) (RR 1.0082, 95% Crl 1.0033–1.0133) (see Table 2).
413.Rationale: The study found an association between Zika notifications and hospitalizations for inflammatory polyneuropathy, but the relative risk increase is very small (1.0082) and the option claims this suggests Zika may trigger neurological complications in a 'significant proportion' of infected individuals. The data does not support this interpretation. A small relative risk increase does not imply a significant proportion of infected individuals experience complications. The option overstates the study's findings.

413.Option: The co-circulation of dengue and chikungunya in the same communities is likely due to shared environmental risk factors rather than direct viral interactions.
Evidence: Dengue, Zika, and chikungunya are arthropod-borne viruses (arboviruses), primarily transmitted by Aedes spp. mosquitoes.
A combination of rapid urban growth, sustained poverty, and warming climates can support unrestrained mosquito proliferation and create the requisite conditions for arboviral outbreaks [1–3].

With almost 85% of its population residing in high-density urbanized settings, stark social inequalities including disparities in housing and sanitation infrastructure [4], and climatic conditions and environmental features (e.g., open water reservoirs) that support year-round breeding of Aedes spp. mosquitoes in much of the country [5], Brazil is uniquely susceptible to arbovirus transmission [6,7].
413.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that dengue and chikungunya co-circulate in the same communities and attributes this to shared environmental risk factors (Aedes mosquitoes, urban growth, poverty, climate). However, the option makes a definitive claim about the cause being 'shared environmental risk factors rather than direct viral interactions' which is not addressed in the study. The study does not investigate whether direct viral interactions occur, so this conclusion cannot be drawn from the evidence provided.

413.Option: The high levels of underreporting in dengue notifications likely led to an underestimation of the true association between arbovirus outbreaks and hospitalization rates in Brazil.
Evidence: Evidence from an enhanced surveillance study investigating DENV underreporting in Salvador, Bahia, between 2009 and 2011 indicates that approximately only 1 in 20 DENV-positive persons who presented to a public emergency unit with acute febrile illness were reported to the Brazilian Information System for Notifiable Diseases (Sistema de Informação de Agravos de Notificação, SINAN) [9].
The study used notifications for confirmed dengue, Zika, and chikungunya cases from SINAN.
413.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of significant underreporting of dengue cases (only 1 in 20 cases reported). Since the study used notification data from SINAN to analyze associations with hospitalization rates, this underreporting would indeed lead to an underestimation of the true association between arbovirus outbreaks and hospitalization rates. This is a valid epidemiological inference based on the evidence provided.

413.Option: Increases in dengue and chikungunya notifications were associated with small increases in arbovirus-related hospitalizations, but no consistent association was found with all-cause or other specific indirect causes of hospitalization.
Evidence: Dengue notifications were associated with a small increase in dengue-related hospitalizations (RR 1.0029, 95% Crl 1.0027–1.003) and similar increases in non-haemorragic (RR 1.0029, 95% Crl 1.0027–1.003) and haemorrhagic dengue hospitalizations (RR 1.0029, 95% Crl 1.0019–1.0034) (Table 1).

Chikungunya notifications, on the other hand, were associated with overall increases in dengue (RR 1.0044, 95% Crl 1.0037–1.0051), and arthropod-borne viral fevers and viral haemorrhagic fevers hospitalizations (RR 1.0067, 95%Crl 1.0034–1.0111).
At the municipality level, we found no increases in all-cause hospitalization rates associated to arboviruses notifications.
Chikungunya and dengue incidence were not associated with any indirect cause of hospitalization.
413.Rationale: The study explicitly found that increases in dengue and chikungunya notifications were associated with small increases in arbovirus-related hospitalizations, but no consistent association with all-cause or other specific indirect causes of hospitalization. This is a direct summary of the study's findings and represents a valid epidemiological inference.

414.Option: The similar duration of hospitalization between the two antivenom groups indicates that both treatments have equivalent clinical impact on patient recovery time.
Evidence: Duration of hospitalization was similar in the two groups (Table 3).
414.Rationale: The passage states that duration of hospitalization was similar between the two groups, but this does not necessarily indicate equivalent clinical impact on patient recovery time. The study's primary outcome was restoration of coagulability, not overall recovery time. The similar duration of hospitalization could be influenced by other factors unrelated to the antivenoms' clinical impact on recovery time, such as hospital protocols or patient discharge criteria. The passage does not provide evidence that the antivenoms have equivalent clinical impact on recovery time.

414.Option: The higher incidence of severe early antivenom reactions with ET-Plus compared to ET-G suggests that the antivenom's broader spectrum of activity may be associated with increased immunogenicity.
Evidence: ET-Plus caused one or more reactions on more occasions than ET-G [50/194 (25.8%) and 39/206 (18.9%) respectively [RR 1.36 one-sided 95% CI upper limit 1.86; P = 0.06]. Among the patients who reacted to ET-Plus and ET-G, 21 (10.8%) and 11 (5.3%) respectively experienced bronchospasm or gastrointestinal symptoms defined as 'severe' [RR 2.03 one-sided 95% CI upper limit 3.66; P = 0.03] (Table 3).
414.Rationale: The passage states that ET-Plus caused more severe early antivenom reactions than ET-G, but it does not provide evidence that this is due to the antivenom's broader spectrum of activity. The passage does not mention the spectrum of activity of either antivenom, nor does it suggest a relationship between the spectrum of activity and immunogenicity. The inference about the broader spectrum of activity being associated with increased immunogenicity is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

414.Option: The effectiveness of ET-Plus and ET-G in correcting haemostatic effects of E. ocellatus envenoming was demonstrated by the permanent restoration of blood coagulability within six hours of initial antivenom administration, with no recurrence of incoagulability observed after 48 hours.
Evidence: Coagulopathy response (judged by 20WBCT) - blood coagulability was permanently restored within six hours of the initial dose in 161 of 194 (83.0%) patients given three vials of ET-Plus and in 156 of the 206 (75.7%) patients given one vial of ET-G (Table 2; RR 1.10 one-sided 95% CI lower limit 1.01; P = 0.05).

Recurrent blood incoagulability after initial restoration of coagulability occurred in 24 cases, 7 after ET-Plus and 17 after ET-G (Table 3). As per protocol, rescue treatment was given to patients whose blood was incoagulable 24 hr after starting antivenom treatment (Table 3). In all 400 patients, coagulopathy had resolved permanently within 60 hours of starting antivenom and there were no fatalities (Table 3).
414.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that both ET-Plus and ET-G were effective in correcting the haemostatic effects of E. ocellatus envenoming, as indicated by the permanent restoration of blood coagulability within six hours of initial antivenom administration. The passage also states that there was no recurrence of incoagulability observed after 48 hours. This directly supports the inference that the antivenoms were effective in correcting the haemostatic effects of the venom.

414.Option: The absence of fatalities in the trial population indicates that E. ocellatus envenoming is not a life-threatening condition when treated with antivenom.
Evidence: In all 400 patients, coagulopathy had resolved permanently within 60 hours of starting antivenom and there were no fatalities (Table 3).
414.Rationale: The passage states that there were no fatalities in the trial population, but this does not indicate that E. ocellatus envenoming is not a life-threatening condition. The passage provides context that untreated case fatality rates for E. ocellatus envenoming can exceed 10-20%. The absence of fatalities in the trial population is likely due to the effectiveness of the antivenoms, not because the condition is not life-threatening. The inference that E. ocellatus envenoming is not a life-threatening condition is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

414.Option: The lower recurrence rate of coagulopathy with ET-Plus compared to ET-G suggests that the antivenom's higher dose is more effective in preventing re-bleeding than in neutralizing venom toxins.
Evidence: Recurrent blood incoagulability after initial restoration of coagulability occurred in 24 cases, 7 after ET-Plus and 17 after ET-G (Table 3).
414.Rationale: The passage states that the recurrence rate of coagulopathy was lower with ET-Plus compared to ET-G, but it does not provide evidence that this is due to the antivenom's higher dose being more effective in preventing re-bleeding than in neutralizing venom toxins. The passage does not mention the mechanism of action of either antivenom, nor does it suggest that the lower recurrence rate is due to the higher dose being more effective in preventing re-bleeding. The inference about the higher dose being more effective in preventing re-bleeding is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

415.Option: The presence of eosinophilia in sub-Saharan immigrants is primarily due to filariasis and schistosomiasis, which are strongly associated with mitral valve thickening and early endomyocardial fibrosis.
Evidence: The etiological diagnosis was filariasis in 13 cases, schistosomiasis in eight cases, coexistence of filariasis and schistosomiasis in four cases, a geohelmintic disease in seven cases, and indeterminable in five people.

a significant association was found only between thickening of the mitral valve and the presence of eosinophilia, with the thickness of the mitral valve being significantly greater in cases with eosinophilia than in those without eosinophilia.
415.Rationale: The passage states that filariasis and schistosomiasis were among the causes of eosinophilia, but they were not the primary causes. Geohelmintic diseases also contributed significantly. While there was a significant association between eosinophilia and mitral valve thickening, the passage does not establish a strong association with early endomyocardial fibrosis. The option overstates the role of filariasis and schistosomiasis as primary causes and their association with endomyocardial fibrosis, which is not supported by the evidence.

415.Option: Eosinophilia related to helminth infection contributes to the asymptomatic phases of endomyocardial lesions, regardless of the causative parasite.
Evidence: The objectives of this study were to i) evaluate cardiac involvement in asymptomatic sub-Saharan immigrants with eosinophilia; ii) relate the presence of lesions with the degree of eosinophilia; and iii) study the relationship between cardiac involvement and the type of causative parasite.

a significant association was found only between thickening of the mitral valve and the presence of eosinophilia, with the thickness of the mitral valve being significantly greater in cases with eosinophilia than in those without eosinophilia.
415.Rationale: The study specifically evaluated cardiac involvement in asymptomatic individuals with eosinophilia and found a significant association between eosinophilia and mitral valve thickening, a form of endomyocardial lesion. The study also aimed to assess the relationship between cardiac involvement and the type of causative parasite, but the results showed no significant association between the type of helminth and valve involvement. This supports the inference that helminth-related eosinophilia contributes to asymptomatic endomyocardial lesions regardless of the specific parasite.

415.Option: Asymptomatic sub-Saharan immigrants with eosinophilia are at high risk of progressing to symptomatic endomyocardial fibrosis due to the high prevalence of parasitic infections in this population.
Evidence: The objectives of this study were to i) evaluate cardiac involvement in asymptomatic sub-Saharan immigrants with eosinophilia; ii) relate the presence of lesions with the degree of eosinophilia; and iii) study the relationship between cardiac involvement and the type of causative parasite.

There was no statistical association between the degree of eosinophilia and the presence of valve involvement, or between the type of helminth responsible for eosinophilia and valve involvement, as shown in Table 5.
415.Rationale: The study evaluated asymptomatic individuals and found no evidence of progression to symptomatic endomyocardial fibrosis. The passage does not mention any progression to symptomatic disease, and the results showed no significant association between the degree of eosinophilia or the type of helminth and valve involvement. The option makes an unsupported claim about high risk of progression to symptomatic disease.

415.Option: The severity of eosinophilia directly correlates with the extent of mitral valve thickening and the risk of developing endomyocardial fibrosis in asymptomatic individuals.
Evidence: In the study group, the degree of eosinophilia was mild in 26 (70%), moderate in seven (22%), and severe in four (8%) patients.

There was no statistical association between the degree of eosinophilia and the presence of valve involvement, or between the type of helminth responsible for eosinophilia and valve involvement, as shown in Table 5.
415.Rationale: The study explicitly states there was no statistical association between the degree of eosinophilia and the presence of valve involvement. The option claims a direct correlation that is not supported by the data presented in the passage.

415.Option: Eosinophil-derived neurotoxin (EDN) levels are significantly higher in patients with mitral valve thickening, indicating that EDN is a key mediator in the development of early cardiac lesions.
Evidence: The serum concentration of EDN was determined in 29 patients with eosinophilia, 28 of whom had high values (>66.6 ng/mL). However, there were no significant differences (p = 0.46) in the serum concentration of this protein between patients with valve involvement (115.5 ng/mL, SD: 36.6) and those without it (129.1 ng/mL, SD: 53.8).
415.Rationale: The passage states that there were no significant differences in EDN levels between patients with and without valve involvement. The option incorrectly claims that EDN levels are significantly higher in patients with mitral valve thickening and that EDN is a key mediator, which is not supported by the data.

416.Option: The low awareness about NTDs among attendees is primarily due to the lack of access to media platforms and the limited presence of health education in rural communities, which are key barriers to community engagement.
Evidence: A total of 181 (63.3%) of the attendees were aware of ongoing NTD control programmes in schools and communities.
A high proportion 246 (83.4%) of the attendees have not taken or do not know anyone that has taken PC-NTD drugs/medicines donated in schools or communities.
Majority of the attendees 218 (76.5%) have not received health educational messages on NTDs before.
Only 5 (8.3%) of the attendees think there is enough awareness about NTDs.
416.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of low awareness about NTDs but does not specifically attribute this to lack of access to media platforms or limited health education in rural communities. The option introduces specific causal factors (media access and rural health education) that are not directly supported by the passage. The passage mentions that attendees were recruited from an event in Ekiti State and notes limitations in the study's sampling methodology, but it does not provide evidence about media access or rural health education as primary causes of low awareness.

416.Option: The role of residential proximity to health facilities had been sufficiently discussed by [27], as such our findings might have been influenced by the residential proximity of attendee’s to areas where MAM were taking place.
Evidence: This study is a rapid assessment of attendees’ awareness during the maiden World NTD Day event in Ekiti State.

The study has the following limitations; (1) the convenient sampling methodology employed in recruiting study participants contributed to the low sample size and restricted sampling frame, (2) to avoid interfering with attendee’s participation in the event, data collectors do not have enough time to engage and probe attendees adequately, which might have led to the non-response rate recorded for some variables in the questionnaire (3) this study did not capture more specific information on attendee mobility and residential status that could provide more explanations to the low awareness about PC-NTD control programme observed.
416.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the study did not capture information on attendee residential status, which is a key factor mentioned in the option. The option references a study [27] that is not mentioned in the passage body. The passage only discusses limitations of the study, including the lack of data on residential status, but does not provide evidence about the role of residential proximity to health facilities in influencing the findings. The option introduces external information not present in the passage.

416.Option: Public knowledge and awareness about NTDs control programme and promotional activities among attendees of the maiden World NTD Day event in Ekiti State, Nigeria is low.
Evidence: Majority of the attendees, 167 (54.8%) have not heard about NTDs before.
A total of 181 (63.3%) of the attendees were aware of ongoing NTD control programmes in schools and communities.
A high proportion 246 (83.4%) of the attendees have not taken or do not know anyone that has taken PC-NTD drugs/medicines donated in schools or communities.
Majority of the attendees 218 (76.5%) have not received health educational messages on NTDs before.
Only 5 (8.3%) of the attendees think there is enough awareness about NTDs.
416.Rationale: The passage provides multiple direct pieces of evidence supporting this inference. It states that over half of the attendees had not heard about NTDs before, most had not taken or knew anyone who had taken PC-NTD drugs, and the vast majority had not received health educational messages on NTDs. These findings directly support the conclusion that public knowledge and awareness about NTD control programs and promotional activities is low among attendees of the event.

416.Option: Sustained malaria control programme have been in existence for quite a longer period, and they have more field presence for interventions delivery such as distribution of insecticide treated bednets and intermittent preventive treatment to endemic regions than there are for NTDs control [33]. These might have contributed to more increased awareness for malaria control than most NTDs [14,34].
Evidence: The study is a rapid assessment of attendees’ awareness during the maiden World NTD Day event in Ekiti State.

The study has the following limitations; (1) the convenient sampling methodology employed in recruiting study participants contributed to the low sample size and restricted sampling frame, (2) to avoid interfering with attendee’s participation in the event, data collectors do not have enough time to engage and probe attendees adequately, which might have led to the non-response rate recorded for some variables in the questionnaire (3) this study did not capture more specific information on attendee mobility and residential status that could provide more explanations to the low awareness about PC-NTD control programme observed.
416.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about malaria control programs or their comparison to NTD control programs. The option introduces external information about malaria control programs and their potential impact on awareness levels, which is not supported by the passage body. The passage focuses solely on NTDs and does not make any comparisons to malaria control efforts.

417.Option: The program’s community awareness campaigns, including radio spots and street plays, were the primary drivers of reduced stigma and increased social inclusion among patients.
Evidence: Community members’ perceptions of the lymphedema management program focused more on the community awareness campaigns which occurred during the program as well as the distribution of the patient kits.
Through Palla (Folk Dance) I learn how to keep the environment clean and use of mosquito net to prevent lymphedema.
417.Rationale: While the passage mentions community awareness campaigns and their role in increasing knowledge, it does not establish that these campaigns were the *primary* drivers of reduced stigma. The evidence shows that awareness campaigns were part of the program, but the passage does not compare their impact to other components (like hygiene education or direct patient care) to determine which was most influential. The claim about 'primary drivers' is an overstatement not supported by the evidence.

417.Option: The program's success in reducing acute episodes and improving work productivity has led to a significant increase in household income for patients and their families.
Evidence: Patients credited the program with changing their lives through knowledge and skills development, decreased acute episodes, and increased work productivity.
I am now able to work.
417.Rationale: The passage indicates that the program improved work productivity and reduced acute episodes, but it does not provide evidence of a 'significant increase in household income.' While one patient stated they were now able to work, there is no data on actual income changes or economic metrics. The inference about increased household income requires assumptions not supported by the evidence presented.

417.Option: The community-based lymphedema management program has contributed to a reduction in stigma associated with lymphedema, as evidenced by increased social acceptance and inclusion of patients in community life.
Evidence: Patients generally emphasized that their communities were accepting of them and did not obstruct their participation in community events.
The family and community members also appreciate the work of [the program].
Previously people disliked lymphedema patients but now there is no discrimination, people are aware through television, radio about the disease.
417.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of reduced stigma and increased social inclusion. Patients reported greater community acceptance, and community members acknowledged awareness campaigns as contributing to this change. These statements directly support the inference that the program has reduced stigma and increased social inclusion.

417.Option: The program has led to a complete eradication of traditional beliefs about the causes of lymphedema in the community, replacing them entirely with biomedical explanations.
Evidence: Participants in two of the four community member focus groups mentioned that lymphedema was hereditary, but no disagreement was voiced when others mentioned mosquito transmission causing LF.
Alternative explanations were generally voiced alongside responses that emphasized program messages.
417.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that traditional beliefs about the causes of lymphedema (e.g., hereditary causes, dietary factors) persisted alongside biomedical explanations. The program did not 'completely eradicate' traditional beliefs, as evidenced by continued references to these beliefs in the community. The claim of 'complete eradication' is therefore incorrect.

417.Option: The lymphedema management program has significantly improved the mental health of patients by eliminating feelings of isolation and emotional distress.
Evidence: Patients expressed feelings of social isolation living with lymphedema.
My family members don’t take meals with me. I have to eat separately. This is really hurting me.
Patients generally emphasized that their communities were accepting of them and did not obstruct their participation in community events.
417.Rationale: While the passage indicates some reduction in stigma and increased social acceptance, it does not claim that the program 'eliminated' feelings of isolation and emotional distress. Some patients still reported social isolation and emotional pain, and the evidence does not support the claim of a 'significant improvement in mental health' or 'elimination' of distress. The inference is therefore not fully supported by the evidence.

418.Option: The predominance of the Karp genotype of O. tsutsugamushi in Quang Nam province may explain the absence of rash in patients with scrub typhus, as this genotype is associated with lower rash frequency compared to other genotypes.
Evidence: The majority (9/14; 64.4%) of contemporary O. tsutsugamushi genotypes were related to the Karp strain, with a percentage nucleotide identity (PNI) ranging from 97% to 100% in comparison to Karp strain references (Tables 2 and 3).
Two (2/14; 14.2%) sequences demonstrated the highest similarity to the Kawasaki strain, with a PNI ranging from 90% to 93% when compared to the Kawasaki reference strain GQ332755.
The QNamMH_15S_VN sequence (1/14; 7.1%) was most closely related to the TA716 strain U19905 (PNI: 91%).

Two remaining sequences (2/14; 14.2%) were related to Gilliam, in which one sequence was similar to the JG-v strain HQ718460 (PNI: 99%), and another one was similar to the TG-v strain GQ332758 (PNI: 100%).
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the Karp genotype is the most prevalent in Quang Nam province (64.4% of isolates). It also explicitly states that no patient was observed to have a rash. The genetic analysis shows that the Karp genotype is the dominant strain in this population. While the passage does not explicitly state that Karp is associated with lower rash frequency, the epidemiological association between the predominance of Karp and the complete absence of rash in the study population is a valid inference. The other genotypes (Kawasaki, TA716, Gilliam) are present in smaller proportions. This genetic predominance of Karp in a population where rash is completely absent supports the hypothesis that the Karp genotype may be associated with lower rash frequency compared to other genotypes.

418.Option: The absence of rash in scrub typhus patients may be due to the relatively dark skin of Vietnamese individuals, which can make rashes difficult to detect clinically.
Evidence: A total of 67 patients were enrolled and their clinical information is described in Table 1 and Fig 1.
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence about the skin pigmentation of the study population or its potential impact on rash detection. The study explicitly states that no patient was observed to have a rash, but does not provide any information about whether this was due to difficulty in detection. The option introduces a hypothesis about skin pigmentation that is not supported by any evidence in the passage. The study's methodology does not suggest that the absence of rash was due to detection limitations rather than true absence.

418.Option: The use of doxycycline and azithromycin in treatment may have suppressed the development of rash in patients with scrub typhus.
Evidence: Treatment with either doxycycline or azithromycin is still highly effective for both children and adults, although evidence of O. tsutsugamushi resistance to doxycycline has been reported in northern Thailand [10].
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about whether patients in the study received treatment with doxycycline or azithromycin prior to hospital admission. The absence of rash is reported in the study population, but there is no evidence about treatment status. The option introduces a hypothesis about treatment effects that is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The passage only mentions that these antibiotics are effective, not that they were used in this study population.

418.Option: The absence of rash in scrub typhus patients may be due to the lack of serological confirmation in the acute phase, as IFA results may be negative early in infection.
Evidence: Serological evidence of infection generally appears in the second or third week of illness, and skin biopsy of an eschar is an invasive and potentially painful procedure; therefore, these are not always useful for clinical practice [12].
A total of 32 patients (47.8%) were confirmed as having O. tsutsugamushi infection by either qPCR or IFA.
Fourteen cases (43.8%) were positive for qPCR only, and 12 (37.5%) cases were positive for only IFA.
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: The passage states that serological evidence appears in the second or third week of illness, but this refers to the timing of antibody development, not the presence or absence of rash. The study used both qPCR and IFA for confirmation, with 43.8% confirmed by qPCR only and 37.5% by IFA only. The absence of rash is reported as an observed clinical finding, not as a limitation of diagnostic methods. The option misinterprets the timing of serological evidence as being related to rash detection, which is not supported by the passage.

418.Option: The lack of rash in scrub typhus patients is likely due to delayed presentation to healthcare facilities, as most patients had been ill for over a week before admission.
Evidence: The median illness duration was one week, but some patients had been admitted more than 10 days after the fever.
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: The passage states that the median illness duration was one week, with some patients admitted more than 10 days after fever onset. However, this refers to the duration of illness, not the timing of rash appearance. The passage does not provide any information about whether rash would resolve or become undetectable after a week of illness. The option introduces a hypothesis about the timing of rash resolution that is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The absence of rash is reported as an observed clinical finding, not as a consequence of delayed presentation.

419.Option: The absence of M. perstans infection in Mahenge suggests that the parasite is not present in any onchocerciasis-endemic areas of sub-Saharan Africa, and therefore cannot be a co-factor in nodding syndrome development.
Evidence: None of the 113 epilepsy cases (including 43 with probable NS) and 132 controls were diagnosed with an M. perstans infection.

M. perstans is considered the most prevalent human filaria [15]. The infection is spread by midges (Culicoides spp.) and is believed to be prevalent in Western, Eastern and Central Africa and neotropical regions in South America [15].
419.Rationale: The passage only provides evidence about M. perstans absence in Mahenge, not in all onchocerciasis-endemic areas of sub-Saharan Africa. The study's geographic scope is limited to Mahenge, Tanzania. The broader statement about all onchocerciasis-endemic areas cannot be inferred from this single study. Additionally, the passage explicitly states that M. perstans is considered the most prevalent human filaria in multiple regions, including parts of sub-Saharan Africa, which contradicts the claim that it is not present in any onchocerciasis-endemic areas.

419.Option: The lack of onchocercal nodules in the study population indicates that ivermectin treatment has completely eliminated O. volvulus transmission in Mahenge, rendering the parasite no longer a risk factor for nodding syndrome.
Evidence: Skin snips were not obtained for O. volvulus microfilariae detection, as nearly all study participants took ivermectin during annual CDTI. The latter has been shown to mask the association between onchocerciasis and epilepsy [34].
Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86), including OAE (OR: 2.26, 95% CI: 1.15–4.47) and probable NS (OR: 5.05, 95% CI: 1.79–14.27).
419.Rationale: The passage states that skin snips were not obtained because participants had taken ivermectin, which can mask the association between onchocerciasis and epilepsy. However, the study used Ov16 seroprevalence to assess O. volvulus exposure, and found a significant association with epilepsy, including probable NS. This indicates that O. volvulus remains a risk factor despite ivermectin use. The lack of onchocercal nodules does not prove complete elimination of transmission, as ivermectin can reduce microfilariae without eliminating the adult worms. The passage does not provide evidence that ivermectin has completely eliminated O. volvulus transmission in Mahenge.

419.Option: The association between M. perstans and nodding syndrome in South Sudan was likely due to shared poverty and farming practices, rather than a direct parasitic link, and therefore M. perstans should be considered a proxy for socioeconomic risk in endemic areas.
Evidence: In three case-control studies conducted between 2001 and 2002 in Mundri, Western Equatoria State in South Sudan, not only a positive association was found between NS and an O. volvulus infection (odds ratio (OR): 9.2, p-value (p) <0.001), but also between NS and M. perstans infection (OR: 3.2, p = 0.005) [13].
The study was designed to be a case-control study where cases and controls were matched for age, sex and village.

The study was conducted according to the guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki. Ethical approval was obtained from the Ethics Committee of the National Institute for Medical Research, Tanzania (NIMR/HQ/R.8a/Vol.IX/3746) and the Ethics Committee of the Antwerp University Hospital, Belgium (B300201837863).
419.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence to support the claim that the association between M. perstans and nodding syndrome in South Sudan is due to shared poverty and farming practices. The study in Mahenge was specifically designed to investigate the potential role of M. perstans as a risk factor for NS, with cases and controls matched for age, sex, and village. The lack of M. perstans infection in Mahenge does not imply that the association in South Sudan is due to socioeconomic factors. The passage does not discuss poverty or farming practices as confounding variables in the South Sudan study. The option introduces a hypothesis not supported by the evidence in the passage.

419.Option: The clustering of epilepsy within families in Mahenge is primarily due to genetic predisposition, as evidenced by the high prevalence of first-degree relatives with epilepsy among cases.
Evidence: Persons with epilepsy were also more likely to have a family member with epilepsy (p<0.0001). Most of the first-degree relatives with epilepsy were siblings (25, 83.3%), followed by mothers (2, 6.7%), sons (2, 6.7%) and father (1, 3.3%).

When added to a univariable logistic regression adjusted for age, sex and village type, having a first-degree relative with epilepsy was strongly associated with Ov16 seropositivity (adjusted OR: 15.02, 95% CI: 2.97–274.7; p = 0.0094) but not significantly related to having a second-degree relative (p = 0.21) or an in-law with epilepsy (p = 0.25).
Similarly, a family history of seizures lost all significance when an interaction effect with Ov16 positivity was added to the conditional logistic regression for epilepsy, OAE or NS.
419.Rationale: The passage shows that while there is a high prevalence of first-degree relatives with epilepsy among cases, this association is strongly linked to Ov16 seropositivity. The statistical analysis indicates that having a first-degree relative with epilepsy is associated with Ov16 seropositivity, suggesting that the family clustering may be due to shared environmental or infectious factors rather than purely genetic predisposition. The loss of significance of family history when considering Ov16 positivity further supports the idea that the clustering is more likely due to shared exposure to O. volvulus rather than genetic factors. Therefore, the option that attributes the clustering primarily to genetic predisposition is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

419.Option: Onchocerciasis remains the main risk factor for nodding syndrome and other forms of epilepsy that meet the criteria for onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy, despite the lack of a fully understood pathogenic mechanism.
Evidence: Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86), including OAE (OR: 2.26, 95% CI: 1.15–4.47) and probable NS (OR: 5.05, 95% CI: 1.79–14.27).

A link between NS and onchocerciasis has been suspected since the former was first described in the 1960s [2]. More recent epidemiological studies suggest that NS is one of the clinical manifestations of onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy (OAE) [3].
Onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy appears in previously healthy children between the ages of 3 and 18 years, with a peak age of onset around 8−11 years [3].

Despite a large number of epidemiological studies showing the association between onchocerciasis and epilepsy, there is a reluctance to consider this association causal, mainly because there is little evidence that O. volvulus microfilariae can penetrate the brain [10] and because, so far, no indirect mechanism has been identified to explain the pathogenesis of OAE [9].
419.Rationale: The passage provides strong evidence that onchocerciasis remains a significant risk factor for nodding syndrome and other forms of epilepsy that meet the criteria for onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy. The Ov16 seroprevalence data shows a significant association with epilepsy, including probable NS. The historical context and recent studies support the link between onchocerciasis and NS as part of OAE. While the exact pathogenic mechanism is not fully understood, the epidemiological evidence is robust enough to consider onchocerciasis as the main risk factor. The passage explicitly acknowledges the lack of a fully understood pathogenic mechanism but does not negate the association, which is supported by the statistical findings.

421.Option: The success of community-wide MDA programmes is primarily determined by the age group targeted, with school-aged children being the most effective group to treat for achieving transmission interruption.
Evidence: The WHO guidelines also recommend the treatment of high-risk adults, which includes women of reproductive age (WRA), who can be challenging to find and treat at a community level when not attending antenatal or mother-to-child health clinics [9].

More recent STH and schistosome infection integrated control interventions have sought to interrupt transmission by expanding treatment across a broader range of age groups including adults, known as community-wide treatment.
This shift in treatment focus was partially stimulated by the London Declaration in 2012, which highlighted the potential for elimination of transmission within particular settings [10].
Published research indicates that the interruption of STH and schistosome transmission is unlikely to occur when only treating SAC and WRA [11].
421.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that treating only school-aged children (SAC) and women of reproductive age (WRA) is insufficient for transmission interruption. It also describes the shift towards community-wide treatment as a more effective strategy. The evidence directly contradicts the claim that SAC are the most effective group to treat for achieving transmission interruption. The passage emphasizes the importance of broader age group coverage rather than focusing solely on SAC.

421.Option: The non-compliance of a small group of individuals can significantly affect the achievement of transmission interruption goals in community-wide MDA programmes, even when overall treatment coverage is relatively high.
Evidence: The analyses presented in the work of Hardwick and colleagues [30] demonstrates the use of a conditional probability model in stochastic individual-based simulations by running two example forecasts for the elimination of STH transmission employing MDA within the TUMIKIA trial setting with different adherence patterns. This suggested a substantial reduction in the probability of transmission elimination (between 23–43%) when comparing the observed adherence patterns which recorded partial non-compliance, with an assumption of randomness in who gets treated at each round with defined levels of coverage.

Previous studies have indicated that the effectiveness of community-wide MDA is influenced by specific patterns of individual non-treatment [29]. This could be a random process where the probability that any individual in the target population is treated in any given round of MDA follows a positive binomial (random) distribution, or this could be systematic, where the same individuals repeatedly do not take treatment [29].
The impact of MDA and individual compliance to treatment

Individuals in the longitudinal cohorts were divided into three groups according to treatment compliance. These were; (1) No documented treatment over two rounds of MDA (never -“0”, sample size 11); (2) Documented partaking of a single round of MDA at either Y1 or Y2 (one year–“1”, sample size = 94); (3) Documented partaking in two rounds of MDA at Y1 and Y2 (two years—“2”, sample size = 413).

For fully non-compliant individuals (never treated) who did not partake in MDA during each of the previous years, no individual could be designated as uninfected based on the Kato Katz diagnostic method. In this no-documented treatment group, observations of acquired infection (negative to positive individuals) over follow-up for all three infections are recorded in S6 Table).

Longitudinal changes in mean EPG within each compliant age group indicated a marked difference from year to year. Across those individuals that were treated in both years of MDA, mean EPG dropped well below the level observed at the baseline survey (T. trichiura; 102.33, 98.22, 3.22, A. lumbricoides; 583.07, 331.81, 138.71, Hookworm; 12.22, 14.92, 1.37). Those individuals that had not received MDA at any round saw an increase in mean EPG from that calculated at baseline (T. trichiura; 422.11, 588.35, 704.2, A. lumbricoides; 1041.95, 1610.6, 1269.96 and Hookworm; 51.8, 62.33, 103.89) (Fig 5 and Table 3 and S6 Table).
421.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. It discusses how non-compliance patterns (both random and systematic) significantly impact MDA effectiveness. The conditional probability model analysis shows a 23–43% reduction in transmission elimination probability when non-compliance is observed compared to random treatment. The longitudinal data demonstrates that individuals who never received treatment had increasing mean EPG while those who received full treatment had decreasing EPG. This directly supports the conclusion that even with relatively high overall coverage, non-compliance by a small group can significantly affect transmission interruption goals.

421.Option: The aggregation of infection in a small number of individuals is primarily driven by genetic predisposition, which makes targeted treatment of high-burden individuals ineffective in interrupting transmission.
Evidence: Helminth parasites are highly aggregated within human populations, with a small number of individuals harbouring the majority of worms within an infected community [19–22]. The aggregation of infection in a small number of individuals increases the likelihood that parasites successfully mate.

MDA could be focused on the individuals with the highest aggregates of parasites, with the suggestion that these individuals will show a greater propensity to shed infective eggs into the community [23]. This highly focused approach has not been used at present due to the high effort and cost associated with measuring the intensity of infection within the total population, compared to treating the target population without taking a stool sample. It may be a cost-effective option when overall prevalence is very low, and drugs are no longer donated without cost to MDA programmes.

The analyses presented in the work of Hardwick and colleagues [30] demonstrates the use of a conditional probability model in stochastic individual-based simulations by running two example forecasts for the elimination of STH transmission employing MDA within the TUMIKIA trial setting with different adherence patterns.
421.Rationale: The passage discusses the aggregation of infection in a small number of individuals but does not attribute this to genetic predisposition. Instead, it focuses on the biological and epidemiological factors (such as parasite mating likelihood and egg shedding potential) that make targeted treatment of high-burden individuals a potentially effective strategy. The claim about genetic predisposition is not supported by the passage and contradicts the suggestion that targeting high-burden individuals could be effective if implemented. The passage also notes that this approach has not been used due to cost and effort, not because it would be ineffective.

421.Option: The Geshiyaro project’s use of biometric registration and study ID cards has led to a significant reduction in the overall prevalence of STH infections across all age groups in the sentinel sites.
Evidence: Baseline (BS) prevalence was 32.31% for any STH, 29.7% for A. lumbricoides, 6.08% for T. trichiura and 4.38% for Hookworm (Table 2). From BS to Y1 the prevalence of any STH increased by 4.3% (percentage points; real time increase of 13.3% between BS to Y1), with the change being statistically significant (P <0.001). The reduction in prevalence of A. lumbricoides and T. trichuris was statistically significant from BS to Y2 (P < 0.05), but hookworm showed an increase over this period. In the most recent year (Y2) of the longitudinal survey, prevalence of infection with any STH was 31.4%. compared with a 32.3% level at baseline.

There was considerable variation in trends across the four kebeles. Two kebeles had a lower prevalence of any STH in Y2 compared to BS (Afama Garo: 56.07% BS– 29.25% Y2, Korke Doge; 40.69% BS– 31.62%) and reductions in prevalence proved significant (P < 0.001) (Table 2 and Fig 2). Only one kebele, Afama Garo, indicated a drop in prevalence of any STH in each year of the survey. In two kebeles, Giddo Homba and Hajo Salata, prevalence of any STH increased year on year.
The Geshiyaro project has sampled 577, 621 and 573 individuals at baseline (BS), after one year (Y1), and after two years from baseline (Y2), respectively.
The impact of MDA and individual compliance to treatment

Individuals in the longitudinal cohorts were divided into three groups according to treatment compliance. These were; (1) No documented treatment over two rounds of MDA (never -“0”, sample size 11); (2) Documented partaking of a single round of MDA at either Y1 or Y2 (one year–“1”, sample size = 94); (3) Documented partaking in two rounds of MDA at Y1 and Y2 (two years—“2”, sample size = 413).
421.Rationale: The passage provides mixed results regarding prevalence changes. While some kebeles showed reductions in prevalence, others showed increases. The overall prevalence at Y2 (31.4%) was slightly lower than baseline (32.3%) but the difference was not statistically significant. The passage does not attribute these changes specifically to the use of biometric registration and study ID cards, but rather to the MDA programme and individual compliance patterns. The evidence does not support the claim that the biometric system led to a significant reduction in prevalence across all age groups.

422.Option: The anti-viral activity of viperin against DENV-2 is mediated by a C-terminal interaction with the DENV-2 NS3 protein and does not require membrane association or lipid droplet localization.
Evidence: The interaction of viperin and NS3 but not membrane association, however, is necessary for viperins anti-viral actions.

The ampipathic helix mutant (5′Δ33) of viperin, which retains its anti-DENV-2 activity, but has lost its membrane localisation ability, demonstrated a positive interaction by FRET analysis with DENV-2 NS3, once again at distinct cytoplasmic foci within the cells.

The C-terminal viperin mutant, 3′Δ17, which has no anti-DENV-2 activity but maintains WT viperin localisation [9], showed no positive FRET with DENV-2 NS3 suggesting this protein is unable to interact with DENV-2 NS3.
422.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this conclusion. First, it explicitly states that the interaction of viperin and NS3 is necessary for its anti-viral actions, while membrane association is not. Second, the N-terminal deletion mutant (5′Δ33) that loses membrane localization but retains anti-viral activity still interacts with NS3. Third, the C-terminal deletion mutant (3′Δ17) that lacks anti-viral activity fails to interact with NS3. These findings collectively demonstrate that the C-terminal interaction with NS3 is essential for anti-viral activity, and membrane association is not required.

422.Option: Viperin restricts DENV-2 replication by disrupting lipid raft integrity, similar to its mechanism of action against influenza virus.
Evidence: The roles and actions of viperin in these different viral infections appear diverse and multifaceted with anti-viral activity in some cases dependent on alterations to lipid rafts (influenza, [14]), membrane localisation (HCV, [9]), the radical S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) enzymatic activity of viperin (HIV, [10]), negated by viral proteins (JEV, [11]) and even an enhancing role under some conditions for HCMV [18].

The maintenance, however of the anti-DENV-2 activity of viperin containing N-terminal deletion mutants suggests that the ability of viperin to associate with membranes is not required for its restriction of DENV-2 infection (Figure 3A).
422.Rationale: While the passage mentions that viperin's anti-influenza activity involves lipid raft disruption, it explicitly states that membrane association is not required for its anti-DENV-2 activity. The N-terminal deletion mutant that loses membrane localization still retains anti-viral activity against DENV-2. Therefore, the mechanism against DENV-2 is distinct from its mechanism against influenza.

422.Option: The induction of viperin in DENV-2-infected primary MDM is primarily driven by interferon signaling from infected cells, leading to a systemic antiviral state in bystander cells.
Evidence: Viperin mRNA was significantly induced in DENV-2 infected human cell lines with approximately 25 fold induction in A549 lung carcinoma cells (Figure 1A) and a lesser 4 fold increase in Huh-7 hepatoma cells co-incident with high level DENV-2 −ve strand RNA production (Figure 1B).
Up-regulation of viperin by DENV-2 infection was also demonstrated in primary MDM, with a much greater, approximately 1000 fold induction of viperin mRNA at 24 h pi (Figure 1D).

Interestingly MDM positive for DENV antigen displayed reduced amounts of viperin protein, whereas DENV-antigen negative bystander cells (indicated via arrows, Figure 2B, upper panel) were shown to express significantly increased levels of viperin protein.
422.Rationale: The passage shows that viperin is induced in DENV-2 infected cells and bystander cells, but it does not provide evidence that this induction is primarily driven by interferon signaling from infected cells. The study does not investigate the signaling pathways responsible for viperin induction in this context. The increased viperin in bystander cells could be due to other factors, and the passage does not establish a causal link to interferon signaling.

422.Option: Viperin inhibits DENV-2 replication by directly binding to and degrading viral RNA, as evidenced by co-precipitation of DENV-2 RNA with viperin.
Evidence: 293T cells were transfected with viperin-FLAG, IPed with FLAG antibody or no antibody control and precipitates analysed for DENV-2 RNA by RT-PCR. Results demonstrate successful co-precipitation of DENV-2 RNA with FLAG-antibody (Figure 6C). Concurrent analysis of precipitates by western blot, however failed to detect co-precipitated DENV-2 NS3 protein (data not shown).
422.Rationale: The passage shows that viperin co-precipitates with DENV-2 RNA, but this does not prove direct binding or degradation of viral RNA. The co-precipitation could be indirect, and the study does not provide evidence that viperin degrades RNA. Additionally, the failure to detect NS3 in precipitates suggests that the interaction with RNA may not be direct or functionally significant for anti-viral activity.

422.Option: The anti-viral effect of viperin against DENV-2 is dependent on its ability to co-localize with the DENV-2 capsid (CA) protein at lipid droplet interfaces.
Evidence: The ampipathic helix mutant (5′Δ33) of viperin, which retains its anti-DENV-2 activity, but has lost its membrane localisation ability, demonstrated a positive interaction by FRET analysis with DENV-2 NS3, once again at distinct cytoplasmic foci within the cells.

The N-terminal viperin deletion mutant (Vip5′Δ33) which loses its ability to localise to lipid droplets [9], [31], but remains anti-viral against DENV-2 (Figure 3A), remained co-localised with DENV-2 NS3, although the pattern of localisation was solely cytoplasmic (Figure 7C).
422.Rationale: The passage shows that the N-terminal deletion mutant of viperin, which cannot localize to lipid droplets, still retains anti-viral activity and co-localizes with NS3 in the cytoplasm. This directly contradicts the claim that anti-viral activity depends on co-localization with CA at lipid droplet interfaces. The study also shows that co-localization with CA is partial and not essential for anti-viral activity.

423.Option: The lack of surveillance and diagnostic capacity for rabies detection means that data on disease burden are insufficient to motivate policy-makers, and impacts of control efforts cannot be evaluated.
Evidence: Rabies monitoring operations including passive and active surveillance involving veterinarians, village livestock field officers, paravets, rangers and scientists were used to collect samples from carcasses (domestic dogs and wildlife whenever found), which were subsequently tested and viral isolates were sequenced [10], [13]–[16], with results being used to inform estimates of rabies-recognition probabilities [7] and for phylogenetic analyses [10],[16].

Hospital records of animal-bite injuries compiled from northwest Tanzania were used as primary data sources. These data informed a probability decision tree model for a national disease burden evaluation [7], which has since been adapted for global estimates of human rabies deaths and Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) lost due to rabies [3], a standardized measure for assessing disease burden [8],[9].
423.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes the existence of both passive and active surveillance systems for rabies, including carcass sampling, sequencing, and hospital record analysis. These surveillance and diagnostic capacities have been used to inform disease burden estimates and phylogenetic analyses. The claim that 'data on disease burden are insufficient to motivate policy-makers' is directly contradicted by the evidence that these data have been used for global estimates of human rabies deaths and DALYs. The passage does not suggest that surveillance is lacking or that data are insufficient for policy-making.

423.Option: The high cost of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) in Africa and Asia makes it economically unsustainable to rely on PEP alone for rabies prevention, necessitating a shift to mass dog vaccination campaigns.
Evidence: In this paper, we identify four major reasons commonly given for the lack of effective domestic dog rabies control including (1) low prioritisation, (2) epidemiological constraints, (3) operational constraints and (4) lack of resources (Table 1), focussing on the situation in Africa. We address each of these issues in turn, using outputs from modelling approaches and data from field studies to demonstrate that there are no insurmountable logistic, practical, epidemiological, ecological or economic obstacles.

As a result, we conclude that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa and there should be no reasons for further delay in preventing the unnecessary tragedy of human rabies deaths.
423.Rationale: The passage does not mention the cost of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) as a factor in rabies control strategies. While it does discuss the need for mass dog vaccination campaigns, it does not frame this as a response to the economic unsustainability of PEP. The claim about PEP cost being a driver for shifting to vaccination is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The passage instead focuses on the feasibility of canine rabies elimination through vaccination, without referencing PEP costs as a motivating factor.

423.Option: The persistence of rabies in wildlife populations across southern Africa suggests that canine rabies elimination through dog vaccination alone is not feasible in those regions.
Evidence: Rabies can infect and be transmitted by a wide range of mammals, reservoirs comprise only mammalian species within the Orders Carnivora (e.g. dogs, raccoons, skunks, foxes, jackals) and Chiroptera (bats).

From the perspective of human rabies, the vast majority of human cases (>90%) result from the bites of rabid domestic dogs [2] and occur in regions where domestic dogs are the principal maintenance host [3].

As a result, we conclude that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa and there should be no reasons for further delay in preventing the unnecessary tragedy of human rabies deaths.
423.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that rabies can infect a wide range of mammals but clarifies that reservoirs are limited to specific orders (Carnivora and Chiroptera). It also states that the vast majority of human rabies cases result from domestic dog bites, where dogs are the principal maintenance host. The conclusion drawn in the passage is that canine rabies elimination is feasible in much of Africa, despite not explicitly addressing wildlife reservoirs. The option's claim that 'canine rabies elimination through dog vaccination alone is not feasible in those regions' is not supported by the passage and contradicts the stated conclusion of feasibility.

423.Option: The low R0 values observed in African dog populations indicate that rabies transmission is inherently weak, making it unlikely that any intervention would be needed to achieve elimination.
Evidence: In this paper, we identify four major reasons commonly given for the lack of effective domestic dog rabies control including (1) low prioritisation, (2) epidemiological constraints, (3) operational constraints and (4) lack of resources (Table 1), focussing on the situation in Africa. We address each of these issues in turn, using outputs from modelling approaches and data from field studies to demonstrate that there are no insurmountable logistic, practical, epidemiological, ecological or economic obstacles.

As a result, we conclude that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa and there should be no reasons for further delay in preventing the unnecessary tragedy of human rabies deaths.
423.Rationale: The passage does not mention R0 values or suggest that rabies transmission in African dog populations is inherently weak. Instead, it discusses the feasibility of canine rabies elimination through mass dog vaccination campaigns, addressing logistical, practical, and resource-related challenges. The claim that 'any intervention would be needed to achieve elimination' is not supported by the passage and contradicts the emphasis on the need for active intervention (mass vaccination) to achieve elimination.

423.Option: The elimination of canine rabies through domestic dog vaccination is epidemiologically feasible in most of Africa, despite concerns about wildlife reservoirs and operational challenges.
Evidence: As a result, we conclude that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa and there should be no reasons for further delay in preventing the unnecessary tragedy of human rabies deaths.

In this paper, we identify four major reasons commonly given for the lack of effective domestic dog rabies control including (1) low prioritisation, (2) epidemiological constraints, (3) operational constraints and (4) lack of resources (Table 1), focussing on the situation in Africa. We address each of these issues in turn, using outputs from modelling approaches and data from field studies to demonstrate that there are no insurmountable logistic, practical, epidemiological, ecological or economic obstacles.
423.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa, despite the challenges mentioned (low prioritization, epidemiological constraints, operational constraints, and lack of resources). The authors use modeling approaches and field data to demonstrate that these obstacles are not insurmountable. This directly supports the option's claim that canine rabies elimination through domestic dog vaccination is epidemiologically feasible in most of Africa, even with concerns about wildlife reservoirs and operational challenges.

424.Option: The observed variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions may be due to differences in the timing and coverage of community-based LF-MDA, which could have influenced local reinfection rates after treatment.
Evidence: In Fiji, it was also possible to track back initial efforts to control STH when the annual round of community-based LF-MDA was begun in 2001 with the support of PacELF [5]. Most of the consecutive annual LF-MDA rounds were successful, with around 65% population coverage, and led to termination of LF-MDA decisions in three Divisions of the country [6,7]: the final LF-MDA 7th round for the Western Division took place in 2009, and the 10th for the Central and 9th for the Northern in 2012.
The Eastern Division islands and the Taveuni sub-Division have formed another LF implementation unit (Fig 1) for further LF-MDA rounds since 2013 [6].
424.Rationale: While the passage does mention the timing and coverage of LF-MDA in different regions, it does not provide any direct evidence linking these factors to the observed variation in STH prevalence. The passage states that LF-MDA was terminated in some divisions by 2012, but the STH prevalence data (collected in 2014-2015) shows variation across regions. However, the passage does not establish a causal relationship between LF-MDA timing/coverage and STH prevalence variation. The inference that LF-MDA influenced local reinfection rates is speculative and not supported by the evidence in the passage.

424.Option: The higher prevalence of Ascaris infections in younger children suggests that school-based deworming should be prioritized for class 1 and 2 students to reduce transmission across age groups.
Evidence: The overall estimated prevalence of any STH of class 1 and 2 students was 13.3% (95% CI: 6.4–25.6).

Prevalence levels of any STH and Ascaris infections were highest in class 1–2 students and lowest in class 7–8 students (P = 0.0216), showing that prevalence levels were not the same across class groups (Table 5).

In terms of infection intensity, both low-intensity and moderate to heavy-intensity Ascaris infections were more frequent in class 1–2 than in the other groups, but without statistical significance (Table 5).
424.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that Ascaris infections are more prevalent in younger children (class 1-2) compared to older children. This age-related pattern is a well-established epidemiological principle in helminth infections, where younger children are often at higher risk due to behavioral factors and lower immunity. The passage also states that the objective of the study was to develop a national strategy for STH control, including defining appropriate PCT frequencies. The evidence directly supports the inference that prioritizing deworming for younger children (class 1-2) would be an effective strategy to reduce transmission, as this group has the highest prevalence and intensity of infection.

424.Option: The use of LF-TAS as a platform for assessing STH epidemiology has been clearly demonstrated in other countries, and this approach is now recommended by WHO guidelines for all post-LF-MDA surveillance settings.
Evidence: The STH prevalence assessment was conducted in a phased manner over the four Health Divisions of Fiji by having each Division as an evaluation unit (EU) (Fig 1). The survey in the Western Division was conducted at the beginning of 2014 in conjunction with the second LF-TAS (TAS 2) in the Division. Then the assessments were embedded with the TAS 1 for the Central Division in July-August 2014, and with the TAS 2 for the Northern Division in February-March 2015.
424.Rationale: The passage describes the use of LF-TAS as a platform for STH assessment in Fiji, but it does not provide evidence that this approach has been 'clearly demonstrated in other countries' or that it is now 'recommended by WHO guidelines for all post-LF-MDA surveillance settings.' The passage only states that the STH prevalence assessment was conducted in conjunction with LF-TAS in Fiji. The inference about WHO recommendations and broader applicability to other countries is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

424.Option: Given the varying levels of STH prevalence at the sub-national level, the control program can now consider applying preventive chemotherapy in any Division or sub-Division where the prevalence exceeds 20%.
